mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old patient who underwent PSARP repair of a bladder neck fistula at infancy is experiencing significant fecal incontinence. The parents ask about prognosis. Which of the following continence rates most closely corresponds to this type of fistula?
mcqs.explanation:
A is correct because the fecal continence rate for bladder neck fistula is approximately 12%, the lowest among the fistula types. This reflects the severe nature of this malformation, which is associated with sacral dysplasia, abnormal pelvic floor musculature, and poor anal sphincter development.

B is wrong because 30% is the continence rate for prostatic fistulae, not bladder neck fistulae.

C is wrong because 50% is the rate for bulbar fistulae.

D is wrong because 70% is the rate for vestibular fistulae.

Teaching point: Bladder neck fistulae have the worst fecal continence prognosis (12%), reflecting the most severe sphincter and sacral anomalies; this should be discussed with families during preoperative counseling.

mcqs.stem: A 12-year-old girl with a history of cloacal anomaly repair is experiencing persistent urinary incontinence despite clean intermittent catheterization. A review of the literature indicates the incontinence rate for her anomaly type is the highest among ARM patients. Which of the following values represents the incontinence rate for cloacal anomalies according to a study of over 300 patients?
mcqs.explanation:
B is correct because a study of over 300 patients found that cloacal anomalies carry the highest urinary incontinence rate at 46%. This was followed by bladder neck fistula (25%), urethral fistula (25%), and vaginal fistula (20%). Patients without a fistula had the lowest rate at 4%.

A is wrong because 25% is the incontinence rate for both bladder neck and urethral fistulae, not cloacal anomalies.

C is wrong because 20% is the rate for vaginal fistula.

D is wrong because 4% is the rate for patients without any fistula, who have the best urinary continence.

Teaching point: Urinary continence after ARM repair follows a similar pattern to fecal continence — more severe malformations (cloaca, bladder neck fistula) have higher incontinence rates, while patients without fistula have the best outcomes.

mcqs.stem: A child with an anorectal malformation and associated spinal anomaly has an incompetent bladder neck and persistent urinary leakage despite optimal bladder management with anticholinergics and clean intermittent catheterization. Which of the following is a surgical option to increase outflow resistance?
mcqs.explanation:
C is correct because for patients with an incompetent bladder neck and sphincter complex, various procedures are available to increase outflow resistance, including injection of bulking agents and formal bladder neck reconstruction.

A is wrong because urethral dilation would decrease outflow resistance and worsen incontinence; it is used for strictures, not incontinence.

B is wrong because ureteral reimplantation addresses vesicoureteral reflux, not bladder neck incompetence.

D is wrong because nephrectomy does not address the underlying problem of bladder neck incompetence and is not indicated for incontinence management.

Teaching point: Bladder neck incompetence in ARM patients, often associated with spinal anomalies, can be managed surgically by increasing outlet resistance through bulking agents or bladder neck reconstruction, and in refractory cases, bladder neck closure with a Mitrofanoff channel.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old patient with a history of ARM and spinal anomaly has refractory urinary incontinence despite previous bladder neck reconstruction. The surgical team recommends bladder neck closure. Which of the following procedures must accompany bladder neck closure to allow bladder emptying?
mcqs.explanation:
D is correct because bladder neck closure must be accompanied by a Mitrofanoff procedure (creation of a catheterizable channel, typically using appendix or ileum) to provide a route for bladder emptying.

A is wrong because an ileal conduit creates an incontinent stoma, which defeats the purpose of achieving continence through bladder neck closure.

B is wrong because a vesicostomy is an incontinent diversion that allows continuous drainage, not a catheterizable channel.

C is wrong because a urethral stent would keep the bladder neck open and is the opposite of what is intended with bladder neck closure.

Teaching point: Bladder neck closure achieves urinary continence but requires a concomitant Mitrofanoff channel to allow intermittent catheterization for bladder emptying; this combination is reserved for severe refractory incontinence.

mcqs.stem: A retrospective study from the Netherlands found that 91% of ARM patients with bladder-sphincter dysfunction on urodynamics had urinary incontinence. Analysis of this group revealed a common underlying association. Which of the following best describes the finding most strongly associated with this bladder-sphincter dysfunction?

options.text: An underlying spinal anomaly was present in nearly all cases
All had undergone prior PSARP procedure
All had vesicoureteral reflux on VCUG

mcqs.explanation:
A is correct because with only one exception, all patients with bladder-sphincter dysfunction on urodynamics had an underlying spinal anomaly. This finding highlights the importance of spinal evaluation in ARM patients with lower urinary tract symptoms.

B is wrong because PSARP is the standard repair technique and would not be a specific risk factor for bladder-sphincter dysfunction; the association is with spinal anomalies.

C is wrong because while VUR is common in ARM, it was not the universal feature among patients with bladder-sphincter dysfunction.

D is wrong because rectovesical fistula is a specific ARM subtype, not a universal finding in patients with bladder-sphincter dysfunction.

Teaching point: Bladder-sphincter dysfunction in ARM is almost invariably associated with a spinal anomaly (most commonly sacral dysplasia or tethered cord), reinforcing the need for spinal imaging and urodynamics in these patients.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old man who underwent repair of a rectourethral fistula in infancy is concerned about sexual function as he begins a new relationship. He had a posterior sagittal approach. Which of the following best describes the expected sexual function outcomes associated with this surgical approach?

options.text: Poor erectile function due to inevitable nerve damage
Better outcomes with reduced risk of iatrogenic nerve damage

mcqs.explanation:
B is correct because the posterior sagittal approach (PSARP) is thought to be associated with better sexual functional outcomes by reducing the risk of iatrogenic damage to the relevant pelvic innervation. One study of 41 males with rectourethral fistulae treated by PSARP found that 90% had normal erections and ejaculation.

A is wrong because PSARP is associated with good erectile function outcomes, not poor outcomes; 90% of patients had normal erections in one series.

C is wrong because the majority of PSARP patients do not require assisted reproduction; fertility is preserved in most cases.

D is wrong because PSARP outcomes are superior to abdominoperineal procedures, which have less satisfactory sexual function outcomes, particularly in men with spinal anomalies.

Teaching point: PSARP preserves pelvic innervation better than older abdominoperineal approaches, resulting in superior sexual function outcomes (90% normal erections and ejaculation), making it the preferred approach for both functional and oncologic nerve preservation.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old man born with a rectourethral fistula who underwent PSARP in infancy presents for a routine follow-up. He reports normal erectile and ejaculatory function. In the published study of 41 males with rectourethral fistulae treated by PSARP, what percentage of patients reported normal erections and ejaculation?
mcqs.explanation:
C is correct because a study examining sexual function in 41 males with rectourethral fistulae treated by PSARP found that 90% of these patients had normal erections and ejaculation and 80% experienced normal orgasms.

A is wrong because 50% is too low; this would be closer to the rate of sexual dysfunction in female patients.

B is wrong because 70% underestimates the excellent outcomes reported after PSARP.

D is wrong because 80% is the rate for normal orgasms in the same study, not erections and ejaculation.

Teaching point: PSARP is associated with excellent preservation of male sexual function, with 90% of patients maintaining normal erectile and ejaculatory function, compared to poorer outcomes with older abdominoperineal approaches.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of repaired anorectal malformation is undergoing evaluation for infertility. Which of the following conditions is NOT a recognized cause of infertility or subfertility in men with anorectal malformations?
mcqs.explanation:
D is correct because exogenous testosterone therapy is not mentioned as a cause of infertility in men with anorectal malformations. The recognized causes include cryptorchidism, genital anomalies, sacral anomalies, recurrent epididymo-orchitis, and iatrogenic injuries.

A is wrong because cryptorchidism is present in 20% of males with ARM and is a well-recognized cause of subfertility.

B is wrong because recurrent epididymo-orchitis, often due to reflux into the vas deferens, can cause obstructive azoospermia.

C is wrong because sacral anomalies can affect pelvic innervation and contribute to ejaculatory dysfunction.

Teaching point: Infertility in men with ARM is multifactorial, resulting from cryptorchidism, genital anomalies, sacral nerve dysfunction, infectious complications (epididymo-orchitis), and iatrogenic injuries — but not from exogenous testosterone.

mcqs.stem: A 15-year-old girl with a history of anorectal malformation and prior vaginal reconstruction presents for a puberty assessment. She has not yet started menstruating. Based on reported outcomes, what proportion of such adolescent females are expected to menstruate normally?
mcqs.explanation:
A is correct because it has been reported that only one-third of female patients with anorectal malformations who had vaginal reconstruction menstruate normally. It is important to ensure that the vagina is not obstructed.

B is wrong because one-quarter is too low; the reported figure is one-third.

C is wrong because one-half overestimates normal menstrual function in this population.

D is wrong because two-thirds overestimates the proportion; only one-third menstruate normally.

Teaching point: Adolescent females with ARM who underwent vaginal reconstruction require careful gynecologic evaluation at puberty, as only one-third menstruate normally and vaginal obstruction must be ruled out.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old woman with a history of anorectal malformation and known uterine didelphys is planning a pregnancy. Which of the following obstetric risks is she at increased risk for?
mcqs.explanation:
B is correct because women with uterine anomalies such as didelphys are at higher risk for preterm labor and miscarriage. Cesarean section delivery is necessary in those who have undergone complex vaginal reconstruction or replacement.

A is wrong because placenta previa is associated with prior cesarean section and uterine scarring, not specifically with uterine didelphys.

C is wrong because preeclampsia is not specifically associated with uterine didelphys or anorectal malformations.

D is wrong because gestational diabetes is a metabolic condition not associated with uterine anomalies.

Teaching point: Women with ARM-related Müllerian anomalies (didelphys, bicornuate uterus) require high-risk obstetric monitoring due to increased rates of preterm labor and miscarriage, and those with prior vaginal reconstruction often require cesarean delivery.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a history of repaired anorectal malformation reports dissatisfaction with her sexual function. A recent study examined sexual function in women with anorectal malformations. What proportion of women in this study reported sexual dysfunction or distress?
mcqs.explanation:
C is correct because one recent study found 50% of women with an anorectal malformation reported sexual dysfunction or distress independent of continence and quality of life. Additionally, 35–45% of female patients surveyed had not been sexually active.

A is wrong because 20% is too low; this would underrepresent the prevalence of sexual dysfunction in this population.

B is wrong because 30% underestimates the reported rate of sexual dysfunction.

D is wrong because 70% overestimates the rate; the reported figure is 50%.

Teaching point: Sexual dysfunction is common in women with ARM (50%), affecting quality of life independently of continence status, and requires a multidisciplinary approach including gynecologic and psychological support.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old child is found to have a left pelvic kidney on ultrasound performed for minor urinary symptoms. The parents ask about the significance of this finding. Which of the following is true regarding the most common form of simple renal ectopia?

options.text: It is most commonly located in the thoracic cavity
It accounts for 60% of all simple ectopic kidneys
It is bilateral in 90% of cases
It occurs more commonly on the right side

mcqs.explanation:
B is correct because pelvic kidneys are the most common form of simple renal ectopia, accounting for 60% of all cases. The majority (90%) are unilateral, and they occur more commonly on the left.

A is wrong because thoracic kidneys are rare and not the most common form of simple ectopia; pelvic kidneys are most common.

C is wrong because the majority (90%) of ectopic kidneys are unilateral, not bilateral.

D is wrong because pelvic kidneys occur more commonly on the left, not the right.

Teaching point: Pelvic kidney is the most common form of simple renal ectopia (60%), is usually unilateral (90%), and occurs more frequently on the left side; it is often an incidental finding.

mcqs.stem: A patient is found to have a horseshoe kidney on imaging. The isthmus is noted to be trapped beneath the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. At which vertebral level does this isthmus typically lie in approximately 40% of cases?
mcqs.explanation:
A is correct because in about 40% of cases, the isthmus of a horseshoe kidney lies at the level of L4 where it is trapped beneath the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery during renal ascent.

B is wrong because L1 is too high; the normal renal position is at the T12-L2 level, but the horseshoe isthmus is lower.

C is wrong because L2 is at the level of normal renal position; the horseshoe isthmus lies lower at L4.

D is wrong because L5 is too low; while the horseshoe kidney lies lower than normal kidneys, the isthmus is typically at L4, not L5.

Teaching point: The horseshoe kidney isthmus is typically positioned at L4, trapped beneath the inferior mesenteric artery origin, and this abnormal anatomy contributes to UPJ obstruction as the most common complication.

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old child with a horseshoe kidney presents with intermittent flank pain and nausea. Ultrasound reveals hydronephrosis. Which of the following is the most common complication of horseshoe kidney that explains these symptoms?
mcqs.explanation:
B is correct because the most common complication of horseshoe kidney is ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) obstruction. This may be due to the deviated course of the proximal ureter as it arches anteriorly over the isthmus, or extrinsic compression by aberrant vasculature, or a combination of both.

A is wrong while renal calculi can occur with horseshoe kidney due to urinary stasis, UPJ obstruction is the most common complication.

C is wrong because reflux nephropathy is associated with vesicoureteral reflux but is not the most common complication of horseshoe kidney specifically.

D is wrong because renal artery stenosis and hypertension are not the most common complications of horseshoe kidney.

Teaching point: UPJ obstruction is the hallmark complication of horseshoe kidney, caused by the anomalous course of the ureter over the isthmus and aberrant vessels; it presents with flank pain and hydronephrosis.

mcqs.stem: A 5-year-old child is found to have crossed fused renal ectopia on imaging. Which of the following best describes the most common fusion configuration in this condition?

options.text: Lower pole of the normally positioned kidney fused to the lower pole of the crossed kidney
Upper pole of the normally positioned kidney fused to the upper pole of the crossed kidney
Upper pole of the crossed kidney fused to the lower pole of the normally positioned kidney
Fusion of the renal pelves of both kidneys

mcqs.explanation:
C is correct because in the most common configuration of crossed fused renal ectopia, the point of fusion is between the upper pole of the crossed kidney and the lower pole of the normally positioned kidney (unilateral fused type). Crossed renal ectopia with fusion accounts for 85% of cases.

A is wrong because lower pole-to-lower pole fusion describes the configuration of horseshoe kidney, not crossed fused ectopia.

B is wrong because upper pole-to-upper pole fusion is not the typical configuration; this would describe an uncommon variant.

D is wrong because fusion of the renal pelves is not a described configuration; fusion occurs at the parenchymal level.

Teaching point: In crossed fused renal ectopia (85% of crossed ectopia cases), the upper pole of the ectopic kidney fuses with the lower pole of the orthotopic kidney, typically with left-to-right crossing being more common.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old child is noted to have an empty renal fossa on routine prenatal and postnatal ultrasound. The child is asymptomatic. Which of the following is the best next imaging step to confirm the presence of functioning ectopic renal tissue?

options.text: CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis

mcqs.explanation:
D is correct because non-dilated pelvic ectopic kidneys may be difficult to visualize on ultrasound, and absence of the kidney in the renal fossa may be misinterpreted as renal agenesis. The presence of ectopic functioning renal tissue is best demonstrated by DMSA renography with 99mTc-dimercaptosuccinic acid.

A is wrong because CT angiography provides excellent vascular detail but involves radiation and contrast; DMSA is more specific for identifying functioning renal tissue.

B is wrong because MAG3 renography assesses drainage and relative renal function but DMSA is superior for detecting and localizing functioning ectopic renal tissue.

C is wrong because intravenous urography provides less detail in young children and has been largely replaced by nuclear medicine and ultrasound for this indication.

Teaching point: When ultrasound suggests renal agenesis, DMSA scan is the imaging modality of choice to identify ectopic functioning renal tissue, as ectopic kidneys (especially pelvic) may be missed on ultrasound when non-dilated.

mcqs.stem: The worldwide incidence of classic bladder exstrophy is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: Classic bladder exstrophy occurs in approximately 1 in 50,000 live births, with a male-to-female ratio of 2:1 (Table 15.1).

mcqs.stem: The exstrophy–epispadias complex (EEC) comprises three distinct anomalies. Which of the following represents the correct order from least to most severe?

options.text: Epispadias, classic bladder exstrophy, cloacal exstrophy
Cloacal exstrophy, classic bladder exstrophy, epispadias
Epispadias, cloacal exstrophy, classic bladder exstrophy
Classic bladder exstrophy, epispadias, cloacal exstrophy

mcqs.explanation: Epispadias is the mildest form, followed by classic bladder exstrophy, with cloacal exstrophy being the most severe variant in the exstrophy–epispadias complex. The text describes cloacal exstrophy as 'a more severe embryological variant.'

mcqs.stem: A newborn male is diagnosed with classic bladder exstrophy. His parents ask about the recurrence risk in future children. The most accurate estimate is:
mcqs.explanation: Bladder exstrophy is a sporadic condition with a very low recurrence risk in subsequent siblings, estimated at less than 1%. The condition arises from failure of development of the lower abdominal wall during early gestation, as described in the chapter.

mcqs.stem: Which hypothesis proposes that the exstrophy–epispadias complex arises from failure of integration of mesodermal, endodermal and ectodermal components of the cloacal plate, causing it to rupture?
mcqs.explanation: The cloacal 'rupture' hypothesis proposes that failure of integration of mesodermal, endodermal and ectodermal components of the cloacal plate causes it to rupture, leading to exposure of the bladder plate and urethra. The alternative 'wedge-effect' hypothesis postulates overgrowth of the cloacal membrane acts as a mechanical wedge separating mesodermal components. Neither adequately explains 'late rupture' or 'covered' variants.

mcqs.stem: A newborn girl is found to have bladder exstrophy. Which of the following best describes the typical female genital findings?

options.text: Separated clitoral corpora with a short urethral plate between the open bladder and vagina
Single fused clitoris with intact urethra
Bifid clitoris with urethra opening into the vagina

mcqs.explanation: In females with bladder exstrophy, the bladder component is identical to males. The clitoral corpora are separated with a short urethral plate between the open bladder and vagina.

mcqs.stem: A term male infant with classic bladder exstrophy undergoes uneventful primary closure on day 3 of life without osteotomy. Following catheter removal, which imaging study is most appropriate prior to discharge?
mcqs.explanation: When all catheters have been removed, a renal ultrasound scan is performed prior to discharge from hospital to assess bladder emptying and the appearances of the upper tracts.

mcqs.stem: In the modern staged repair of bladder exstrophy (MSRE), the typical timing of bladder neck reconstruction is:

options.text: At the time of neonatal closure

mcqs.explanation: In the modern staged repair (popularised by Jeffs and Gearhart), neonatal bladder closure is followed by correction of the epispadiac component at around 2 years of age and bladder neck reconstruction at around 5 years of age. If continence is not achieved due to inadequate capacity, further reconstruction may be needed.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is NOT a recognised predisposing factor for wound breakdown or bladder dehiscence after primary exstrophy closure?

options.text: Pooling of urine at the new urinary meatus
Stents emerging through the midline closure

mcqs.explanation: Frog-leg plasters are used for pelvic fixation postoperatively and do not predispose to dehiscence. Predisposing factors include pooling of urine at the new meatus, stents emerging through the midline closure, retention of the umbilical stump (a focus of infection), and tissue ischaemia from closure under tension.

mcqs.stem: Pelvic osteotomy for bladder exstrophy closure is most likely to be required in which scenario?

options.text: Delayed closure beyond the neonatal period
Newborn closure within 72 hours of birth
Closure within the first 2–3 weeks of life with a moderate diastasis
All exstrophy closures regardless of timing

mcqs.explanation: The pelvis in newborn exstrophy patients is sufficiently flexible to allow closure without osteotomy in 95% of cases in the first 2–3 weeks. Osteotomy is indicated when the pubic diastasis is wide, closure is delayed, or for revision cases and correction of cloacal exstrophy.

mcqs.stem: The overall rate of wound breakdown and bladder dehiscence complications following primary exstrophy closure is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: Complications including wound breakdown and partial or complete bladder dehiscence occur in around 10% of cases after primary exstrophy closure.

mcqs.stem: Regarding vesicoureteric reflux (VUR) after primary bladder closure in classic bladder exstrophy, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: Some degree of VUR is almost invariably present
VUR is rare because the ureters have normal oblique tunnels
VUR is seen in fewer than 10% of patients
VUR only occurs if the closure is performed under tension

mcqs.explanation: In classic bladder exstrophy, the ureters usually enter the bladder without the oblique transmural tunnel that would normally confer an anti-reflux mechanism. For this reason, some degree of VUR is almost invariably present after primary bladder closure.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old boy with repaired bladder exstrophy has persistent incontinence due to poor outlet resistance. Which minimally invasive option may be considered before resorting to open surgery?

options.text: Cystoscopic injection of a bulking agent (e.g. Deflux)
Transurethral resection of the bladder neck

mcqs.explanation: Incontinence due to poor outlet resistance can sometimes be reduced by cystoscopic injection of a bulking agent such as Deflux (dextranomer/hyaluronic acid copolymer) into the region of the bladder outlet. If this and similar measures fail, surgical closure of the bladder neck may be the only remaining option.

mcqs.stem: In the Kelly operation, detachment of the penile corpora from the inferior pubic rami is combined with release of which structure to achieve penile mobility?

options.text: The pudendal pedicles in Alcock's canal
The suspensory ligament of the penis

mcqs.explanation: Detachment of the penile corpora from the inferior pubic rami is combined with release of the pudendal pedicles, which lie beneath the pelvic floor muscles in Alcock's canal, running from the greater sciatic notch to the base of the penis. This enables the two corporeal bodies to be brought together in the midline, eliminating the pubic diastasis effect and increasing penile protrusion.

mcqs.stem: The senior author's proposed grading system for continence after exstrophy surgery defines Grade III as:

options.text: Dry by day and night; no protection or accidents
Dribbles urine all the time with no control
Able to retain urine with a dry interval but still wearing protection
Sufficient dry intervals by day but wet at night

mcqs.explanation: Grade III represents dryness by day and night with no protection or accidents, equivalent to a 'normal child' continence status. Grade 0 is dribbling with no control, Grade I is dry intervals with protection, and Grade II is dry by day but wet at night without needing protection.

mcqs.stem: In the senior author's series of exstrophy patients treated with the Kelly procedure and followed for 10 years, what proportion of males achieved dryness by day (Grade II)?
mcqs.explanation: In the cohort followed up for 10 years, 81% of males achieved dryness by day (Grade II) and 44% were dry by day and night (Grade III). Overall, 13% of males required bladder augmentation.

mcqs.stem: According to long-term follow-up data, the cumulative risk of malignancy in the reconstructed exstrophy bladder after 30 years is estimated to be approximately:
mcqs.explanation: The risk of malignancy in the reconstructed exstrophy bladder has been estimated to be in the region of 4% after 30 years of follow-up.

mcqs.stem: In males with classic bladder exstrophy, fertility is reduced to approximately what rate?
mcqs.explanation: The fertility rate in men with exstrophy is around 20%, probably due to disruption of the ejaculatory ducts during bladder neck surgery or consequences of episodes of epididymo-orchitis, which occurs in up to one-third of men.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following congenital anomalies is most commonly associated with classic bladder exstrophy?
mcqs.explanation: The incidence of other congenital anomalies is low in classic bladder exstrophy, with the exception of inguinal herniae, which are present in 80% of males and 15% of females.

mcqs.stem: Why did the UK adopt a policy of confining bladder exstrophy management to two supraregional centres?

options.text: To minimise treatment costs through bulk purchasing
The rarity of the condition made it difficult for individual urologists to acquire adequate experience
The required surgical equipment is only available in specialist centres
To facilitate uniform data collection for research

mcqs.explanation: The relative rarity of bladder exstrophy (1:50,000 live births) made it difficult for paediatric urologists to acquire adequate experience, as even major regional centres received only one or two new referrals a year. Centralising care to two supraregional centres enabled high levels of expertise and facilitated development of innovative approaches.

mcqs.stem: A newborn with classic bladder exstrophy is delivered at 34 weeks' gestation weighing 2.0 kg. What is the most appropriate initial approach?

options.text: Delayed closure after stabilisation, allowing time for weight gain
Osteotomy and closure at 1 week of age

mcqs.explanation: In premature infants, a longer period of stabilisation is preferable and it is not unreasonable to delay closure for several weeks if medically indicated.

mcqs.stem: The standard pelvic osteotomy technique for exstrophy involves division of the bony pelvis between which anatomical landmarks?

options.text: Anterior superior iliac spine and greater sciatic notch
Posterior superior iliac spine and sacroiliac joint
Anterior inferior iliac spine and lesser sciatic notch

mcqs.explanation: The standard technique consists of division of the bony pelvis between the anterior superior iliac spine and the greater sciatic notch on both sides. Drawbacks include prolonged operating time, increased blood loss and longer postoperative immobility.

mcqs.stem: A 3-month-old boy with classic bladder exstrophy underwent uncomplicated neonatal closure. His bladder capacity is adequate and he is voiding with some dribbling. According to the modern staged repair, the next planned procedure should be:

options.text: Epispadias repair at around 2 years of age

mcqs.explanation: In the modern staged repair (MSRE), after neonatal bladder closure, the next stage is correction of the epispadiac component at around 2 years of age, followed by bladder neck reconstruction around age 5. The MSRE was popularised by Jeffs and Gearhart.

mcqs.stem: The complete primary repair of bladder exstrophy differs from the staged approach primarily because it:

options.text: Delays epispadias repair until school age
Combines full anatomical reconstruction including penile reconstruction at the initial closure

mcqs.explanation: The complete primary repair, devised by Mitchell, combines full anatomical reconstruction with the initial bladder closure, including extensive mobilisation of the penile corpora for complete penile reconstruction during the same operation. It is technically challenging in a newborn and safeguarding the blood supply to the penis is of particular importance.

mcqs.stem: In the Kelly operation, the ureters are reimplanted using which technique?
mcqs.explanation: In the Kelly operation, the bladder is reopened in the midline and both ureters are reimplanted using the Cohen (cross-trigonal) technique.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following best describes the male genital anatomy in classic bladder exstrophy?

options.text: The exposed urethral plate extends over the dorsal surface of the corpora and glans
The urethral plate courses over the ventral surface of the corpora

mcqs.explanation: In male bladder exstrophy, the exposed urethral plate extends over the dorsal surface of the corpora and glans. The testes are usually descended. The scrotum is present with distinctive upwards direction of the rugae.

mcqs.stem: A 5-year-old boy with repaired bladder exstrophy has undergone bladder neck reconstruction but remains incontinent with small bladder capacity. The most appropriate next step is:

options.text: Cystoscopic injection of a bulking agent
Repeat bladder neck reconstruction with ileocystoplasty and Mitrofanoff conduit

mcqs.explanation: In children with severe incontinence after bladder neck reconstruction, it is usually necessary to perform a repeat bladder neck reconstruction in combination with ileocystoplasty and the formation of a Mitrofanoff catheterisable channel. Ideally this should be timed to enable the child to become continent by around age 6.

mcqs.stem: In the senior author's series of patients treated with the Kelly procedure, what proportion of females required bladder augmentation?
mcqs.explanation: Bladder augmentation was required in 33% of females (compared with 13% of males) in the senior author's series of 101 patients operated between 1999 and 2014.

mcqs.stem: Regarding sexual function in adult males with bladder exstrophy, which statement is correct?

options.text: 90% can achieve erections, though some require correction of persistent dorsal chordee

mcqs.explanation: Males have normal sexual libido and 90% can achieve erections, although some require corrective surgery for severe persistent dorsal chordee. The majority can experience orgasm but may have slow or retrograde ejaculation. Fertility is reduced to around 20%, not normal.

mcqs.stem: Woodhouse documented long-term renal damage in what proportion of exstrophy patients?
mcqs.explanation: Renal damage due to a combination of lower tract obstruction, vesicoureteric reflux and urinary infection was identified in 25% of patients in Woodhouse's long-term follow-up series.

mcqs.stem: In females with bladder exstrophy, which statement regarding pregnancy is correct?

options.text: Fertility is significantly reduced compared to the normal population
Pregnancy is often complicated by vaginal prolapse

mcqs.explanation: For females with exstrophy, the prospects for fertility are normal. Pregnancy is often complicated by vaginal prolapse, although this may prove less problematic following contemporary surgical procedures. Delivery by caesarean section is recommended, with a urologist in attendance if the patient has previously undergone bladder augmentation.

mcqs.stem: The incidence of primary epispadias is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: Primary epispadias occurs in approximately 1 in 120,000 live births with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 5:1 (Table 15.1).

mcqs.stem: In glanular epispadias, the urethral meatus is located on the:
mcqs.explanation: Glanular epispadias is the mildest form in which the urethra opens distally on the dorsal aspect of the glans. Epispadias involves a dorsal urethral opening, in contrast to hypospadias where the opening is ventral.

mcqs.stem: The Cantwell–Ransley repair for primary epispadias involves all of the following EXCEPT:

options.text: Mobilisation of the exposed urethral plate from the corporeal bodies
Relocation of the urethra to a ventral position

mcqs.explanation: The Cantwell–Ransley repair mobilises and tubularises the urethral plate (leaving it attached distally to the glans) and relocates the urethra ventrally, with approximation of the corpora and Heineke–Mikulicz type glans plasty. Release of pudendal pedicles is part of the Kelly operation, not the Cantwell–Ransley repair.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with lifelong stress incontinence that failed to improve with toilet training. Examination reveals a bifid clitoris and a short, wide urethra. The most likely diagnosis is:
mcqs.explanation: Female primary epispadias presents with a bifid clitoris, short and wide urethra, and deficient bladder neck leading to severe stress-type incontinence in all patients. The diagnosis is often considerably delayed because many junior paediatricians are unfamiliar with the appearances of the genitalia in this rare condition.

mcqs.stem: For a male with distal primary epispadias, good penile length and adequate penile size, the repair of choice is:
mcqs.explanation: The Cantwell–Ransley repair is indicated for boys with more distal epispadias if the penis is of good size, the verumontanum is distal to the bladder neck and they appear to void without dribbling. The Kelly operation is more appropriate for more severe cases with a pubic diastasis or smaller phallus.

mcqs.stem: In cloacal exstrophy, the approximate incidence of spinal dysraphism is:
mcqs.explanation: Spinal dysraphism (including myelomeningocoele, lipoma and tethered cord) occurs in approximately 50% of patients with cloacal exstrophy (Table 15.3). The sacrum is also foreshortened.

mcqs.stem: In cloacal exstrophy, the hindgut is typically managed by:

options.text: Tubularised distal hindgut brought out as an end colostomy

mcqs.explanation: A tubularised distal hindgut tube is created and usually brought out in the left iliac fossa as an end colostomy, in preference to an incontinent anal canal.

mcqs.stem: Historically, males with cloacal exstrophy were often assigned female gender at birth. This approach is no longer generally practised in the UK because:

options.text: Phallic reconstruction always produces a functional penis
There is greater understanding of the role of hormonal factors responsible for male gender imprinting in intrauterine life
Fertility rates are normal in males with cloacal exstrophy
Oestrogen therapy in utero has been shown to improve outcomes

mcqs.explanation: Female gender assignment for males with cloacal exstrophy is no longer generally practised in the UK because of greater understanding of the role of hormonal factors responsible for male gender imprinting in intrauterine life.

mcqs.stem: If insufficient small intestine is available for augmentation cystoplasty in cloacal exstrophy, which of the following is an alternative?

options.text: A patch of stomach or a hindgut segment

mcqs.explanation: If there is insufficient small intestine, alternatives for augmentation include a patch of stomach or a hindgut segment.

mcqs.stem: Colostomy complications in cloacal exstrophy occur in approximately what proportion of patients?
mcqs.explanation: Colostomy complications occur in around 50% of cloacal exstrophy patients, and it is occasionally necessary to remove the reconstructed hindgut and replace it with an ileostomy.

mcqs.stem: In a newborn with classic bladder exstrophy awaiting surgery, the exposed bladder plate should be:

options.text: Left exposed to air to allow drying
Covered with saline-soaked gauze changed hourly
Protected with plastic film inside the nappy
Covered with petroleum jelly impregnated gauze

mcqs.explanation: The exposed bladder plate should be protected with plastic film inside the nappy. Antibiotics or intravenous access are not routinely required at this stage. Urgent transfer to the specialist centre is rarely indicated.

mcqs.stem: Approximately what proportion of bladder exstrophy cases are detected antenatally?
mcqs.explanation: Approximately 50% of cases of bladder exstrophy are now detected antenatally, and in a further 20% of cases the diagnosis can be made retrospectively upon further review of the antenatal scans.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is a recognised antenatal ultrasound feature of bladder exstrophy?

options.text: Lack of urine in the fetal bladder and low-set umbilical cord

mcqs.explanation: Diagnostic antenatal features include lack of urine in the fetal bladder, low-set umbilical cord, short wide penis and bulging bladder plate. Maternal serum AFP is also elevated as a result of exposure of bladder mucosa to amniotic fluid.

mcqs.stem: Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is elevated in bladder exstrophy because of:

options.text: Exposure of bladder mucosa to amniotic fluid

mcqs.explanation: Maternal levels of serum AFP are elevated as a result of exposure of bladder mucosa to amniotic fluid. This is one of the diagnostic features that may raise suspicion of bladder exstrophy on antenatal screening.

mcqs.stem: Stone formation has been reported to occur in what proportion of exstrophy patients after reconstructive surgery?
mcqs.explanation: Stone formation has been reported to occur in up to 25% of exstrophy patients after reconstructive surgery, as documented in the late outcomes section of the chapter.

mcqs.stem: Epididymo-orchitis occurs in what proportion of men with bladder exstrophy?
mcqs.explanation: Episodes of epididymo-orchitis occur in up to one-third of men with bladder exstrophy, and these episodes contribute to reduced fertility alongside disruption of the ejaculatory ducts during bladder neck surgery.

mcqs.stem: According to Table 15.3, sacral agenesis occurs in what proportion of cloacal exstrophy cases?
mcqs.explanation: Sacral agenesis occurs in approximately 60% of cloacal exstrophy cases, according to Table 15.3. Other common associated anomalies include small bowel defects (65%), spinal dysraphism (50%) and orthopaedic deformity (40%).

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is true regarding the Kelly operation in females with bladder exstrophy?

options.text: Genital reconstruction requires multiple separate stages
Genital reconstruction can be successfully completed in a single stage

mcqs.explanation: In girls, the two components of the bifid clitoris are mobilised with preservation of their pudendal pedicles and the labia minora remain attached. Reconstruction of the bladder neck and urethra is undertaken in a similar fashion to males. Genital reconstruction can be successfully completed in a single stage in girls.

mcqs.stem: Patients with cloacal exstrophy who achieve continence inevitably require which procedure because of the combination of exstrophy and neuropathic dysfunction?
mcqs.explanation: The combination of exstrophy and neuropathic dysfunction inevitably means that all patients with cloacal exstrophy will require enterocystoplasty if they are to achieve continence and storage of urine at safe pressures. Most children can be successfully managed with ileocystoplasty and Mitrofanoff or Monti procedure.

mcqs.stem: According to the chapter, which of the following statements about primary epispadias is correct?

options.text: Primary epispadias is more common than bladder exstrophy
In girls, the diagnosis is often delayed because junior paediatricians are unaware of this rare anomaly
Urinary incontinence is rare in primary epispadias

mcqs.explanation: In girls, the diagnosis of primary epispadias is often considerably delayed because most junior paediatricians are unaware of this rare anomaly and fail to identify it on routine neonatal examination. The classic presentation is with dribbling or stress incontinence and failed toilet training. The condition is rarer than bladder exstrophy (1:120,000 vs 1:50,000) with a 5:1 male-to-female ratio.

mcqs.stem: A newborn is noted to have a ventral penile curvature, an abnormal ventral meatus, and a dorsal hooded foreskin. Which of the following best defines the embryologic basis of this condition?

options.text: Hypoplasia of the tissues forming the ventral aspect of the penis beyond the division of the corpus spongiosum
Failure of fusion of the urethral folds with intact corpus spongiosum
Excessive androgen receptor expression in the genital tubercle
Hyperplasia of the dorsal penile tissues with ventral migration of the meatus

mcqs.explanation: Hypospadias is defined as a hypoplasia of the tissues forming the ventral aspect of the penis beyond the division of the corpus spongiosum, characterized by a ventral triangular defect whose apex is the division of the corpus spongiosum.

mcqs.stem: The reported incidence of hypospadias varies geographically. Which of the following best represents the incidence in Europe?
mcqs.explanation: The incidence of hypospadias varies from 0.006–0.7% in Asia, 0.2% in Europe, and 0.34% in North America.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with proximal hypospadias and a left undescended testis. The parents are concerned about associated anomalies. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Full evaluation for disorders of sex development (DSD)
Routine renal ultrasound to screen for urinary tract anomalies
Observation as this is an expected association with no further workup needed
Cystoscopy to look for a prostatic utricle

mcqs.explanation: All patients with hypospadias who have undescended testes should be fully investigated for DSD, as up to 50% may have an underlying genetic, gonadal, or phenotypic abnormality.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following environmental pollutants has been associated with hypospadias as a potential endocrine disruptor?
mcqs.explanation: Phthalates, phytoestrogens (found in soy), and mycoestrogens have been identified as environmental pollutants associated with hypospadias, acting as endocrine disruptors.

mcqs.stem: A newborn is diagnosed with hypospadias. The parents have no family history of the condition. Which of the following genetic patterns is most likely, given that this is a distal form of hypospadias?
mcqs.explanation: Distal forms of hypospadias are more likely to occur as sporadic (non-inherited) anomalies, whereas genetic factors apply mainly to the more severe forms of proximal hypospadias.

mcqs.stem: The incidence of hypospadias in first-degree relatives is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: The incidence of hypospadias in first-degree relatives is of the order of 7–10%, rising to 20% in siblings of boys with severe hypospadias.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following genes, implicated in hypospadias, is involved in androgen production and signaling?
mcqs.explanation: SRD5A2 is involved in androgen production and signaling. SHH and WT1 are involved in genital tubercle development, and DGKK is also implicated in hypospadias but not specifically in androgen signaling.

mcqs.stem: A 6-month-old boy with perineal hypospadias is found to have bilateral undescended testes. The incidence of undescended testis in such severe forms of hypospadias is:
mcqs.explanation: The overall incidence of undescended testis is 5–10%, rising to 50% in those with severe perineal or penoscrotal forms of hypospadias.

mcqs.stem: The authors' classification of hypospadias recognizes three main types. Which of the following is the third category?

options.text: Hypospadias with a distal division of the corpus spongiosum
Hypospadias persisting despite multiple previous operations
Hypospadias with a proximal division of the corpus spongiosum

mcqs.explanation: The authors' classification recognizes: (1) hypospadias with distal division of corpus spongiosum, (2) proximal division with chordee, and (3) hypospadias persisting despite multiple previous operations (formerly 'hypospadias cripples').

mcqs.stem: A surgeon is planning a hypospadias repair and wants to classify the severity based on the level of division of the corpus spongiosum rather than the meatal position. This approach is preferred because:

options.text: Meatal position cannot be determined until surgical exploration
The meatal position always correlates with surgical complexity
Spongiosum division is visible on prenatal ultrasound
The level of spongiosum division better distinguishes anterior hypospadias with little or no chordee from posterior forms

mcqs.explanation: The level of division of the corpus spongiosum may provide a more accurate means of distinguishing anterior hypospadias with little or no chordee from posterior hypospadias with chordee and ventral hypoplasia.

mcqs.stem: A 9-month-old boy undergoes hypospadias repair. The surgeon degloves the penis and the chordee resolves. In what percentage of patients does simple degloving correct the chordee?
mcqs.explanation: Degloving the penis by fully mobilizing the overlying skin will correct the chordee in most patients (80%).

mcqs.stem: After degloving and excision of atretic spongiosum, ventral curvature persists in a child undergoing proximal hypospadias repair. The next appropriate step in chordee correction is:

options.text: Dissection and elevation of the urethral plate off the corpora cavernosa

mcqs.explanation: After degloving and excision of atretic spongiosum, the next step is dissection of the urethral plate, carefully elevating it off the corpora cavernosa. If chordee persists, dorsal plication is then performed.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old presents with recurrent ventral curvature after having undergone hypospadias repair in childhood. This phenomenon is attributed to:

options.text: Tendency for ventral curvature to recur at puberty despite dorsal plication
Failed initial chordee correction due to inadequate degloving

mcqs.explanation: Despite dorsal plication, there is a tendency for ventral curvature to recur at puberty. If dorsal plication is insufficient or if preferred, a more extensive corporal procedure (corporoplasty) may be indicated.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is NOT a cause of penile chordee in hypospadias?

options.text: Intrinsic flexion deformity of the corpora cavernosa
Abnormal tethering of the penile shaft skin
Tethering of the urethral plate to the corpora cavernosa
Dorsal atrophy of the corpus spongiosum

mcqs.explanation: Chordee results from: abnormal tethering of skin, tethering of the urethral plate to the corpora cavernosa, atretic corpora spongiosum tissue, or intrinsic flexion deformity of the corpora cavernosa. Dorsal atrophy is not described as a cause.

mcqs.stem: What is the recommended age range for surgical correction of hypospadias?
mcqs.explanation: Surgical correction is generally recommended between 6 months and 18 months of age to achieve optimal cosmetic and functional outcomes, and to complete surgery before genital awareness begins at around 18 months.

mcqs.stem: In the Snodgrass (TIP) urethroplasty, the urethral plate is incised vertically in the midline. What is the primary purpose of this incision?

options.text: To allow tubularization without tension and permit epithelialization that contributes to neourethral circumference
To excise the urethral plate completely
To create a dorsal graft bed

mcqs.explanation: The vertical incision (Snodgrass modification) allows the urethral plate to be tubularized without tension, and epithelialization of the incision may contribute to the circumference of the neourethra.

mcqs.stem: During a TIP repair, a surgeon places a vascularized de-epithelialized pedicle of tissue over the neourethra. The primary purpose of this step is to:

options.text: Augment the caliber of the neourethra
Provide blood supply to the urethral plate

mcqs.explanation: A vascularized de-epithelialized pedicle of tissue is placed over the anastomosis to reduce fistula formation by providing a 'waterproofing' layer between the neourethra and the skin.

mcqs.stem: A 10-month-old undergoes TIP urethroplasty. Regarding postoperative urinary drainage, which of the following is correct according to the authors?

options.text: A 6–8 Fr dripping stent is always required for a minimum of 14 days
A Foley catheter is preferred to prevent stent dislodgement
A stent is rarely needed in children under 12 months of age

mcqs.explanation: The authors note that a stent is rarely needed in children under 12 months of age. No stent is necessary in many infants undergoing surgery for distal hypospadias.

mcqs.stem: A patient has a urethral plate that is wide and sufficiently supple to be tubularized over an 8 Fr catheter without needing a relaxing incision. This technique is known as the:
mcqs.explanation: If the urethral plate is wide and supple enough to be tubularized over an 8 Fr catheter without needing a relaxing incision, it can be rolled into a tube using the Duplay procedure (simple tubularization).

mcqs.stem: In the TIP repair, some surgeons modify the technique by inlaying a free graft into the incision in the urethral plate. Which of the following is the rationale for this modification?

options.text: To increase the vascularity of the urethral plate
To reduce the risk of contraction and stenosis of the neourethra

mcqs.explanation: Some surgeons favor inlaying a free graft of preputial skin into the incision in the urethral plate in the hope of reducing the risk of contraction and stenosis of the neourethra.

mcqs.stem: A child with severe proximal hypospadias with significant chordee is scheduled for repair. Which of the following is the most appropriate surgical approach?
mcqs.explanation: Two-stage repair is generally reserved for severe proximal or perineal hypospadias with chordee and for 'hypospadias cripples'. Single-stage repair is appropriate for distal, midshaft, and proximal hypospadias without significant chordee.

mcqs.stem: In the first stage of a two-stage hypospadias repair, after chordee correction, the most commonly used graft material is:
mcqs.explanation: The most commonly used graft material is inner preputial skin. Postauricular Wolfe skin grafts can also be used for primary repair. Buccal mucosa and bladder mucosa are less frequently used for standard two-stage repairs but have a role in salvage repairs.

mcqs.stem: During the first stage of a two-stage repair, 'windows' are made in the graft and midline quilting sutures are placed. The purpose of the windows is to:

options.text: Increase graft vascularity from the sides

mcqs.explanation: 'Windows' are made in the graft to allow hematomas to escape, and a few midline quilting sutures are placed to anchor the graft to its base.

mcqs.stem: The interval between the first and second stages of a two-stage hypospadias repair is typically:
mcqs.explanation: After 1 week the dressing and catheter from the first stage are removed. The second stage is usually performed after 6 months. This step involves tubularization of the graft into the neourethra.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following procedures is suitable only for cases in which the urethra is mobile and simple traction can move the meatus to the tip of the glans?
mcqs.explanation: The MAGPI (meatal advancement and glanuloplasty incorporated) procedure is only suitable for cases in which the urethra is mobile, confirmed if simple traction can move the meatus to the tip of the glans.

mcqs.stem: A 3-year-old boy underwent the MAGPI procedure for glanular hypospadias. On follow-up, the meatus has moved back proximally. This complication is known as:
mcqs.explanation: Partial meatal regression is a relatively common problem with the MAGPI procedure and leads to an inferior cosmetic result.

mcqs.stem: The Mathieu (meatal-based flap) procedure is limited by the maximum flap length, which should be no more than:

options.text: Three times the width of the base
Two times the width of the base

mcqs.explanation: The maximum length of the Mathieu flap should be no more than three times the width of the base, and this technique is unsuited for a urethroplasty exceeding 1.5 cm.

mcqs.stem: A cosmetic drawback commonly associated with the Mathieu procedure is:

options.text: A crescentic meatus giving a poor cosmetic result

mcqs.explanation: In the Mathieu repair, the meatus is often crescentic in appearance, giving a poor cosmetic result. Wide glans mobilization and a midline dorsal incision of the urethral plate may reduce this problem.

mcqs.stem: In the preputial onlay island flap procedure, after creating the pedicle flap from the inner prepuce, the flap is brought to the ventral surface:

options.text: Around the side of the penis
Through a buttonhole in the scrotum

mcqs.explanation: The preputial flap is brought around the side of the penis to its ventral surface and sutured to the urethral plate.

mcqs.stem: The Koyanagi procedure for severe proximal hypospadias utilizes which of the following tissues to create an extended urethral plate?

options.text: The urethral plate, neighboring ventral strip tissues, and the inner aspect of the preputial hood

mcqs.explanation: The Koyanagi procedure uses the urethral plate, the neighboring strip of ventral tissues, and the inner aspect of the preputial hood transferred ventrally with its pedicle. This large vascularized material allows the penis to straighten and an extended plate to be built.

mcqs.stem: A surgeon is managing a child with chordee but a normally located meatus on the glans with a circumferential prepuce. Which of the following statements is most accurate?

options.text: This is a distinct entity unrelated to hypospadias
This represents a form of hypospadias where the distal penile urethra is flimsy despite a glanular meatus
This is always caused by a short urethra

mcqs.explanation: Chordee without hypospadias is usually a misnomer—it represents a form of hypospadias in which the distal penile urethra is flimsy despite the presence of a glanular meatus and circumferential prepuce.

mcqs.stem: The megameatus intact prepuce (MIP) variant is most commonly discovered:

options.text: In a boy about to undergo circumcision for cultural or medical reasons
When the child starts toilet training

mcqs.explanation: Because there is no external clue to the presence of MIP, it sometimes comes to light for the first time in a boy who is about to undergo circumcision for cultural or medical reasons.

mcqs.stem: A child presents with a urethrocutaneous fistula 3 months after hypospadias repair. Before attempting fistula repair, it is imperative to:

options.text: Exclude meatal and/or distal urethral strictures
Wait at least 2 years after the original repair

mcqs.explanation: Before attempting to repair a fistula, it is imperative to exclude meatal and/or distal urethral strictures, since the presence of distal obstruction will predispose to recurrence of the fistula.

mcqs.stem: A 5-year-old boy develops a urethrocutaneous fistula after hypospadias repair. What is the recommended minimum interval between the original repair and fistula closure?
mcqs.explanation: Although early closure was favored in the past, it is now recommended that an interval of at least 6 months should elapse between the original repair and fistula closure.

mcqs.stem: Simple closure (freshening and closing the fistula edges and overlying skin) for a hypospadias fistula is:

options.text: Associated with a high recurrence rate and not recommended by the authors

mcqs.explanation: Simple closure is associated with a high recurrence rate and is not recommended by the authors. A flap-based repair with a vascularized subcutaneous layer is more effective, with a 90% success rate.

mcqs.stem: A large fistula is identified after hypospadias repair that represents a failure of the original urethroplasty. The most appropriate management is:
mcqs.explanation: When a fistula is very large and represents a failure of the original urethroplasty, surgery limited to fistula closure is generally doomed to failure. A formal repeat urethroplasty is required for a satisfactory result.

mcqs.stem: A child presents with a narrowed urinary stream and spraying after hypospadias repair. On examination, the meatal caliber is less than 8 Fr. This complication is best managed initially with:
mcqs.explanation: Meatal stenosis (<8 Fr caliber) can be treated initially with gentle dilatation, which can be an outpatient procedure for minor degrees or under general anesthesia for moderate/severe stenosis.

mcqs.stem: Urethral stenosis after hypospadias repair has become a rare complication because:

options.text: Modern procedures avoid a circular (circumferential) anastomosis

mcqs.explanation: Urethral stenosis has become a rare complication because modern procedures avoid a circular (circumferential) anastomosis, which was a major risk factor for stricture formation.

mcqs.stem: A baggy, dilated section of neourethra is noted in a child after hypospadias repair. This is associated with postvoid dribbling and poor urinary stream. Which of the following is the most appropriate term for this finding?
mcqs.explanation: Urethrocele refers to a dilated, 'baggy' section of neourethra, often resulting from distal obstruction causing 'back pressure' distension, or from absence/deficiency of supportive corpus spongiosum.

mcqs.stem: Balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO) after hypospadias surgery is characterized by:

options.text: Acute bacterial infection of the glans requiring intravenous antibiotics
Chronic inflammation and fibrosis of the glans and meatus causing scarring and stenosis

mcqs.explanation: BXO is a rare complication caused by chronic inflammation and fibrosis of the glans and meatus, resulting in scarring and meatal stenosis. Topical steroids help some patients, but most require formal meatoplasty.

mcqs.stem: According to the authors, which of the following is their preferred method for achieving a bloodless operating field during hypospadias surgery?
mcqs.explanation: The authors' current policy is to use a tourniquet followed by a compressive dressing at the end of the operation. A tourniquet, adrenaline, and bipolar diathermy are the three described techniques.

mcqs.stem: The suture material currently preferred by the authors for hypospadias repair is:
mcqs.explanation: The authors currently prefer 7-0 or 6-0 polydioxanone (PDS): fine absorbable monofilament sutures that provide excellent strength and are absorbed before suture tracts form.

mcqs.stem: A surgeon is planning a salvage hypospadias repair where there is extensive scarring and residual chordee. Which of the following graft materials is most widely used for this purpose?
mcqs.explanation: For salvage repair with extensive scarring and residual chordee, buccal mucosa (harvested from the lower lip or cheek) is more widely used. Postauricular skin is suitable but buccal mucosa is the preferred option.

mcqs.stem: A child is undergoing assessment before hypospadias repair and the glans width is measured at 12 mm. Regarding the association between glans size and surgical outcomes:

options.text: Larger glans width predicts higher fistula rates
Glans width has no correlation with surgical outcomes
A glans width ≤14 mm is associated with an increased risk of complications

mcqs.explanation: Anatomic factors such as glans width correlate with surgical outcomes, with a glans width ≤14 mm being associated with an increased risk of complications.

mcqs.stem: Regarding preoperative testosterone administration before hypospadias repair, which of the following best describes the authors' position?

options.text: It is recommended only for proximal hypospadias
It is contraindicated due to risk of premature epiphyseal closure
It is strongly recommended for all patients
Due to paucity of literature and insufficient evidence, the authors do not advocate its use

mcqs.explanation: Some surgeons administer preoperative testosterone to increase penile length, glans circumference, and tissue vascularity. However, due to paucity of literature and insufficient evidence, the authors do not advocate its use.

mcqs.stem: The re-operation rate following correction of distal hypospadias is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: Re-operation rates following correction of distal hypospadias are approximately 5%, but rise to 50% for proximal hypospadias and 23% following secondary repairs.

mcqs.stem: In Bracka's series of 600 patients managed by two-stage repair, the overall fistula rate following the second procedure was:
mcqs.explanation: In Bracka's series, the overall fistula rate following the second procedure was 6%, with 'redo' or 'salvage' repairs having a higher fistula rate of 10% compared with 3% for primary procedures.

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old man who underwent hypospadias repair in childhood is concerned about sexual function. Which of the following is most accurate regarding psychosexual outcomes?

options.text: Most men report unsatisfactory sexual function
Up to 80% of men report satisfactory sexual function, but they may be more apprehensive about seeking sexual contact
All men eventually develop erectile dysfunction
Sexual function is universally unaffected with no psychological impact

mcqs.explanation: Studies have found that the majority of men (80%) report satisfactory sexual function, but they may be more apprehensive to seek out sexual contact. Fertility is normal unless there are coexisting undescended testes.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old man who underwent hypospadias correction in childhood has no recollection of his surgery. Compared with men who remember undergoing hypospadias surgery, this patient is more likely to have:

options.text: No difference in any measured outcome
Improved body image and greater satisfaction with overall appearance

mcqs.explanation: Men who had no recollection of their surgery had an improved body image and greater satisfaction with their overall appearance when compared with men who could remember having undergone hypospadias surgery. This is why surgical correction is recommended before 18 months of age.

mcqs.stem: Severe perineal or penoscrotal hypospadias is associated with a persistent prostatic utricle in approximately what percentage of cases?
mcqs.explanation: Severe forms of hypospadias (perineal or penoscrotal) are associated with a persistent prostatic utricle in 20% of cases. When present, it can create difficulties with urethral catheterization of the bladder.

essay.title: Hypospadias Classification and Preoperative Assessment
essay.scenario: A 14-month-old boy is referred by his GP with a history of the urinary stream spraying from the ventral aspect of the penis since birth. His parents have noticed that the foreskin is hooded dorsally and the penis appears slightly curved during erection. On examination in clinic, the urethral meatus is noted to be on the distal shaft. You plan to take the child to the operating theatre for assessment under anaesthesia and definitive correction.

parts.text: the anatomical subtypes of hypospadias based on meatal location, listing the structures encountered along the ventral penile surface from glans to perineum.
the GAP (glans-urethral meatus angle) classification and its relevance to assessing chordee in hypospadias.
the step-by-step technique for performing an artificial erection test under anaesthesia to quantify chordee. Include the expected findings for mild, moderate, and severe ventral curvature.
the key anatomical features that must be documented before planning surgical correction in this patient. Address the significance of glans size, urethral plate width, and degree of chordee.
a management algorithm for a child found to have 40 degrees of ventral chordee on artificial erection with a mid-shaft meatus. Include decision points for single-stage versus two-stage repair.

essay.markingGuide: Part (a) — 6 marks: 1 mark for each correct level: (1) glanular, (2) coronal, (3) distal penile, (4) mid-penile, (5) proximal penile, (6) penoscrotal/scrotal/perineal. Accept either 5 or 6 levels; credit any logical subdivision. Must mention ventral ectopic meatus. Common error: omitting penoscrotal junction. Part (b) — 6 marks: 2 marks for defining GAP as angle between glans axis and urethral meatus, 2 marks for normal erection (straight vs curved), 2 marks for clinical utility in objectively measuring chordee. Distinction candidates relate GAP to risk of persistent curvature after degloving. Part (c) — 12 marks: 1 each for: (1) prep and drape with tourniquet, (2) 25G butterfly needle, (3) sterile saline injection into corpus cavernosum, (4) gradual injection, (5) observe erection quality, (6) measure chordee with goniometer, (7) <10° mild, (8) 10–30° moderate, (9) >30° severe, (10) document after degloving, (11) repeat after dorsal plication if performed, (12) photograph for records. Trap: injecting into corpus spongiosum produces false erection. Part (d) — 12 marks: 2 each for: (1) meatal position and quality (stenotic vs wide), (2) urethral plate width and elasticity, (3) glans size and configuration (flat vs conical vs deep groove), (4) degree of chordee (ventral vs lateral), (5) penile length and scrotal anomalies (transposition, penoscrotal webbing), (6) prepuce quality (dorsal hood vs circumcised). Common error: forgetting to assess prepuce quality for preputial onlay flaps. Part (e) — 14 marks: Award 2 marks for distinguishing degloving alone vs formal correction. Algorithm: (1) degloving alone — re-assess chordee; (2) if <30° after degloving — dorsal plication (Nesbit or Baskin) TIP; (3) if 30–45° after degloving — consider urethral plate transection and two-stage (Bracka); (4) if >45° — two-stage repair; (5) single-stage TIP acceptable if plate wide and chordee resolves; (6) single-stage with dorsal plication if mild residual; (7) two-stage with buccal graft if proximal and severe. Trap: attempting TIP with narrow plate and severe chordee leads to fistula/stenosis.
essay.modelAnswer: Part (a): Hypospadias is classified by meatal position following penile degloving. From distal to proximal: glanular (within glans), coronal (at corona), distal penile (subcoronal to midshaft), mid-penile, proximal penile, penoscrotal (at penoscrotal junction), scrotal, and perineal (most proximal). The ventral structures that fail to fuse include the corpus spongiosum, Buck fascia, dartos fascia, and skin. The foreskin is classically deficient ventrally with a dorsal hood. Part (b): The GAP classification measures the angle between the long axis of the glans and the urethral meatus during erection. Normal males have a GAP of 0–10° (straight). Hypospadias patients with chordee have an increased GAP, reflecting ventral tethering. This provides an objective, reproducible measure of chordee severity. A GAP >30° correlates with significant chordee requiring correction. Part (c): After induction and aseptic preparation, a tourniquet is applied at the penile base. A 25-gauge butterfly needle is inserted into one corpus cavernosum. Sterile normal saline (or 1:1000 heparinised saline) is injected slowly until full erection is achieved. The degree of ventral curvature is measured using a goniometer or protractor. Mild = <10°, moderate = 10–30°, severe = >30°. The artificial erection is repeated after penile degloving to determine if curvature persists (intrinsic chordee) or resolves (cutaneous chordee). Findings are documented photographically. Part (d): Key features: (1) Meatal position and morphology — the meatus may be stenotic, wide, or slit-like; meatal calibre determines whether meatotomy is needed. (2) Urethral plate — width and quality from meatus to glans tip; a plate <8 mm may not tubularise easily and a narrow plate risks stricture. (3) Glans size and shape — a small, flat glans makes TIP repair difficult; a deep urethral groove is favourable. (4) Chordee — degree and whether it persists after degloving determines the need for dorsal plication or urethral plate transaction. (5) Penile length and scrotal anomalies — penoscrotal transposition or webbing influences surgical approach. (6) Prepuce — if present, may be used for preputial onlay or tubularised flaps. Part (e): Algorithm: (1) Perform initial artificial erection → 40° chordee. (2) Penile degloving — reflect skin and dartos to penile base. (3) Repeat artificial erection → if chordee <30°, proceed with dorsal plication (midline plication at 12 o'clock or Baskin technique) and perform TIP urethroplasty if urethral plate width >8 mm and glans adequate. (4) If chordee 30–45° after degloving, transect the urethral plate at the point of maximal curvature and measure the resultant gap. (5) If gap <1.5 cm and plate proximal quality good, consider staged repair: first stage — straightening with urethral plate transection and glans wings; second stage — tubularisation of neourethra after 6 months. (6) If gap >1.5 cm or severe chordee >45°, perform first-stage Bracka with buccal mucosal graft inlay. (7) After 6 months proceed to second-stage tubularisation. A single-stage TIP with dorsal plication is appropriate only if chordee is <30° after degloving and urethral plate is healthy. Single-stage must not compromise chordee correction.
distinguishingFeatures: Uses objective chordee measurement (GAP/goniometer) rather than subjective grading
Describes decision algorithm that acknowledges degloving alone can resolve apparent chordee
Recognises that urethral plate quality (width >8 mm) determines suitability for TIP
Correctly identifies that >30° residual chordee after degloving warrants plate transection and two-stage repair

essay.title: Surgical Techniques in Distal Hypospadias Repair
essay.scenario: A 10-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric urology clinic with glanular hypospadias. The meatus is at the tip of the glans but ventrally placed within a shallow groove. There is no chordee on examination and the prepuce is intact. The parents are very anxious and have researched MAGPI and Snodgrass (TIP) procedures online. They ask you which technique is best for their son and what the expected outcomes and complication rates are.

parts.text: the indications and contraindications for MAGPI (meatal advancement and glanuloplasty) versus tubularised incised plate (TIP/Snodgrass) urethroplasty for distal hypospadias.
the key steps of the Snodgrass (TIP) urethroplasty, including the incision of the urethral plate and why it is necessary.
the published evidence comparing complication rates (fistula, meatal stenosis, glans dehiscence, urethral stricture) between MAGPI and TIP procedures for distal hypospadias.
the principles of fistula prevention in hypospadias surgery. Detail the role of multilayer closure, flap coverage, and urinary diversion in reducing fistula formation.
the long-term outcomes of hypospadias repair from the patient's perspective, including cosmetic appearance, urinary function, and sexual function. How should you counsel the parents preoperatively?

essay.markingGuide: Part (a) — 10 marks: 2 each for: (1) MAGPI indicated for glanular hypospadias with non-stenotic, mobile meatus and good urethral plate mobility, (2) MAGPI contraindicated when chordee >15°, stenotic meatus, or shallow glans groove, (3) TIP suitable for glanular to distal penile with deep urethral groove and plate width >8 mm, (4) TIP can correct mild chordee (<30°) if plate is healthy, (5) both techniques require adequate glans size. Trap: MAGPI in a shallow groove results in meatal regression. Part (b) — 10 marks: 1 per step: (1) stay suture in glans, (2) U-shaped incision around meatus with parallel limbs in glans groove, (3) degloving penile shaft, (4) artificial erection to confirm no chordee, (5) midline incision of urethral plate from meatus to tip, (6) tubularise plate over 8Fr stent with 7/0 absorbable suture, (7) second layer (dartos flap) coverage, (8) glansplasty with interrupted sutures, (9) meatoplasty at tip, (10) dressing and stent. Why incision: relaxes plate, widens neourethra to 14–16Fr. Part (c) — 10 marks: 2 each: (1) fistula rates: TIP 5–10% vs MAGPI <3% for distal cases, (2) meatal stenosis: TIP 2–5% vs MAGPI 5–10% (plication effect), (3) glans dehiscence: TIP 3–5% vs MAGPI 1–2%, (4) urethral stricture: TIP 1–2% (rare), MAGPI essentially 0%, (5) reoperation rate: TIP 7–12% vs MAGPI 5–8%. Credit reference to ESPU/BAPS audit data. Common error: quoting fistula rates above 20% for primary distal TIP — recall bias for complex cases. Part (d) — 10 marks: 2 each: (1) multilayer closure with at least two layers of epithelial and subepithelial tissue, (2) dartos flap coverage (de-epithelialised or island flap) to separate neourethra from skin suture lines, (3) tension-free anastomosis with spatulation, (4) appropriate stent size (8–10Fr) for 5–7 days, (5) avoid urethral catheter traction and early voiding against resistance. Trap: not using dartos flap increases fistula rate 2–3×. Part (e) — 10 marks: 2 each: (1) cosmetic: straight erection, meatus at tip, normal slit-like urinary stream — 80–90% good/excellent, (2) urinary: peak flow rates comparable to controls, but reduced flow in 10–15% (not usually symptomatic), (3) sexual: long-term data show normal ejaculation and sensation in >90% after TIP, (4) psychological: boys with hypospadias have higher rates of anxiety about genital appearance — sensitive preoperative counselling essential, (5) realistic expectations: 70–80% single-stage success, up to 20% may need secondary procedure.
essay.modelAnswer: Part (a): MAGPI is indicated for glanular hypospadias with a mobile, non-stenotic meatus situated at or within the glans, a good urethral plate that advances easily, and no significant chordee. It is contraindicated when there is a shallow glans groove, >15° chordee, a stenotic meatus that would cause tension, or a meatus that cannot be advanced to the tip. TIP (Snodgrass) urethroplasty is indicated for glanular to distal penile hypospadias with a deep urethral groove and a plate width >8 mm. It can correct mild chordee (<30°) if the chordee disappears after degloving. TIP is contraindicated when the urethral plate is narrow, atretic, or severely scarred (e.g., previous surgery), or when chordee >30° persists after degloving, in which case plate transection and two-stage repair is preferred. Part (b): Key steps: (1) Stay suture in glans for traction. (2) Mark U-shaped incision around the meatus with parallel limbs extending to the glans tip within the urethral groove. (3) Penile degloving: incise ventrally and reflect skin and dartos to penile base. (4) Artificial erection: confirm no or minimal chordee. (5) Midline relaxing incision of the urethral plate from within the meatus to the glans tip, deepening until Buck fascia is visible — this widens the plate from 8–10 mm to 14–16 Fr calibre. (6) Tubularise the incised plate over an 8Fr silicone stent using 7/0 or 6/0 absorbable suture (e.g., Monocryl or PDS) in a continuous subepithelial fashion. (7) Harvest a dorsal dartos flap (de-epithelialised preputial or shaft skin) and transpose ventrally to cover the neourethra. (8) Glansplasty: approximate glans wings over the neourethra in two layers. (9) Meatoplasty: suture neourethra to glans epithelium at the tip. (10) Dressing and stent secured for 5–7 days. The relaxing incision is necessary because the native urethral plate is usually too narrow (8–10 mm) to tubularise to an adequate calibre; incising it down to corpus spongiosum allows the plate to epithelialise to a wider calibre (up to 14–16 Fr) by secondary healing. Part (c): Published meta-analyses (e.g., Wilkinson et al. 2012, Huang et al. 2016) show fistula rates of 5–10% for TIP vs <3% for MAGPI in primary distal hypospadias. Meatal stenosis is higher with MAGPI (5–10%) due to the circumferential plication effect, vs 2–5% with TIP if the relaxing incision is adequate. Glans dehiscence is 3–5% for TIP vs 1–2% for MAGPI — often related to glans size <14 mm. Urethral stricture is rare for both (1–2% TIP, essentially 0% MAGPI). Overall reoperation rates are 7–12% for TIP and 5–8% for MAGPI. Important caveat: these data reflect experienced surgeons; TIP has been shown to be reproducible by trainees with comparable outcomes. The choice should be individualised based on anatomy rather than assumed superiority of one technique. Part (d): Fistula prevention begins with (1) tension-free anastomosis — spatulate the neourethra to prevent a narrow-calibre junction. (2) Multilayer closure: a running subepithelial layer inverts epithelium; a second layer of spongiosum or subcutaneous tissue reinforces the suture line. (3) A well-vascularised dartos flap (de-epithelialised dorsal preputial or lateral shaft skin) is rotated ventrally to interpose between the neourethra and skin closure, which reduces fistula rate by 50–70%. (4) Urinary diversion: an 8Fr silicone stent diverts urine away from the repair for 5–7 days; transurethral catheter may be used for complex repairs.
