(5) Avoid over-distension of the neourethra — appropriate stent size and prevention of catheter traction. The most critical factor is the interposition dartos flap; lack of flap coverage increases fistula risk 2–3 fold. Part (e): Long-term outcomes: (1) Cosmetic appearance — 80–90% of patients and parents rate outcome as good or excellent after TIP for distal hypospadias. The meatus should be slit-like and at the tip. (2) Urinary function — uroflowmetry shows peak flow rates within normal range in most patients, but up to 15% have mildly reduced flow, usually asymptomatic. (3) Sexual function — long-term studies show normal ejaculation, sensation, and erectile function in >90% of TIP patients. (4) Psychosexual — some adolescents report embarrassment about penile appearance even after successful repair; preoperative counselling should address body image. Preoperative counselling should include: 70–80% success in a single stage, up to 20% may require secondary procedure (fistula repair, meatoplasty); appearance will be near-normal but not identical to an uncircumcised penis; urinary function is expected to be normal; long-term sexual function is normal; and the child should be informed about his surgery at an appropriate developmental stage.
distinguishingFeatures: Provides objective comparison of MAGPI vs TIP with evidence-based complication rates
Explains the physiological rationale for the midline relaxing incision in TIP
Emphasises dartos flap interposition as the critical step in fistula reduction
Addresses patient-reported outcomes and psychological aspects of genital surgery

essay.title: Fistula Management After Hypospadias Repair
essay.scenario: A 3-year-old boy presented 6 months ago with a proximal penile hypospadias and underwent a Snodgrass (TIP) urethroplasty. He passed urine well initially. His mother now reports a second urinary stream from the ventral aspect of the penis during micturition. On examination a 2 mm fistula is identified at the coronal sulcus. The meatus appears normal and there is no evidence of meatal stenosis or urethral stricture.

parts.text: four predisposing factors associated with fistula formation after hypospadias repair.
why a minimum waiting period of 6 months is recommended before attempting fistula repair.
the preoperative investigations required to assess the urethra before fistula closure.
the surgical principles of fistula closure, including excision, multilayer closure, and flap coverage.
the management options if the fistula is found to be associated with distal urethral stricture at the time of surgery.

essay.markingGuide: Part (a) — 4 marks (1 each): (1) distal obstruction (meatal stenosis or urethral stricture), (2) inadequate flap coverage between neourethra and skin suture lines, (3) infection or ischaemia of the neourethra, (4) narrow urethral plate (<8 mm) necessitating tight tubularisation or high-tension closure. Also accept: poor vascularity, use of mono-filament vs braided suture, or postoperative catheter trauma. Part (b) — 4 marks (2 each): (1) wound maturity — oedema and inflammation from the primary repair need to resolve fully to allow healthy vascularised tissue for reoperation, (2) to distinguish persistent fistula from delayed healing — small fistulae may close spontaneously within 6 months if there is no distal obstruction. Trap: operating earlier risks recurrence due to poor tissue quality. Part (c) — 5 marks (1 each): (1) careful physical examination including meatal calibration with 8Fr feeding tube, (2) observation of voiding stream, (3) retrograde urethrogram (RUG) to delineate urethral anatomy, (4) voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) to assess for stricture or diverticulum, (5) urethroscopy under anaesthesia immediately before repair. Common error: proceeding without imaging and missing an unrecognised stricture. Part (d) — 6 marks (1–2 each): (1) elliptical incision around fistula tract excising scarred epithelium to healthy tissue, (2) closure of neourethra in 2 layers (subepithelial running 7/0 absorbable, second layer of spongiosum/subcutaneous), (3) dartos or tunica vaginalis flap interposition between urethral closure and skin, (4) ensure no distal obstruction before closure — calibrate meatus and urethra, (5) tension-free skin closure with fine absorbable sutures and urine diversion (stent) for 5–7 days. Trap: simple closure without flap coverage has >50% recurrence. Part (e) — 6 marks: (1) perform urethroscopy to confirm stricture location and severity, (2) for short (<5 mm) stricture — visual internal urethrotomy (VIU) or dilation, followed by fistula repair, (3) for long (>5 mm) or recurrent stricture — formal urethral reconstruction with excision of stricture and redo urethroplasty, (4) staged approach may be needed — correct stricture first, allow healing, then repair fistula at a separate sitting, (5) buccal mucosa onlay graft for long anterior urethral strictures, (6) urine diversion with suprapubic catheter if repair is complex. Trap: repairing fistula without addressing distal stricture guarantees recurrence.
essay.modelAnswer: (a) Four predisposing factors: (1) distal urethral obstruction (meatal stenosis or stricture), (2) absence of a dartos/tunica vaginalis barrier flap between neourethra and skin closure, (3) infection or haematoma compromising neourethral vascularity, (4) high-tension tubularisation of a narrow urethral plate (<8 mm). (b) A minimum 6-month waiting period is recommended because (1) it allows complete resolution of postoperative oedema and inflammation, providing well-vascularised healing tissue for reoperation, and (2) some small fistulae close spontaneously within this period if there is no distal obstruction — operating before 6 months risks operating on a temporary problem and leads to higher recurrence. (c) Preoperative investigations: (1) clinical examination with meatal calibration using an 8Fr feeding tube to exclude distal stenosis; (2) observation of voiding to assess for a poor stream; (3) retrograde urethrogram to delineate the full urethral anatomy and identify stricture, diverticulum, or other fistulae; (4) voiding cystourethrogram to assess for posterior urethral pathology; (5) urethroscopy immediately before repair to directly visualise the fistula, check for synchronous strictures, and confirm urethral plate quality. (d) Surgical principles: (1) excise the fistula tract in an elliptical fashion through healthy tissue; (2) close the neourethra in two layers using 7/0 absorbable sutures — first a subepithelial running suture to invert the epithelium, then a second layer of spongiosal or subcutaneous tissue; (3) harvest an interposition flap — either a local dartos flap or tunica vaginalis flap — to separate the urethral closure from the skin suture line; (4) confirm absence of distal obstruction by calibrating the meatus and distal urethra before committing to closure; (5) close skin without tension and divert urine with a 6–8Fr stent for 5–7 days. (e) If a distal urethral stricture is found: (1) confirm with urethroscopy; (2) for a short (<5 mm) stricture, perform visual internal urethrotomy or serial dilation and then proceed with fistula repair; (3) for a longer (>5 mm) or recurrent stricture, excise the strictured segment and perform a formal redo urethroplasty (onlay buccal mucosal graft); (4) consider a staged approach: repair the stricture first, allow 6 months of healing, then close the fistula in a second stage; (5) in complex cases, divert urine with a suprapubic catheter and schedule staged reconstruction. The fistula must not be closed until the stricture is definitively corrected, or the fistula will inevitably recur.
distinguishingFeatures: Emphasises 6-month waiting period and the rationale for delayed repair
Highlights mandatory preoperative imaging to exclude distal obstruction
Stresses the absolute requirement for flap interposition to prevent recurrence
Recognises that distal stricture must be treated simultaneously or fistula will recur

essay.title: Preoperative Hormone Therapy in Hypospadias
essay.scenario: A 14-month-old boy with proximal penoscrotal hypospadias is assessed for surgery. The glans is small (diameter 10 mm) and flat. The urethral plate is narrow (6 mm). The surgeon is considering the use of preoperative testosterone or HCG to augment penile size before the planned single-stage TIP urethroplasty.

parts.text: the physiological rationale for using androgen stimulation before hypospadias repair.
the regimens for preoperative testosterone and HCG, including dose, route and timing relative to surgery.
the published evidence from randomised trials and meta-analyses on the effect of preoperative androgens on surgical outcomes (complication rates, glans dimensions, penile length).
the potential adverse effects of preoperative androgen therapy in infants and young children.
whether you would use preoperative androgen therapy in this patient. Justify your decision based on the anatomy, evidence, and risk-benefit balance.

essay.markingGuide: Part (a) — 4 marks: (1) Hypospadias is a form of incomplete virilisation; the penis and glans are often hypoplastic for age, (2) androgens stimulate penile growth by increasing penile length, glans circumference, and vascularity, (3) larger glans allows better glansplasty and neourethra accommodation, (4) improved penile length and skin quality facilitates flap harvesting and wound closure. Common error: suggesting hormones repair the underlying embryological defect — they only augment existing tissues. Part (b) — 5 marks: Testosterone: (1) testosterone enanthate 2 mg/kg IM every 2–4 weeks × 3 doses, OR topical testosterone 2.5% cream applied twice daily to penile shaft for 4–6 weeks, (2) last dose/application 4–6 weeks before surgery to allow any transient growth plate effects to resolve. HCG: (1) 500 IU IM every 5–7 days × 3–4 doses, (2) last dose 3–4 weeks before surgery. Both equivalent in effect. Trap: giving hormones too close to surgery (<4 weeks) may cause tissue oedema and friability increasing complications. Part (c) — 6 marks: (1) Meta-analyses (e.g., Wright et al. 2013, Gorduza et al. 2011) show significant increase in penile length (mean +1.2 cm) and glans diameter (+1.5 mm) after therapy, (2) no consistent reduction in overall complication rate (fistula, stenosis) in randomised trials, (3) some series suggest improved glans dehiscence rates in proximal hypospadias with glans <14 mm, (4) no difference in reoperation rate, (5) heterogeneity in dosing and timing limits pooled analysis, (6) current ESPU guidelines state preoperative androgens may be considered for proximal hypospadias with hypoplastic glans but evidence level is C (expert opinion / small studies). Trap: quoting a proven complication benefit when RCTs have not demonstrated this. Part (d) — 5 marks: (1) temporary penile growth and pubic hair regrowth (reversible within 6 months), (2) transient acceleration of linear growth and bone age advancement, (3) behavioural changes (irritability, aggression), (4) local skin reactions with topical cream (rash, erythema), (5) theoretical risk of premature epiphyseal plate closure — not observed with short courses. Reassure that all effects are reversible. Part (e) — 5 marks: Recommend use in this patient. Justification: (1) glans diameter is only 10 mm — below the 14 mm threshold associated with higher dehiscence rates, (2) narrow urethral plate (6 mm) may benefit from increased vascularity, (3) proximal hypospadias with hypoplasia is the subgroup most likely to benefit, (4) evidence is limited but the risk profile of short-course androgens is well-established as safe, (5) discuss risks with parents and obtain informed consent. Alternative: proceed without hormones but counsel on higher reoperation risk. Distinction candidates cite the need for properly designed multicentre RCT.
essay.modelAnswer: (a) The physiological rationale is that hypospadias represents partial androgen insufficiency during development, leading to a hypoplastic penis with small glans and narrow urethral plate. Androgen stimulation increases penile length, glans circumference, and local vascularity, facilitating surgical reconstruction. A larger glans allows better glans wings approximation with reduced dehiscence risk, while increased penile skin vascularity improves healing. (b) Testosterone: intramuscular testosterone enanthate 2 mg/kg every 2–4 weeks for 3 doses, with the final dose 4–6 weeks before surgery, OR topical testosterone 2.5% cream applied to the penile shaft twice daily for 4–6 weeks. HCG: 500 IU intramuscularly every 5–7 days for 3–4 doses, finishing 3–4 weeks preoperatively. Both are equally effective. Treatment should not be given closer than 4 weeks from surgery to avoid tissue oedema and friability. (c) Meta-analyses (Wright 2013, Gorduza 2011, Kaya 2016) confirm that preoperative androgens significantly increase penile length (mean 1.0–1.5 cm) and glans diameter (1.5–2 mm). However, randomised controlled trials (e.g., the only double-blind placebo-controlled trial by Gorduza 2011) showed no statistically significant reduction in fistula (25% vs 22%), meatal stenosis (8% vs 10%), or reoperation rates. Subgroup analyses suggest improved glans dehiscence in proximal hypospadias with glans diameter <14 mm. The overall complication reduction has not been proven. (d) Potential adverse effects: (1) temporary penile growth and pubic hair that regress after stopping; (2) transient linear growth acceleration with minor bone age advancement (1–3 months); (3) mild behavioural changes (irritability, increased activity) reported in 10–15%; (4) topical cream may cause local skin irritation. All effects are reversible within 3–6 months of cessation. No long-term effect on final adult height or fertility has been documented with short courses. (e) I would recommend preoperative testosterone therapy in this patient. The glans diameter is 10 mm, well below the 14 mm threshold associated with increased dehiscence, and the urethral plate is narrow at 6 mm. The best available evidence, although limited, shows clear anatomical benefit. The risk profile is acceptable with reversible side effects. I would use IM testosterone enanthate 2 mg/kg at weeks –8, –6, and –4 before surgery and explain to the parents that while it may reduce certain complications (especially glans dehiscence), the overall reoperation rate data are not definitive. A well-designed multicentre RCT is needed.
distinguishingFeatures: Clearly states that evidence shows anatomical benefit but no proven reduction in overall complications
Distinguishes appropriate timing (finish 4–6 weeks preoperatively) to avoid tissue friability
Identifies glans diameter <14 mm as the threshold that may benefit most
Recommends therapy in selected proximal cases while acknowledging limited evidence quality

essay.title: Redo Hypospadias with BXO – Two-Stage Bracka Repair
essay.scenario: A 6-year-old boy presents for review. He underwent a TIP urethroplasty at 18 months of age for distal penile hypospadias. The repair broke down within the first year with complete glans dehiscence and the meatus has now retracted to a mid-shaft position. On examination there is a white, sclerotic, firm ring of tissue around the meatus extending proximally along the ventral shaft. The prepuce was used in the initial repair so is not available. There is 20° of ventral curvature on erection. The clinical appearance is consistent with lichen sclerosus (BXO). He has been passing urine with difficulty and a narrow stream.

parts.text: the clinical condition and explain the pathological relationship between BXO (lichen sclerosus) and failed hypospadias repair.
the principles of the Bracka two-stage repair technique, detailing the first stage (excision, buccal mucosal graft harvest and inlay) and the second stage (tubularisation).
the alternative reconstructive options for redo hypospadias with BXO when buccal mucosa is insufficient or unavailable.
a postoperative care plan including catheter management, wound care, and follow-up surveillance for recurrence in a child with BXO undergoing staged reconstruction.

essay.markingGuide: Part (a) — 8 marks: Diagnosis: balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO) / lichen sclerosus. 2 marks for describing the clinical features (white sclerotic ring, firm texture, meatal stenosis, progressive nature). 3 marks for pathological relationship: (1) BXO is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis of unknown aetiology that causes progressive fibrosis of the glans, meatus, and urethra, (2) it may be unmasked or exacerbated by the initial surgery — trauma and inflammation can trigger latent BXO, (3) it causes progressive scarring, neourethral contracture, and repair breakdown. 3 marks for clinical significance: BXO is found in up to 30% of redo hypospadias cases and is the single most important risk factor for recurrent failure if not recognised and treated with complete excision of affected tissue. Trap: treating BXO conservatively without excision leads to 100% recurrence. Part (b) — 10 marks: First stage: (1) complete excision of all BXO-affected skin and urethral plate until healthy vascularised tissue is reached (frozen section may guide), (2) correct chordee with dorsal plication if needed (± urethral plate transection), (3) harvest buccal mucosa graft from lower lip/cheek (2–3 cm × 1–1.5 cm) — defat and fenestrate, (4) graft is quilted and sutured into the ventral defect from neomeatus to glans tip over a stent, (5) bolster dressing for 5 days to immobilise graft. Second stage (≥6 months later): (1) confirm adequate graft take and pliability, (2) U-shaped incision around graft, (3) tubularise graft over 10–12Fr stent with 2-layer closure, (4) dartos flap coverage if local tissue available, (5) glansplasty and meatoplasty. Credit mention of buccal graft as the gold standard in BXO because genital skin is also affected. Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks each: (1) post-auricular Wolfe graft (full-thickness skin graft) — good colour match and less hair-bearing than genital skin but less robust than buccal mucosa, (2) lingual mucosa graft (from lateral tongue) — similar properties to buccal, useful if buccal insufficient, (3) tunica vaginalis flap — from scrotum, provides well-vascularised tissue but may have cosmetic mismatch, (4) Two-stage with preputial skin graft — only if prepuce spared by BXO (rare). Trap: using genital skin in BXO will re-disease and fail. Part (d) — 4 marks: (1) catheter (8–10Fr silicone) for 7–10 days to divert urine, (2) suprapubic catheter for complex cases where transurethral catheter may disrupt graft, (3) antibiotic prophylaxis while catheterised, (4) regular follow-up to monitor for BXO recurrence (annually for 5 years) as late recurrence may occur. BXO tends to recur in the graft bed at a rate of 5–15% over long-term follow-up, requiring lifelong surveillance.
essay.modelAnswer: (a) This patient has balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO), the genital manifestation of lichen sclerosus, superimposed on failed hypospadias repair. BXO is characterised by a white, sclerotic, parchment-like skin with follicular plugging and progressive fibrosis. Pathologically, it is a chronic lymphocytic dermatosis of unknown aetiology that results in epidermal atrophy and dermal sclerosis. In the context of hypospadias, BXO may be triggered or unmasked by surgical trauma. It causes progressive fibrosis of the meatus, neourethra, and glans, leading to the characteristic white ring, meatal stenosis, and eventual repair breakdown. Up to 30% of redo hypospadias repairs show BXO on histology. Complete excision of all affected tissue down to healthy, bleeding tissue is essential because BXO invariably recurs if any affected skin remains; buccal mucosa is the preferred substitute because it is not susceptible to BXO. (b) Bracka two-stage repair — Stage 1: (1) Excise all BXO-affected ventral skin and the diseased urethral plate; extend excision into healthy, bleeding tissue. (2) Perform artificial erection; correct residual chordee with dorsal midline plication (Baskin technique) if >20° persists. (3) Harvest buccal mucosa from the lower lip (avoiding the parotid duct); defat the graft with fine scissors and make small fenestrations to prevent haematoma. (4) Suture the buccal graft into the ventral defect with 6/0 absorbable sutures from the neomeatus proximally to the glans tip distally; quilt the graft to the underlying tunica albuginea with several quilting sutures. (5) Place a silicone stent and a bolster dressing for 5 days to immobilise the graft. Stage 2 (≥6 months later): (1) Assess graft for adequate take — it should be pink, pliable, and well-integrated. (2) Make a U-shaped incision along the borders of the graft. (3) Tubularise the graft over a 10–12Fr stent in two layers using running subepithelial 6/0 absorbable sutures. (4) Cover with a dartos flap if available. (5) Perform glansplasty and meatoplasty. (c) Alternative grafts when buccal mucosa is unavailable: (1) Post-auricular full-thickness skin graft — excellent colour match for penile shaft, low hair-bearing potential, but less durable than buccal. (2) Lingual mucosal graft — similar histological properties to buccal, harvested from lateral tongue, useful when prior buccal harvest has been performed. (3) Tunica vaginalis flap — pedicled flap from parietal tunica vaginalis, can cover long defects, well-vascularised, but may have cosmetic mismatch and has been associated with graft contracture. (4) Colonic or bladder mucosa grafts — rarely used, reserved for pan-urethral stricture disease. The key principle is that genital skin must not be used in BXO because it will undergo identical pathological change. (d) Postoperative care: (1) Transurethral 8–10Fr silicone catheter for 7–10 days until initial graft take is secure. (2) In complex cases, a suprapubic catheter provides optimal urinary diversion without risking graft disruption. (3) Oral or intravenous antibiotics while catheterised. (4) Review at 2 weeks for stent removal, 6 weeks for clinical assessment, then annually. Lifelong surveillance is required because BXO can recur in the graft bed in 5–15% of cases, sometimes years later, and may present as delayed stricture or meatal stenosis. Early recurrence can be managed with topical corticosteroids, but established recurrence requires re-excision and regrafting.
distinguishingFeatures: Recognises BXO as the single most important risk factor for recurrent hypospadias failure
States that complete excision of BXO tissue is mandatory, and genital skin must not be reused
Places buccal mucosa as the gold standard graft with clear justification (not susceptible to BXO)
Emphasises lifelong surveillance for late BXO recurrence in the graft bed

essay.title: Consent and Ethical Issues in Hypospadias Surgery
essay.scenario:
Theme: Consent, ethics, and shared decision-making in childhood hypospadias surgery.

Option list:
A. Gillick competence assessment
B. Parental responsibility and consent
C. Best interests determination
D. Court-ordered (Fraser) ruling
E. MDT discussion including child psychologist
F. Delayed surgery until the child can consent
G. Second surgical opinion
H. Child protection referral
I. Informed consent with specific risks
J. Capacity assessment under Mental Capacity Act

parts.text: the most appropriate next step when a 15-year-old boy with uncorrected distal hypospadias refuses surgery that his parents request.
the most appropriate action when a 14-year-old with previous failed hypospadias repair requests a revision but his parents are reluctant.
the most appropriate approach when divorced parents disagree about circumcision for their 4-year-old son during hypospadias repair.
the most appropriate framework when deciding whether to operate on an infant with proximal hypospadias and a micropenis.
the most appropriate intervention when a 12-year-old with fully corrected hypospadias develops depression and body dysmorphia.
the most appropriate step when parents refuse life-saving adrenal crisis treatment for their child with CAH and hypospadias.
the most appropriate option for a 16-year-old who wants surgery but whose parents disagree, and the surgeon believes the procedure is in the patient's best interest.
the most appropriate approach for consent when the mother of a 6-month-old with hypospadias is a teenager without parental support.
the most appropriate action when an adolescent with corrected hypospadias presents requesting reversal of circumcision for cosmetic reasons.
the most appropriate step when extended family members (grandparents) demand a specific surgical technique for a child over the parents' objection.

essay.markingGuide: Each part 3 marks. Must select the single best option. Part (a) — A (Gillick competence assessment): at 15, the boy is likely Gillick-competent; his refusal should be respected if competence is confirmed. Common error: D (court-ordered) — court would not overrule a competent child's refusal of non-life-saving surgery. Part (b) — A (Gillick competence assessment): the 14-year-old's wishes should be independently assessed; his desire for surgery should be considered. Trap: assuming parents' reluctance overrides the adolescent's wishes. Part (c) — B (parental responsibility): if both parents have parental responsibility, they must agree; if not, MDT discussion may help, but ultimately court may decide. Part (d) — C (best interests determination): MDT assessment of best interests considering clinical need, parental views, and the child's future autonomy. Part (e) — E (MDT discussion including child psychologist): psychological support and MDT review for body dysmorphia after apparently successful surgery. Part (f) — H (child protection referral): refusal of life-saving treatment is a child protection concern; emergency social services involvement. Trap: B (parental responsibility) does not extend to refusing life-saving care. Part (g) — J (assessment under Mental Capacity Act) / A (Gillick): at 16, presumed competent under MCA; if competent, his consent is sufficient. Part (h) — B (parental responsibility): the teenage mother has parental responsibility unless a court orders otherwise; no legal requirement for grandparent consent. Part (i) — E (MDT discussion/psychology): request for reversal requires psychological evaluation to understand motivations and expectations. Part (j) — G (second surgical opinion): extended family have no legal standing; offer a second opinion to address concerns but parents' decision is final.
essay.modelAnswer: (a) A — Gillick competence assessment. At 15, the boy is likely competent to make this decision. His refusal of elective surgery should be respected. The medical team should document his competence assessment and counsel the family. (b) A — Gillick competence assessment. The 14-year-old's wishes must be independently assessed. He may be Gillick-competent to request revision surgery for functional or cosmetic reasons. (c) B — Parental responsibility and consent. If parents disagree and both have parental responsibility, attempt mediation. If no agreement, the court may need to decide. The surgeon should not proceed without either both parties' consent or a court order. (d) C — Best interests determination. For an infant with proximal hypospadias and micropenis, the MDT (paediatric urologist, endocrinologist, psychologist, clinical ethics committee) should determine the best interests, incorporating clinical necessity, parental views, and the child's future autonomy. (e) E — MDT discussion including child psychologist. Depression and body dysmorphia after technically successful surgery require psychological assessment and support. The MDT should explore his concerns, offer cognitive behavioural therapy, and only consider revision after psychological clearance. (f) H — Child protection referral. Refusal of life-saving treatment for adrenal crisis constitutes neglect. Emergency social services and child protection procedures must be initiated. (g) J — Capacity assessment under Mental Capacity Act. At 16, the patient is presumed to have capacity. If capacity is confirmed, his consent alone is legally sufficient, even if parents disagree. (h) B — Parental responsibility. The teenage mother retains parental responsibility. Her consent alone is sufficient. No other family member has automatic legal standing unless they hold a parental responsibility order. (i) E — MDT discussion with psychologist. Reversal of circumcision is an unusual request. Psychological evaluation is essential to understand underlying motivations, body image concerns, and to set realistic expectations about surgical outcomes. (j) G — Second surgical opinion. Extended family have no legal authority over the child's medical care. Offer a second surgical opinion to address their concerns, but the parents' decision regarding technique is final.
distinguishingFeatures: Differentiates Gillick competence from Fraser guidelines and Mental Capacity Act for 16-year-olds
Recognises that parental refusal of life-saving care triggers child protection, not deferral to parental rights
Distinguishes adolescent consent from parental consent in elective genital surgery
Emphasises MDT and psychological input for body image concerns after hypospadias repair

mcqs.stem: At what gestational week does the prepuce first appear as an epithelial ridge?
mcqs.explanation: The prepuce first appears as an epithelial ridge at 8 weeks' gestation and then advances over the developing glans. By 16 weeks it is fully formed, but there is no plane of separation at the epithelial interface at that stage.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to clinic because his foreskin cannot be retracted. His parents are concerned this is abnormal. Which of the following best describes the natural history of the foreskin at this age?

options.text: Forceful retraction should be performed daily to prevent adhesions
The prepuce should be fully retractable by 12 months of age
The prepuce is almost invariably non-retractable at birth and remains so for a variable period thereafter
Non-retractility at 2 years is diagnostic of pathological phimosis

mcqs.explanation: The prepuce is almost invariably non-retractable at birth and remains so for a variable period thereafter. Attempted retraction during this period creates the appearance of a blanched, tight ring. This is physiological, not pathological.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following statements best describes the landmark studies on the natural history of the non-retractile foreskin?

options.text: Gairdner (1949) showed that most boys require circumcision by age 5
Øster (1968) found that preputial adhesions persist indefinitely in 50% of men
Both studies recommended routine neonatal circumcision to prevent complications
Gairdner (1949) and Øster (1968) demonstrated that without intervention the prepuce spontaneously becomes retractable in the overwhelming majority of boys by late childhood

mcqs.explanation: Gairdner (1949) and Øster (1968) convincingly demonstrated that without intervention the prepuce spontaneously progresses to become fully and easily retractable by late childhood in the overwhelming majority of boys.

mcqs.stem: A 3-year-old boy is noted to have ballooning of the foreskin during micturition. The parents are worried about urinary obstruction. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Arrange urgent uroflowmetry and post-void bladder ultrasound
Prescribe a course of topical betamethasone cream
Reassure the parents that this is a transient self-limiting phenomenon that resolves spontaneously

mcqs.explanation: Ballooning typically occurs between 2 and 4 years of age when the foreskin is not yet fully retractile but substantial separation from the glans has taken place. It is transient, self-limiting, and does not require any intervention. Studies have confirmed that urine flow rates, post-void residual volumes, and bladder-wall thickness are unaffected.

mcqs.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with a white, scarred, non-retractile foreskin and a poor urinary stream. On examination the preputial orifice is stenotic with a pale, sclerotic appearance. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: The combination of a scarred, pale, stenotic preputial orifice with a deteriorating urinary stream in a school-age child is classic for BXO (lichen sclerosus et atrophicus). BXO typically presents after age 5 with a peak incidence between 9 and 11 years and is the cause of true pathological phimosis.

mcqs.stem: What percentage of boys are estimated to develop balanitis xerotica obliterans before the age of 16?
mcqs.explanation: It is estimated that approximately 0.5% of boys will develop BXO before the age of 16. It is rare under the age of 5, with a peak incidence between 9 and 11 years.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following histological features is characteristic of balanitis xerotica obliterans?

options.text: Neutrophilic microabscesses in the stratum corneum
Hyperkeratosis, lymphoedema, hyalinosis and chronic inflammatory infiltrate

mcqs.explanation: The histological features of BXO are the same as lichen sclerosus et atrophicus of the vulva and include hyperkeratosis, lymphoedema, hyalinosis and chronic inflammatory infiltrate.

mcqs.stem: What is the preferred treatment for balanitis xerotica obliterans in childhood?
mcqs.explanation: The preferred treatment for BXO is circumcision. Pathological phimosis due to BXO constitutes the only absolute indication for circumcision in childhood. Preputioplasty alone is not worthwhile because the ongoing inflammatory process leads to recurrent stenosis of the preputial opening.

mcqs.stem: In approximately what percentage of boys undergoing circumcision for BXO is concomitant meatal stenosis severe enough to require meatotomy or meatoplasty?
mcqs.explanation: In approximately 5% of cases of BXO, meatal stenosis necessitates meatotomy or meatoplasty at the time of circumcision. The risk of recurrent meatal stenosis can be significantly reduced by postoperative application of topical steroid creams.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following statements about the aetiology of balanitis xerotica obliterans is correct?

options.text: There is a strong familial predisposition
Recurrent episodes of balanoposthitis predispose to BXO
BXO is caused by human papillomavirus
The aetiology is unknown with no familial predisposition or identifiable infective basis

mcqs.explanation: The aetiology of BXO is unknown with no familial predisposition or identifiable infective basis. Recurrent episodes of balanoposthitis do not predispose to BXO.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old uncircumcised boy presents with acute onset of a purulent discharge from the prepuce, with overlying oedema and erythema extending onto the penile shaft. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Acute balanoposthitis in its most severe form consists of purulent, pyogenic infection of the entire preputial space with purulent discharge, overlying oedema and cellulitis of the prepuce, which may occasionally extend to involve the entire penile shaft.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most common causative organism in acute balanoposthitis?
mcqs.explanation: The most common causative organisms are Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli and Proteus spp. The preputial discharge is sterile on culture in a third of cases.

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old boy has had three episodes of balanoposthitis in the past year. What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Therapeutic intervention including consideration of topical steroid creams, preputiolysis or preputioplasty as alternatives to circumcision

mcqs.explanation: Therapeutic intervention should be limited to boys who have suffered from recurrent episodes. Where appropriate, topical steroid creams, preputiolysis or preputioplasty should be considered as alternatives to circumcision.

mcqs.stem: In a mild case of posthitis (inflammation limited to the preputial orifice), which of the following clinical features would typically be present?

options.text: Necrosis of the foreskin with black eschar
Localised erythema, oedema and discomfort without purulent discharge

mcqs.explanation: In less severe forms, more correctly termed posthitis, the inflammatory process is limited to the preputial orifice and outer lining of the foreskin. Clinical features comprise localised erythema, oedema and discomfort but do not include purulent discharge.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with acute onset of a swollen, oedematous prepuce that cannot be returned to its normal position over the glans after retraction. His mother states he was asked to start retracting his foreskin as treatment for a 'tight foreskin'. What is the diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Paraphimosis results from manipulation of the foreskin and a failure to draw the prepuce forward again following retraction. It can be an unforeseen consequence of instructing the boy to retract his foreskin as a treatment for physiological phimosis.

mcqs.stem: What is the first-line management of a 9-year-old boy presenting with paraphimosis?

options.text: Emergency dorsal slit under general anaesthesia
Observation only, as it will resolve spontaneously within 24 hours
Reduction of preputial oedema by manual compression, multiple needle puncture or topical cooling before drawing the foreskin forwards over the glans

mcqs.explanation: Treatment consists of reducing the preputial oedema by manual compression, multiple needle puncture or topical cooling before drawing the foreskin forwards over the glans. It should not usually be necessary to resort to urgent surgical intervention such as a dorsal slit.

mcqs.stem: After successful reduction of a first episode of paraphimosis in a 6-year-old boy, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start a course of topical steroid cream
Reassure that a single episode is not an indication for surgery and advise on proper foreskin care

mcqs.explanation: The occurrence of paraphimosis does not signify underlying pathology of the foreskin and a single episode is not an indication for surgery. Recurrent episodes are relatively unusual, but circumcision is a reasonable option in such cases.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with a firm, mobile, yellowish subcutaneous lump at the coronal level. His general practitioner suspected a dermoid cyst. On examination the lump is superficial and appears to be associated with the prepuce. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Trapped smegma collections can form yellowish subcutaneous lumps that are often misdiagnosed by general practitioners as sebaceous cysts or lipomas of the penile shaft. Reassurance is all that is required, as the trapped smegma will be discharged when surrounding adhesions separate spontaneously.

mcqs.stem: What is the recommended management for preputial adhesions in a young boy?

options.text: Surgical division of adhesions under general anaesthesia
Forceful retraction of the foreskin at every bath
Reassurance, as they usually resolve spontaneously during childhood

mcqs.explanation: Preputial adhesions usually resolve spontaneously during the course of childhood. There is only a very limited role for surgical division because disruption of the epithelial surface of the glans tends to promote the development of recurrent adhesions.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is true regarding the condition 'congenital megaprepuce'?

options.text: It typically presents with a good urinary stream
The natural history is well documented and spontaneous improvement is the rule
On cursory inspection it resembles a buried penis with an enormously capacious preputial sac
Surgical correction is straightforward and rarely requires revision

mcqs.explanation: Congenital megaprepuce resembles a buried penis on cursory inspection, but closer examination reveals an enormously capacious preputial sac engulfing the whole penile shaft and upper scrotum. The natural history is poorly documented because surgery is usually performed when the condition first presents.

mcqs.stem: In congenital megaprepuce, which of the following best describes the voiding pattern?

options.text: A normal urinary stream with complete bladder emptying
Forceful projection of urine with high pressure
Urine dribbles more or less continuously from the preputial orifice and there is never a normal urinary stream

mcqs.explanation: In congenital megaprepuce, urine dribbles more or less continuously from the preputial orifice and although the capacious urine-filled sac can be readily emptied by compression there is never a normal urinary stream.

mcqs.stem: A newborn presents for ritual circumcision. On examination the glans has a cleft-like abnormality. The prepuce appears entirely normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Megameatus intact prepuce is a rare congenital abnormality of the glans rather than the prepuce (the prepuce appears entirely normal). It is often recognised for the first time during the course of a medical circumcision and can be mistaken for an iatrogenic injury.

mcqs.stem: Why may megameatus intact prepuce first be recognised shortly after a ritual circumcision?

options.text: Circumcision causes the meatus to dilate
The characteristic cleft-like abnormality of the glans becomes visible after the prepuce is removed, and may be mistakenly attributed to an iatrogenic injury
Infection develops at the meatus after circumcision
The Plastibell device causes meatal trauma

mcqs.explanation: On some occasions the abnormality is first recognised shortly after a ritual circumcision, prompting parents and doctors to mistakenly attribute the characteristic cleft-like abnormality of the glans to an iatrogenic circumcision injury. Even experienced paediatric urologists have made this error.

mcqs.stem: In the United Kingdom, non-medical circumcision is now largely confined to which groups?

options.text: Muslim and Jewish religious communities and some West African Christian communities
Only those with a family history of penile cancer

mcqs.explanation: In the UK, ritual circumcision is largely confined to the Muslim and Jewish religions, but it is also practised within predominantly Christian West African communities. It is generally not funded within NHS hospitals, though some cities with large Muslim populations provide circumcision services under local NHS arrangements.

mcqs.stem: The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) Circumcision Policy Statement endorses newborn circumcision. Which of the following best describes the European perspective on this guideline?

options.text: It is fully endorsed by all European paediatric societies
European guidelines recommend routine circumcision for all male newborns
It is considered ambiguous, based on flawed analysis of the evidence and partly influenced by financial rather than scientific considerations
The European perspective agrees but recommends circumcision only in the first 24 hours of life

mcqs.explanation: From a European perspective, the AAP guidelines appear to be ambiguous, based on a flawed analysis of the evidence and partly influenced by financial rather than scientific considerations. The AAP statement also contrasts strongly with published guidelines of the UK, the Netherlands, Denmark and Canada, which conclude that the weight of evidence does not support routine neonatal circumcision.

mcqs.stem: Approximately how many normal male infants would need to be circumcised to prevent one urinary tract infection in the first year of life?
mcqs.explanation: Calculations based on systematic reviews indicate that around 110–125 normal male infants would need to be circumcised to prevent one UTI. Neonatal circumcision cannot be regarded as a rational routine prophylactic measure on this basis.

mcqs.stem: In which group of patients is circumcision considered a worthwhile intervention for reducing the risk of urinary tract infection?

options.text: Male infants with predisposing urinary pathology such as vesicoureteric reflux or posterior urethral valves
All healthy male infants under 6 months
Boys with a family history of recurrent UTIs

mcqs.explanation: There is persuasive evidence that circumcision can be a worthwhile intervention in reducing the risk of UTI in male infants with predisposing urinary pathology, such as vesicoureteric reflux or urinary tract dilatation associated with posterior urethral valves.

mcqs.stem: Randomised controlled trials in sub-Saharan Africa have demonstrated that circumcision in men and adolescent males confers partial protection against which infection?
mcqs.explanation: Randomised controlled trials in sub-Saharan Africa have demonstrated that circumcision in men and adolescent males confers partial protection against heterosexually acquired HIV infection. However, this is far less relevant to routine circumcision of newborn male infants in countries with a much lower prevalence of HIV.

mcqs.stem: Penile cancer is virtually confined to uncircumcised men. Which of the following statements best reflects the recommended approach to prevention?

options.text: Routine neonatal circumcision for all boys
Health education and treatment of phimosis are more appropriate preventative measures than widescale circumcision of healthy newborns
Screening all uncircumcised men annually with biopsy

mcqs.explanation: Penile cancer is a rare malignancy in developed countries. Health education and treatment of phimosis are much more appropriate preventative measures than widescale circumcision of healthy newborn infants.

mcqs.stem: What is the estimated percentage of boys aged 0–16 years who undergo circumcision for medical reasons in the UK?
mcqs.explanation: Current estimates indicate that around 3–5% of boys aged 0–16 years undergo circumcision for medical reasons in the UK. The principal indications being 'phimosis' (BXO) and balanoposthitis.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to newborn circumcision?
mcqs.explanation: Hypospadias, epispadias or ambiguous genitalia are absolute contraindications to newborn circumcision because removal of the prepuce is highly likely to compromise the outcome of any later genital reconstruction.

mcqs.stem: A surgeon notices that after routine circumcision of a boy with a 'buried penis', there is insufficient skin to cover the penile shaft. What technical point has been violated?

options.text: The Plastibell device was too small
Too much preputial skin was removed in a boy with buried penis; a modified technique to redistribute rather than remove preputial skin is required
The inner preputial layer was not fully separated
Bipolar diathermy was used instead of ligation

mcqs.explanation: The risk of removing too much skin is greatest in boys with so-called 'buried penis' in whom even a standard circumcision may result in a denuded penile shaft and a requirement for skin grafting. A modified technique is required to redistribute rather than remove preputial skin.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is true regarding the Plastibell technique for ritual circumcision?

options.text: The Plastibell ring is sutured in place permanently
The Plastibell technique requires general anaesthesia in all cases
A haemostatic ligature is tied around the base of the prepuce overlying a groove in the Plastibell device, and the plastic ring detaches spontaneously after 3–7 days
Complications specific to this technique include urethral fistula formation

mcqs.explanation: In the Plastibell technique, a haemostatic ligature is tied around the base of the prepuce overlying a groove in the Plastibell device. The plastic ring remains in place before detaching spontaneously after an interval of 3–7 days. Complications include haemorrhage due to slippage of the ligature and delayed separation of the ring.

mcqs.stem: What is the reported incidence of haemorrhage of sufficient severity to necessitate early re-operation following circumcision?
mcqs.explanation: Haemorrhage of sufficient severity to necessitate early re-operation has been reported to occur in 2–5% of boys following circumcision. Early complications also include urinary retention, which can be prevented by adequate analgesia or intraoperative local block.

mcqs.stem: Which serious but rare complication of circumcision has been linked to dorsal penile nerve block used for postoperative pain relief?
mcqs.explanation: Glans ischaemia is a serious but fortunately rare complication that may be related to the dorsal penile nerve block commonly used for postoperative pain relief. It can resolve with treatment including oral sildenafil, subcutaneous enoxaparin and caudal anaesthesia.

mcqs.stem: A 3-month-old infant presents with glans ischaemia shortly after circumcision. Colour Doppler ultrasound of the penile vasculature shows normal appearances. Which of the following treatments has been described for this complication?

options.text: Immediate surgical exploration and microvascular repair
Oral sildenafil, subcutaneous enoxaparin and caudal anaesthesia with levobupivacaine

mcqs.explanation: This complication has been reported to resolve completely within a few days following treatment with oral sildenafil, subcutaneous enoxaparin and caudal anaesthesia with levobupivacaine (for its vasodilatory effect), with no long-term implications.

mcqs.stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a swollen, bruised penis and a purpuric rash on his legs and buttocks. The preputial swelling was initially thought to be balanoposthitis. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Schönlein–Henoch purpura involving the penis can cause preputial and penile swelling that may be confused with balanoposthitis, particularly if these appear before the characteristic purpuric rash on the legs, lower abdomen and buttocks.

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old boy has a persistently non-retractile foreskin. His parents are anxious and request intervention. There is no scarring, no history of balanoposthitis, and the boy is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Prescribe a 4-week course of topical antibiotic cream
Reassure the parents about the natural history of the non-retractile foreskin and advise against intervention

mcqs.explanation: Conservative treatment of the non-retractable foreskin involves advising parents of the natural history of the foreskin and its normal tendency to become retractable when preputial adhesions resolve spontaneously. Alternatives are not appropriate simply because of parental concerns about non-retractility.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following topical steroid preparations has been used to treat a non-retractile foreskin?
mcqs.explanation: Among the preparations used for treating a non-retractile foreskin are 0.05% betamethasone cream, 0.05% clobetasol propionate cream and 0.02% triamcinolone acetonide cream. Typical regimens comprise twice daily application for 4–8 weeks.

mcqs.stem: Reported success rates for topical steroid treatment of a non-retractile foreskin range between 70–95%. Which of the following best describes the interpretation of these figures?

options.text: Success is consistently defined as full retractability in all studies
'Success' is often loosely and subjectively defined, with some studies including partial response or retractability deemed 'appropriate for age'
The figures are based on large randomised placebo-controlled trials
Success rates are significantly lower in boys over 10 years of age

mcqs.explanation: Although 'success rates' of 70–95% have been reported, 'success' is often loosely and subjectively defined – with some authors extending the definition to include partial response or retractability deemed 'appropriate for age'. Application of the steroid cream must be combined with regular attempts at gentle retraction.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following boys is the most suitable candidate for preputioplasty?

options.text: A 3-year-old with physiological phimosis and ballooning
A 14-year-old with a persistently non-retractile foreskin who is motivated to perform regular postoperative self-retraction

mcqs.explanation: Preputioplasty is particularly suited to older boys with a persistently non-retractile foreskin predicted to cause problems after the onset of sexual activity. Regular postoperative self-retraction is essential for a satisfactory outcome, so the procedure is less suitable for younger boys and those unwilling to comply.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is true regarding preputioplasty?

options.text: It results in a circumcised appearance
The procedure consists of incising the constriction ring and closing the incision transversely with interrupted sutures
It is the treatment of choice for BXO
It requires no postoperative care or follow-up

mcqs.explanation: Preputioplasty involves incising the 'constriction ring' apparent on retracting the foreskin and closing this incision transversely with interrupted sutures. Initially the appearance resembles a limited dorsal slit but with time and regular retraction this evolves to create the appearance of a normal foreskin.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is NOT an appropriate alternative to circumcision for a non-retractile foreskin?

options.text: Dorsal slit alone with no reconstruction

mcqs.explanation: Effective alternatives to circumcision for the non-retractable foreskin include topical steroid therapy, preputioplasty and preputiolysis. A dorsal slit is an emergency procedure for paraphimosis, not an elective alternative. Alternatives are also not appropriate for boys with BXO, in whom circumcision is required.

mcqs.stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with secondary diurnal enuresis. He also has a poor urinary stream and a history of dysuria. On examination the foreskin is scarred and non-retractile with a pale, stenotic orifice. Ultrasound shows thickening of the bladder wall. What is the most likely underlying cause of his enuresis?

options.text: Chronic outflow obstruction due to BXO causing functional bladder changes

mcqs.explanation: Advanced cases of BXO can sometimes present with acute urinary retention or secondary diurnal or nocturnal enuresis resulting from chronic outflow obstruction. Ultrasonography may reveal thickening of the bladder wall and/or significant residual urine, which may very occasionally be accompanied by upper tract dilatation.

mcqs.stem: A mother has been told her son needs surgical division of preputial adhesions. What is the most appropriate advice regarding this proposed procedure?

options.text: Surgical division is the gold standard treatment
It must be performed before the age of 2 to be effective
There is only a very limited role for this because disruption of the epithelial surface of the glans tends to promote recurrent adhesions
It should be combined with a meatotomy

mcqs.explanation: There is only a very limited role for surgical division of preputial adhesions because disruption of the epithelial surface of the glans tends to promote the development of recurrent adhesions. Topical steroid cream, circumcision or preputioplasty (in older boys) are preferable options.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following statements about Schönlein–Henoch purpura involving the penis is correct?

options.text: It is always accompanied by haemarthrosis
Circumcision is required to prevent recurrence
Preputial swelling may appear before the characteristic purpuric rash and can be confused with balanoposthitis

mcqs.explanation: The preputial and penile swelling and oedema which may be features of Schönlein–Henoch purpura can be confused with balanoposthitis, particularly if these appear before the characteristic purpuric rash on the legs, lower abdomen and buttocks.

mcqs.stem: What is the purpose of combining preputioplasty with intralesional injection of a potent steroid in the management of BXO?

options.text: It is the standard first-line treatment for all cases of BXO
It has been claimed to be curative in milder cases of BXO
It replaces the need for circumcision in advanced BXO
It prevents recurrence of meatal stenosis

mcqs.explanation: Preputioplasty alone is not a worthwhile option for BXO because the ongoing inflammatory process leads to recurrent stenosis. However, the combination of preputioplasty and intralesional injection of a potent steroid has been claimed to be curative in milder cases.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old uncircumcised boy is concerned about the appearance of small yellowish lumps under the skin of his penile shaft at the coronal level. He is otherwise asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Arrange urgent ultrasound of the penis
Reassure him that these are smegma retention collections that will resolve when preputial adhesions separate spontaneously

mcqs.explanation: Smegma retention 'cysts' are mobile, subcutaneous, firm yellowish lumps at coronal level often misdiagnosed as dermoid cysts or lipomas. Reassurance is all that is required. The trapped smegma will be discharged when surrounding adhesions separate spontaneously.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following statements best summarises the key management principles for the non-retractile foreskin?

options.text: All boys with non-retractile foreskin should be circumcised by age 5
Topical steroids are contraindicated in children

Conservative treatment involves advising parents of the natural history; effective alternatives include topical steroid therapy and preputioplasty; surgical intervention is justifiable for recurrent balanoposthitis
Preputioplasty is the preferred treatment for all cases

mcqs.explanation: The key points are: (1) conservative treatment involves advising parents of the natural history; (2) effective alternatives include topical steroid therapy and preputioplasty; (3) surgical intervention is justifiable for recurrent balanoposthitis; (4) BXO is the only absolute indication for circumcision but is rare under age 5.

mcqs.stem: An adolescent boy undergoes circumcision for BXO. Postoperatively, what can be used to significantly reduce the risk of recurrent meatal stenosis?

options.text: Postoperative application of topical steroid creams

mcqs.explanation: The risk of recurrent meatal stenosis can be significantly reduced by postoperative application of topical steroid creams. In approximately 5% of cases, meatal stenosis necessitates meatotomy or meatoplasty at the time of circumcision.

mcqs.stem: A term newborn is noted to have an empty scrotum on routine examination. Which of the following best describes the incidence of congenital undescended testis in term infants?
mcqs.explanation: The incidence of undescended testis in term infants is 1.5–3%. In premature infants it is up to 30%. Spontaneous descent can occur during the first few months due to mini-puberty.

mcqs.stem: A 4-month-old infant with a known undescended testis is brought for follow-up. The testis previously noted to be absent from the scrotum is now palpable at the scrotal base. This is most likely due to which physiological phenomenon?

options.text: Activation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis in early infancy

mcqs.explanation: Spontaneous descent occurs in response to 'mini-puberty' — high levels of circulating testosterone driven by pituitary gonadotrophins in the early postnatal period.

mcqs.stem: A paediatric urologist is counselling the parents of a 6-month-old boy with bilateral undescended testes. By what age would spontaneous descent no longer be expected?
mcqs.explanation: Spontaneous descent does not occur after 6 months of age. Beyond this point a 'wait and watch' strategy is not justified, and orchidopexy is recommended.
mcqs.topic: Undescended testis — timing of spontaneous descent

mcqs.stem: At what age does gonocyte transformation into adult dark (Ad) spermatogonia normally occur in the human testis?

options.text: In utero during the second trimester

mcqs.explanation: Gonocytes transform into Ad spermatogonia between 3 and 9 months of age in response to the surge of gonadotrophins and testosterone during mini-puberty.

mcqs.stem: In undescended testes, impaired gonocyte transformation leads to which of the following consequences?

options.text: A diminished pool of adult stem cells for spermatogenesis and persistence of neonatal gonocytes

mcqs.explanation: In UDT, gonocyte transformation into Ad spermatogonia is impaired, reducing the stem cell pool for spermatogenesis. Redundant gonocytes that would normally undergo apoptosis persist, increasing malignancy risk.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred for a possible undescended testis. On examination the testis can be brought fully to the floor of the scrotum without tension and remains there. What is the most appropriate diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: A retractile testis is defined as one that, regardless of its initial position, can be brought fully to the floor of the scrotum without tension. This is a normal variant common between 3 and 7 years.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate management for a boy with a genuinely retractile testis?

options.text: Annual review until the testis is no longer retractile and has reverted to a normal scrotal position

mcqs.explanation: Retractile testes should be followed annually as some progress to become ascending testes. If the testis remains retractile or reverts to a normal position, no surgery is needed.

mcqs.stem: A 10-year-old boy who had a normally descended testis in infancy now has a testis fixed in the upper scrotum that cannot be manipulated to the scrotal base. This is most consistent with which condition?
mcqs.explanation: An ascending (acquired) UDT is one that was previously scrotal but has adopted a fixed elevated position. It occurs due to failure of spermatic cord elongation or tethering by a patent processus vaginalis remnant.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most widely accepted explanation for the phenomenon of ascending testis?

options.text: Failure of the spermatic cord to elongate proportionally with growth, or tethering by a processus vaginalis remnant

mcqs.explanation: The distance from the internal ring to the scrotum doubles from infancy to puberty. Failure of this elongation or tethering by a patent processus vaginalis remnant causes secondary ascent.

mcqs.stem: What proportion of boys with ascending testes are reported to have passed through a retractile phase?
mcqs.explanation: In 10–30% of cases, the ascending testis has passed through a retractile phase. This highlights the need for follow-up of boys with retractile testes.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old boy is found to have a palpable undescended testis in the inguinal canal. What is the currently recommended age range for elective orchidopexy?
mcqs.explanation: Current guidelines recommend orchidopexy between 6 and 12 months of age. Earlier surgery may improve germ cell transformation, testicular growth, and reduce malignancy risk.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most common reason that an intra-abdominal testis cannot be brought to the scrotum during orchidopexy?

options.text: Inadequate length of the testicular blood vessels
Adhesions from a patent processus vaginalis

mcqs.explanation: The difficulty in bringing an intra-abdominal testis to the scrotum usually arises from inadequate length of the testicular blood vessels, not the vas deferens.

mcqs.stem: During a Fowler–Stephens orchidopexy for an intra-abdominal testis, after ligation of the testicular vessels, the blood supply to the testis is maintained by which of the following?

options.text: The artery to the vas deferens

mcqs.explanation: During the Fowler–Stephens procedure, following ligation of the testicular vessels, the blood supply is provided by collateral vessels surrounding the vas deferens (the artery to the vas).

mcqs.stem: What is the recommended interval between the first and second stages of a staged Fowler–Stephens orchidopexy?
mcqs.explanation: The second stage is undertaken approximately 6 months after the first, by which time a more substantial collateral blood supply has developed in response to occlusion of the testicular vessels.

mcqs.stem: In microvascular orchidopexy, the testicular vessels are anastomosed to which recipient vessels?
mcqs.explanation: In microvascular orchidopexy, the testicular vessels are anastomosed to the inferior epigastric vessels in the inguinal canal. This is an option for intra-abdominal testes, especially when the vas is too short for a Fowler–Stephens procedure.

mcqs.stem: A 9-month-old boy undergoes inguinal orchidopexy for a palpable undescended testis. The surgeon isolates and divides the patent processus vaginalis from the cord structures. What is the single most important step in this procedure?

options.text: Complete mobilisation of the cord structures back to the internal inguinal ring
Fixation of the testis to the scrotal septum

mcqs.explanation: The crucial step in standard inguinal orchidopexy is isolating the processus vaginalis from the cord structures to allow complete mobilisation back to the internal ring, which provides adequate length.

mcqs.stem: What is the reported success rate of single-stage open inguinal orchidopexy for a palpable undescended testis?
mcqs.explanation: Published outcome data indicate that single-stage open inguinal orchidopexy has a success rate in excess of 95% for palpable undescended testes.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most frequently cited figure for the risk of testicular atrophy following orchidopexy before 2 years of age?
mcqs.explanation: Comparative studies have found the atrophy rate to be approximately 5% in boys undergoing orchidopexy under 2 years of age. Some series have reported rates as low as 1.4%.

mcqs.stem: A 6-month-old boy is found to have bilateral impalpable testes and proximal hypospadias. Which of the following should be considered most strongly?

options.text: An underlying disorder of sex development (DSD)

mcqs.explanation: The combination of bilateral impalpable testes and hypospadias should always raise the possibility of an underlying DSD, notably 46XX DSD due to congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

mcqs.stem: What proportion of undescended testes are impalpable on clinical examination?
mcqs.explanation: Approximately 10–15% of undescended testes are impalpable. An impalpable testis may be intra-abdominal, atrophic following intrauterine torsion, or concealed within the inguinal canal.

mcqs.stem: A 14-month-old boy with an impalpable left testis is scheduled for surgery. Which investigation is considered the first choice for evaluating an impalpable testis?

options.text: Ultrasound of the abdomen and inguinal region

mcqs.explanation: Laparoscopy is the investigation of first choice for impalpable testes. It is highly reliable and provides positive guidance for further management. Ultrasound and MRI are unreliable in this setting.

mcqs.stem: At laparoscopy for an impalpable testis, the vas and vessels are seen to end blindly together at or above the internal ring. This finding is most likely the result of which event?

options.text: Failure of migration of the gubernaculum
Congenital absence of the Wolffian duct

mcqs.explanation: A 'vanished testis' with blind-ending vas and vessels at or above the internal ring is usually thought to result from intrauterine torsion. No further exploration is required in this situation.

mcqs.stem: During laparoscopy for an impalpable testis, a testis is seen lying adjacent to the internal inguinal ring. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform a single-stage orchidopexy via conventional or preperitoneal approach

mcqs.explanation: Testes adjacent to the internal ring are usually amenable to a single-stage orchidopexy using a conventional or preperitoneal approach.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a painless groin swelling that becomes larger by the evening and smaller in the morning. Transillumination is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: A communicating hydrocoele typically presents as a painless scrotal swelling that varies in size throughout the day — small in the morning and larger later in the day as fluid accumulates via the patent processus vaginalis.

mcqs.stem: What is the underlying aetiology common to both indirect inguinal hernias and communicating hydrocoeles in children?

options.text: Weakness of the posterior wall of the inguinal canal
Failure of closure of the processus vaginalis

mcqs.explanation: Both conditions share a common aetiology: failure of closure of the patent processus vaginalis following descent of the testis. The two differ mainly in the diameter of the processus.

mcqs.stem: A newborn male is noted to have a left communicating hydrocoele. What percentage of such hydrocoeles in infants would be expected to resolve spontaneously without intervention?
mcqs.explanation: More than 90% of communicating hydrocoeles in infants resolve spontaneously following spontaneous closure of the patent processus vaginalis.

mcqs.stem: Surgical intervention is typically indicated for a congenital communicating hydrocoele that persists beyond what age?
mcqs.explanation: Surgical intervention is indicated for congenital hydrocoeles that persist beyond 2 years of age, and for hydrocoeles presenting for the first time in later childhood.

mcqs.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with an acute painful groin swelling. Ultrasound reveals a cystic structure within the spermatic cord separate from the testis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: An encysted hydrocoele of the cord presents as a cystic swelling within the spermatic cord, separate from the testis. It can mimic an irreducible hernia and ultrasound is helpful in diagnosis.
mcqs.topic: Hydrocoele — encysted hydrocoele of the cord

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with a 1-year history of a gradually enlarging right scrotal swelling that is non-tender and does not change in size throughout the day. Transillumination is strongly positive. Ultrasound shows fluid surrounding the testis. Which type of hydrocoele is most likely?
mcqs.explanation: Non-communicating hydrocoeles are rare in boys and occur mainly around or after puberty. They present as a gradually enlarging, non-tender scrotal swelling that does not vary in size, unlike communicating hydrocoeles.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most common approximate incidence of varicocoele in adolescent males aged 15–19 years?
mcqs.explanation: Population-based studies show a prevalence of 14.1% in boys aged 15–19 years. The prevalence increases with age: 0.8% at 2–6 years, 1% at 7–10 years, and 7.8% at 11–14 years.

mcqs.stem: Why are more than 90% of varicocoeles left-sided?

options.text: The left testicular vein is longer and drains into the renal vein at a right angle
The left testicular artery compresses the left testicular vein
The left spermatic cord is anatomically shorter
The left cremasteric reflex is more active

mcqs.explanation: The left testicular vein drains into the left renal vein, whereas the right testicular vein drains directly into the vena cava. The longer, right-angled drainage of the left vein creates higher hydrostatic pressure, predisposing to valvular incompetence.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old boy is found on examination to have a left varicocoele that is palpable only during a Valsalva manoeuvre and is not visible at rest. How would this be classified according to the Dubin and Amelar system?
mcqs.explanation: Grade I varicocoele is palpable only on Valsalva manoeuvre. Grade II is palpable at rest but not visible. Grade III is visible and palpable at rest.

mcqs.stem: An adolescent male presents with a left-sided scrotal swelling described as feeling like a 'bag of worms'. This is characteristic of which condition?
mcqs.explanation: A varicocoele is classically described as a 'bag of worms' feeling within the scrotum, representing the tortuous, dilated veins of the pampiniform plexus.

mcqs.stem: When examining a patient for a varicocoele, the patient should be examined in which of the following positions?

options.text: Both supine and standing, with a Valsalva manoeuvre

mcqs.explanation: The patient should be examined both lying and, more importantly, standing. The patient should also be asked to perform a Valsalva manoeuvre during examination.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old boy with a left grade III varicocoele is found to have a >20% difference in testicular volumes. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Semen analysis and consider surgical intervention

mcqs.explanation: The current tendency is to advise intervention for larger grade III lesions, particularly with testicular asymmetry >20%. From mid-teens, the decision can be guided by semen analysis.

mcqs.stem: In a child with a newly diagnosed varicocoele, which investigation should be performed to exclude a secondary cause?
mcqs.explanation: Scrotal ultrasound for varicocoele assessment should be combined with an abdominal scan to exclude a renal tumour, which can cause venous obstruction and secondary varicocoele (though this accounts for <1% of cases in childhood).

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is a recognised advantage of lymphatic preservation during varicocoele surgery?
mcqs.explanation: Preserving lymphatics during varicocoele surgery minimises the incidence of postoperative hydrocoele. Methylene blue injection into the scrotum can help demonstrate lymphatics.

mcqs.stem: A 13-year-old boy is diagnosed with a right-sided varicocoele. Which of the following additional findings should raise particular concern about a possible retroperitoneal mass?

options.text: A varicocoele that disappears when supine

mcqs.explanation: While >90% of varicocoeles are left-sided, an isolated right-sided varicocoele should raise suspicion of retroperitoneal pathology such as a tumour causing venous obstruction.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following best describes the effect of a varicocoele on testicular temperature?

options.text: It decreases scrotal temperature by increasing venous return
It increases scrotal temperature, reducing the normal temperature differential needed for spermatogenesis
It has no effect on scrotal temperature
It causes intermittent temperature spikes only during exercise

mcqs.explanation: A varicocoele causes an increase in temperature of the scrotal contents, reducing the normal temperature differential (2–3°C below core body temperature) necessary for normal spermatogenesis.

mcqs.stem: In the Palomo technique for varicocoele repair, which of the following structures are ligated?

options.text: The testicular artery, vein and lymphatics en bloc
The vas deferens and its artery

mcqs.explanation: Palomo's original description involves ligation and division of the testicular artery, vein and lymphatics together en bloc via a high retroperitoneal approach. Many surgeons now ligate only the veins.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with acute onset right hemi-scrotal pain, nausea, and vomiting. The right testis is swollen, tender, and lies horizontally with an absent cremasteric reflex. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Acute testicular torsion presents with sudden onset severe scrotal pain, often with nausea/vomiting. The testis is swollen, tender, and may lie horizontally. The cremasteric reflex is typically absent. This is a surgical emergency.

mcqs.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with 3 days of mild left scrotal pain. On examination, there is a small, tender nodule at the upper pole of the testis with a 'blue dot' visible through the scrotal skin. The testis itself is not swollen and has a normal lie. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Torsion of the appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni)

mcqs.explanation: Torsion of the testicular appendix typically presents with subacute onset of mild to moderate pain. A pathognomonic 'blue dot' sign may be visible at the upper pole of the testis. The testis itself is usually normal.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most common congenital anomaly in males?
mcqs.explanation: Undescended testis is the commonest congenital anomaly in males, with a reported incidence of 1.5–3% in term infants and up to 30% in premature infants.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following statements regarding testicular microcalcification in children and adolescents is correct?

options.text: It is an absolute indication for testicular biopsy
It requires lifelong annual ultrasound surveillance in all cases
In asymptomatic individuals without other risk factors it is usually an incidental finding and surveillance is not required
It is pathognomonic of testicular malignancy

mcqs.explanation: The weight of evidence indicates that asymptomatic testicular microcalcification is an incidental finding in most individuals. Prolonged ultrasound surveillance is not required unless other risk factors are present. Testicular biopsy is not indicated in adolescents.

mcqs.stem: A man with a history of bilateral undescended testes treated with orchidopexy in childhood seeks counselling about fertility. Based on published data, what approximate paternity rate would he be counselled to expect?
mcqs.explanation: Paternity rates for men with a history of bilateral UDT range from 45–65% in published series, compared to 80–90% for unilateral UDT. Semen analysis is normal in only 25–30% of men with bilateral UDT.

mcqs.stem: A cohort study examines testicular cancer risk in men with a history of orchidopexy. Compared to the general population, the relative risk of testicular cancer in men who underwent orchidopexy after 13 years of age is closest to which value?
mcqs.explanation: In a Swedish study, men who had orchidopexy after 13 years of age had a risk of testicular cancer 5.4 times higher than the normal population, compared to 2.3 times higher in those operated under age 13.

mcqs.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with an empty right hemi-scrotum and a visible swelling in the perineum just lateral to the scrotum. What is this finding most consistent with?
mcqs.explanation: An ectopic testis lies outside the normal line of descent. The most common ectopic location is the perineum, lateral to the scrotum. Other ectopic sites include the thigh and crossed ectopia (contralateral hemiscrotum).

mcqs.stem: In an infant, which clinical feature helps distinguish an inguinal hernia (which requires prompt surgery) from a communicating hydrocoele (which may be observed)?

options.text: Transillumination is positive in hernia but negative in hydrocoele
A hernia is reducible whereas a hydrocoele is not
A hydrocoele transilluminates but this does not reliably distinguish the two
Hernias present at birth whereas hydrocoeles present only after 2 years

mcqs.explanation: Transillumination does not reliably distinguish between the two in infants because a hernia will also transilluminate. While hernias are typically reducible and hydrocoeles are not, the key distinction is clinical context.

mcqs.stem: At what age do primary spermatocytes first appear in the human testis?

options.text: During the second decade of life

mcqs.explanation: Ad spermatogonia proliferate and mature into primary spermatocytes at around 5–6 years of age. These primary spermatocytes remain quiescent until puberty, when they undergo meiotic division.

mcqs.stem: A 6-month-old boy is undergoing inguinal orchidopexy. During the procedure, the surgeon finds the inguinal canal is empty despite the testis having been palpable preoperatively.
