mcqs.explanation: Traumatic injuries of the female urethra are extremely rare because of the shorter length of the urethra and the more protected location. These injuries typically occur in conjunction with severe pelvic fractures and vaginal injury.

mcqs.stem: In the neonatal period, penile injuries are most commonly related to which of the following?
mcqs.explanation: In the neonatal period, penile injuries are usually iatrogenic and related to circumcision.
mcqs.topic: External genitalia â€” neonatal penile injury

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old uncircumcised boy presents with his foreskin trapped in a zipper. Which of the following is an appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Use of mineral oil to release the skin or dividing the zipper with bone cutters
Surgical excision of the trapped skin

mcqs.explanation: Zipper injuries usually occur in uncircumcised boys and may be treated using mineral oil to release the trapped skin from the zipper or dividing the zipper with bone cutters.

mcqs.stem: A 12-year-old boy falls onto a bicycle crossbar and presents with scrotal pain and swelling. Testicular rupture is suspected. What is the mechanism by which a bicycle crossbar typically injures the testis?

options.text: Penetrating injury through the scrotal wall
The testis is compressed against the pubic ramus

mcqs.explanation: The testis may be injured as a result of a bicycle crossbar injury in which the testis is compressed against the pubic ramus, causing disruption of the tunica albuginea.
mcqs.topic: External genitalia â€” testicular injury mechanism

mcqs.stem: For evaluating suspected testicular injury after scrotal trauma, which imaging modality is considered excellent?
mcqs.explanation: In cases of scrotal trauma, ultrasound is an excellent investigation to assess any testicular injury.

mcqs.stem: A 12-year-old boy presents after a straddle injury with significant scrotal pain and hematoma. Ultrasound suggests testicular rupture with a tear in the tunica albuginea. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Observation with scrotal support and analgesia

mcqs.explanation: Early scrotal exploration should be undertaken if there is clinical or ultrasound evidence of testicular rupture or a significant tear in the tunica albuginea.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with high-flow priapism following perineal trauma from a bicycle accident. What is the most appropriate initial management approach?
mcqs.explanation: High-flow priapism may be managed non-operatively because of the likelihood of spontaneous resolution within days to weeks after injury. If conservative measures fail, embolization with autologous clot is the preferred treatment.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with a genital injury that appears inconsistent with the reported mechanism. What is the most important consideration in this scenario?

options.text: Immediate surgical repair of any visible injury
High suspicion of possible sexual abuse

mcqs.explanation: A high index of suspicion of possible sexual abuse is required whenever a girl presents with a genital injury, particularly if the nature of the injury seems discordant with the history. Sexual abuse or assault is implicated in up to 25% of girls presenting with genital injuries.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old girl sustains a significant vaginal straddle injury. After primary repair, what is the most important reason for long-term follow-up into puberty?

options.text: To exclude possible vaginal stenosis or hematocolpos

mcqs.explanation: Follow-up into puberty is essential to exclude possible stenosis or hematocolpos, especially in patients with extensive urethrovaginal injuries. The most significant complication following vaginal trauma is stricture.
mcqs.topic: External genitalia â€” vaginal trauma follow-up

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a dog bite to the penis. After initial assessment under anesthesia, which of the following adjunctive measures should be administered?
mcqs.explanation: Genital injuries caused by animal bites should be assessed under anesthesia to fully assess the extent of trauma. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and tetanus prophylaxis should be administered.

mcqs.stem: Excessive removal of penile skin due to neonatal circumcision has occurred. What is the most appropriate management in the majority of cases?

options.text: Immediate complex reconstruction with free skin grafts

mcqs.explanation: Excessive removal of penile skin due to neonatal circumcision can be managed conservatively in the majority of cases. Later reconstructive surgery (including free skin grafts) may occasionally be required if there has been extensive excision of penile shaft skin.

mcqs.stem: A 3-year-old boy with an impalpable testis is scheduled for diagnostic laparoscopy. In 1976, who first reported using laparoscopy therapeutically for cryptorchidism?
mcqs.explanation: Cortesi was the first to report the use of laparoscopy for a therapeutic procedure in the treatment of cryptorchidism in 1976.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is NOT a recognised benefit of laparoscopic surgery in children?
mcqs.explanation: Laparoscopic surgery is associated with fewer wound complications, not higher. The other options are all established benefits.

mcqs.stem: An 8-year-old is listed for laparoscopic nephrectomy. What anatomical advantage does a smaller patient confer during laparoscopy?

options.text: Insufflation pressures can be higher than in adults
Both upper and lower urinary tract are accessible through the same port sites
The Hasson technique is not required

mcqs.explanation: The smaller size of the paediatric patient allows access to both the upper and lower urinary tract through the same port sites, a great advantage.

mcqs.stem: During a robotic-assisted procedure, the operating surgeon notices the absence of haptic feedback. Which major limitation of the Da Vinci system does this describe?
mcqs.explanation: A major limitation of the Da Vinci robotic system is the lack of tactile feedback, requiring the surgeon to rely far more on visual information.

mcqs.stem: When considering the economic impact of laparoscopic surgery in paediatric patients, how does the value of reduced hospital stay compare with that in adults?

options.text: It is more important in children
It is of less importance in children than in adults
It is the primary driver for laparoscopic adoption in children
It is of equal importance in both groups

mcqs.explanation: The economic advantage of reduced hospital stay is of less importance in children than in adults, as stated in the chapter.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old child with bilateral MCDK requires surgery. Which condition would be an absolute contraindication to a minimally invasive approach?
mcqs.explanation: Uncorrectable coagulopathy is listed as an absolute contraindication to MIS, along with significant comorbidity and sepsis.

mcqs.stem: A 6-month-old infant requires diagnostic laparoscopy. What is the preferred method for establishing a pneumoperitoneum in children?
mcqs.explanation: The Hasson technique (open technique) is the preferred method for establishing a pneumoperitoneum in children. The Veress needle is a blind technique.

mcqs.stem: A trainee is selecting a laparoscope for a routine paediatric nephrectomy. Which size and angle provides optimum visualisation for most procedures?
mcqs.explanation: A 5 mm 30° laparoscope provides optimum visualisation for most procedures. Laparoscopic instruments are available in 2, 2.5, 3 and 5 mm sizes.

mcqs.stem: Why are bladeless trocars considered safer for use in children than bladed trocars?

options.text: They are significantly cheaper than bladed trocars
The paediatric abdominal wall is more compliant and less muscular
They can be inserted without a skin incision

mcqs.explanation: Bladeless trocars are generally safer for use in children because the paediatric abdominal wall is more compliant and less muscular.

mcqs.stem: During a transperitoneal laparoscopic procedure on a 1-year-old, why must trocar insertion always be performed under direct vision?

options.text: It is faster than blind insertion
The liver, spleen and bladder are relatively large compared with the working space
The Hasson cannula cannot be used blindly

mcqs.explanation: Because of the limited working space and relatively large size of the liver, spleen and bladder in children, trocars and instruments should always be inserted under direct vision to avoid iatrogenic complications.

mcqs.stem: A 4-month-old undergoes laparoscopic pyeloplasty. The anaesthetist plans close monitoring in the early postoperative period because young children:

options.text: Have a higher risk of wound infection
Require higher insufflation pressures than adults
Are more likely to require conversion to open surgery
Absorb proportionately more CO2 and have greater transient CO2 elimination postdesufflation

mcqs.explanation: Younger children absorb proportionately more CO2 during pneumoperitoneum and may experience greater transient elimination of CO2 postdesufflation, necessitating close monitoring.

mcqs.stem: A neonate is undergoing a prolonged laparoscopic procedure. Why are underbody warming mats routinely used?

options.text: Children have a significant risk of hypothermia, especially with prolonged operating times and high gas flow
To reduce the risk of hyperthermia from insufflation

mcqs.explanation: Children are at significant risk of developing hypothermia during laparoscopic surgery, especially with prolonged operating times and high gas flow, so warming mats are used routinely.

mcqs.stem: During a laparoscopic nephrectomy, the surgeon anticipates needing an Endopouch retrieval device. What size of Hasson cannula is required?
mcqs.explanation: A 10 mm cannula is required when an Endopouch retrieval device is needed for specimen extraction. For diagnostic and reconstructive procedures, a 5 mm cannula is sufficient.

mcqs.stem: A 6-month-old infant is scheduled for laparoscopic nephrectomy. The surgeon chooses an approach that avoids colonic mobilisation. Which approach is being used?
mcqs.explanation: The retroperitoneoscopic approach obviates the need for colonic mobilisation and avoids the risks of injury to hollow viscera and the potential for adhesion formation.

mcqs.stem: Which disadvantage is associated with the retroperitoneoscopic approach for nephrectomy?

options.text: Reversed orientation of the kidney and hilum with the patient semiprone

mcqs.explanation: The retroperitoneoscopic approach has the disadvantage of reversed orientation of the kidney and hilum with the patient semiprone or prone, and the comparatively smaller working space makes it more difficult to master.

mcqs.stem: During retroperitoneoscopic nephrectomy, after dividing the hilar vessels, the ureter is traced and divided. If vesicoureteric reflux is present, what additional step is recommended?

options.text: A ureteric stent is placed for 4 weeks
The ureter is ligated before division or a urethral catheter is left for 48 hours

mcqs.explanation: If there is reflux into the ureter, it is ligated before being divided, or alternatively the bladder is drained with a urethral catheter for 48 hours.

mcqs.stem: A systematic review comparing transperitoneal (TP) and retroperitoneoscopic (RP) nephrectomy found that RP nephrectomy had which outcome advantage?

options.text: Faster recovery with a lower conversion rate (<3%) and fewer complications

mcqs.explanation: A systematic review found that while overall results were broadly similar, recovery was faster after RP nephrectomy, with a lower conversion rate (<3%) and fewer complications.

mcqs.stem: A child with end-stage renal disease requires bilateral nephrectomy before peritoneal dialysis. Why is the retroperitoneoscopic approach particularly suitable?

options.text: The peritoneum remains intact, so peritoneal dialysis can begin immediately
It preserves the ureters for transplantation
It has a lower risk of bleeding than TP

mcqs.explanation: Because the peritoneum remains intact during RP nephrectomy, postoperative peritoneal dialysis can be performed immediately, avoiding the need for a period of haemodialysis.

mcqs.stem: The authors' institution has performed over 150 SIMPL nephrectomies. What does SIMPL stand for?

options.text: Standardised intracorporeal manual pure laparoscopic nephrectomy

mcqs.explanation: SIMPL stands for 'single instrument port laparoscopic technique nephrectomy', used in patients from 1 month to 18 years of age.

mcqs.stem: In the SIMPL nephrectomy technique, where can a second instrument port be placed if needed?

options.text: Through the sacrospinalis muscle medial to the camera port

mcqs.explanation: If required, a second instrument port can be placed through the sacrospinalis muscle in a position medial to the camera port site.

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old girl has a poorly functioning upper pole moiety with an ectopic ureter causing continuous urinary incontinence. What is the most commonly performed laparoscopic procedure?
mcqs.explanation: Upper pole heminephrectomy is the most commonly performed heminephrectomy. It is used to remove a poorly functioning upper pole with an ectopic ureter causing incontinence in girls.

mcqs.stem: Lower pole heminephrectomy is most commonly performed for which indication?

options.text: Reflux-associated nephropathy with loss of function

mcqs.explanation: Lower pole heminephrectomy is mostly performed for reflux-associated nephropathy or, rarely, lower moiety PUJ obstruction with loss of function.

mcqs.stem: During retroperitoneoscopic heminephrectomy, after dividing the blood supply to the affected moiety, what intraoperative finding demarcates it from the healthy moiety?

options.text: Pallor of the affected renal parenchyma

mcqs.explanation: Pallor of the renal parenchyma following division of the blood vessels to the affected moiety serves to demarcate it from the healthy moiety.

mcqs.stem: Which statement accurately describes retroperitoneoscopic heminephrectomy?

options.text: It has a long learning curve, especially for lower pole heminephrectomy
It is easier than transperitoneal heminephrectomy
It is contraindicated in children under 2 years
It does not allow direct visualisation of the duplex kidney

mcqs.explanation: Retroperitoneoscopic heminephrectomy is a challenging procedure with a long learning curve, especially for lower pole heminephrectomy.

mcqs.stem: In a child with recurrent UTIs and dense adhesions from prior inflammation, which approach may be preferable for heminephrectomy?

options.text: Laparoscopic upper pole heminephrectomy is always preferred
Percutaneous ablation of the affected moiety

mcqs.explanation: In children with recurrent or recent UTIs causing dense adhesions, an open approach may be preferable for technical reasons and to avoid excessive blood loss.

mcqs.stem: A 9-year-old with symptomatic PUJ obstruction has an ultrasound showing pelvic AP diameter 25 mm with calyceal dilatation and differential function of 38%. Which of the following is an indication for pyeloplasty?

options.text: Pelvic AP diameter >15 mm with calyceal dilatation
Pelvic AP diameter >10 mm without calyceal dilatation
Differential function of 45% with no symptoms
Symptomatic obstruction with AP diameter >20 mm and reduced function <40%

mcqs.explanation: Symptomatic PUJ obstruction with AP diameter >20 mm and calyceal dilatation and reduced function (<40%), or severe hydronephrosis with AP >30 mm, are indications for pyeloplasty.

mcqs.stem: Which anatomical variant is a contraindication to laparoscopic pyeloplasty?
mcqs.explanation: Contraindications for laparoscopic pyeloplasty include a small extrarenal pelvis, previous renal surgery and anatomical variants such as a horseshoe kidney.

mcqs.stem: During laparoscopic pyeloplasty, what is the function of the 'hitch stitch'?

options.text: To retract the liver during right-sided procedures
To close the retroperitoneum at the end of the case
To stabilise the renal pelvis by passing it through the abdominal wall

mcqs.explanation: A 'hitch stitch' is used to stabilise the renal pelvis by passing it through the abdominal wall, facilitating suturing during dismembered pyeloplasty.

mcqs.stem: After completing the posterior wall of the anastomosis during laparoscopic pyeloplasty, how is urinary drainage established before completing the anterior wall?

options.text: A JJ stent is introduced antegradely into the bladder over a guidewire
The hitch stitch is removed and a nephrostomy is placed
A urethral catheter is inserted before closure
An external drain is placed in the renal pelvis

mcqs.explanation: A JJ stent is introduced and passed antegradely down to the bladder over a guidewire before completion of the anastomosis with a further continuous absorbable suture.

mcqs.stem: Some authors prefer a 'nephrostent' over a JJ stent after laparoscopic pyeloplasty. What advantage does it offer?

options.text: It drains externally and can be removed without a second procedure
It reduces the risk of stricture formation

mcqs.explanation: The nephrostent drains externally, overcoming complications of an indwelling JJ stent, and can be removed without a second procedure.

mcqs.stem: During robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty, what minimum distance between ports is required to maximise robotic arm movement?
mcqs.explanation: Ports should be separated by at least 6–8 cm to maximise robotic arm movement during robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty.

mcqs.stem: In a large single-centre study, robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty (RALP) had what overall success rate?
mcqs.explanation: The overall success rate for RALP in a large single-centre study was 96%, with 98% performed via a transperitoneal approach and mean operating time of 199 minutes.

mcqs.stem: A comparative study of open, conventional laparoscopic and robotic-assisted pyeloplasty found no difference in success rates. What advantage did RALP specifically demonstrate?

options.text: Shorter operating time than open surgery
Longer hospital stay but better cosmesis
Reduced analgesic requirements and shorter hospital stay

mcqs.explanation: RALP was associated with reduced analgesic requirements and shorter hospital stay, with no difference in success rates between the three modalities.

mcqs.stem: The Da Vinci surgical instruments provide how many degrees of freedom in movement?
mcqs.explanation: The Da Vinci instruments provide seven degrees of freedom in movement, with 8-mm instruments articulating via a pitch-roll-yaw mechanism and 5-mm instruments in a 'serpentine' manner.

mcqs.stem: Why is the Da Vinci robot currently unsuitable for use in neonates?

options.text: 3-mm instruments are unavailable and a minimum 8 cm port separation is required
Neonates cannot tolerate general anaesthesia for robotic surgery
There are no approved robotic procedures for neonates
The neonatal abdominal wall is too compliant for safe port placement

mcqs.explanation: The lack of commercially available 3-mm instruments combined with the minimum 8 cm distance required between each port precludes the use of the robot in neonates.

mcqs.stem: In antenatally detected hydronephrosis, lower pole crossing vessels are present in what percentage of cases?
mcqs.explanation: Lower pole crossing vessels are found in only 6–11% of cases of antenatally detected hydronephrosis, compared with 50–60% of PUJ obstruction in older children and adults.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old with intermittent flank pain and no history of antenatal hydronephrosis has extrinsic PUJ compression by crossing vessels without intrinsic obstruction. Which procedure is indicated?
mcqs.explanation: If obstruction is due to extrinsic compression without intrinsic obstruction, a vascular hitch can be considered, where vessels are relocated and anchored to the anterior wall of the renal pelvis.

mcqs.stem: What advantage does the vascular hitch procedure have over dismembered pyeloplasty?

options.text: Higher success rates in all age groups
Suitable for all types of PUJ obstruction
Can be performed without a stent or drain
Avoids transecting the pelvis, reducing leak risk, and is less technically demanding

mcqs.explanation: The vascular hitch avoids transecting the pelvis, minimising urinary leak risk, is less technically demanding, carries a lower complication rate and results in shorter hospital stay.

mcqs.stem: With appropriate patient selection, a recent multicentre study of the vascular hitch procedure reported what rate of symptom resolution?
mcqs.explanation: With appropriate selection, the vascular hitch offers 100% resolution of symptoms, decreased hydronephrosis and improved MAG3 renogram drainage.

mcqs.stem: Which finding is a contraindication to laparoscopic adrenalectomy?

options.text: Tumour larger than 8 cm in diameter

mcqs.explanation: Contraindications to laparoscopic adrenalectomy include tumours exceeding 8 cm, previous renal surgery, tumour thrombus in the adrenal vein or IVC, coagulation disorders and suspected adrenal carcinoma.

mcqs.stem: A child with phaeochromocytoma is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy. What is the recommended preoperative pharmacological regimen?

options.text: α-blockers for 7 days, with β-blockers added if required

mcqs.explanation: Children receive 7 days preoperative α-blockers such as phenoxybenzamine; β-blockers such as propranolol may be added to decrease tachyarrhythmia risk.

mcqs.stem: Why is left laparoscopic adrenalectomy considered more difficult than right?

options.text: The gland and adrenal vein are smaller with fewer clear landmarks like the IVC
The left adrenal gland is larger and more vascular
The left adrenal vein is longer and more fragile
The spleen constantly obstructs the view

mcqs.explanation: Left adrenalectomy is more difficult due to the smaller gland and adrenal vein and the lack of clear landmarks such as the IVC.

mcqs.stem: What is the single most critical intraoperative priority during laparoscopic adrenalectomy for tumour?

options.text: Using the transperitoneal rather than RP approach
Avoiding capsular breach and tumour spillage

mcqs.explanation: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy remains challenging, particularly due to the paramount importance of avoiding capsular breach and tumour spillage.

mcqs.stem: In vesicoscopic ureteric reimplantation using the Cohen technique, how is the working space created?

options.text: The bladder is insufflated with CO2 to create a pneumovesicum
Via a suprapubic catheter inserted under vision

mcqs.explanation: In the vesicoscopic technique, the bladder is insufflated with CO2 to create a pneumovesicum, and the refluxing ureter is mobilised and reimplanted via a cross-trigonal submucosal tunnel.

mcqs.stem: A recognised risk of laparoscopic extravesical ureteral reimplantation, shared with the open approach, is:

options.text: Ureteric stricture at the reimplantation site
Damage to pelvic innervation causing bladder dysfunction and urinary retention

mcqs.explanation: As with open extravesical reimplantation, the laparoscopic technique carries risk of damage to pelvic innervation, with consequent bladder dysfunction, urinary retention and constipation.

mcqs.stem: In a child with a disorder of sex development (DSD), what role does laparoscopy play?

options.text: It is restricted to gonadal biopsy alone
It is contraindicated due to anatomical ambiguity
It allows direct visualisation of internal genitalia, gonadal biopsy and gonadectomy when indicated
It is only used for Müllerian remnant excision

mcqs.explanation: Laparoscopy in DSD permits direct visualisation of internal genital anatomy, gonadal biopsies, and removal of dysgenetic gonads or those discordant with the sex of rearing.

mcqs.stem: Before docking the robot for a robotic-assisted laparoscopic augmentation ileocystoplasty with Mitrofanoff, what preliminary step should be performed?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy to confirm adequate appendix length

mcqs.explanation: Diagnostic laparoscopy is performed initially to confirm adequate appendix length for a Mitrofanoff procedure prior to docking the robot.

mcqs.stem: What currently limits widespread acceptance of robotic reconstructive surgery in paediatric urology?

options.text: Paucity of published data and lack of randomised controlled trials
Higher complication rates compared with open surgery
Inability to perform intracorporeal suturing with the robot
Longer recovery times than conventional laparoscopy

mcqs.explanation: Acceptance of robotic reconstructive surgery is limited by the paucity of published data and lack of randomised controlled trials.

mcqs.stem: What is the current role of minimally invasive surgery for Wilms tumour?

options.text: In selected patients results are comparable to open surgery, but most centres still favour open surgery
MIS is the standard of care for all Wilms tumours
MIS is contraindicated in all Wilms tumour cases
MIS is used only for bilateral Wilms tumour

mcqs.explanation: In selected patients, laparoscopic total nephrectomy and lymphadenectomy results are comparable to open surgery, but most oncology centres still favour open surgery for Wilms tumour.

mcqs.stem: What are the three major components of the Da Vinci robotic surgical system?

options.text: Master console, slave unit, and video tower

mcqs.explanation: The three components are: the robot (mobile tower with arms), bedside cart (image processing and light source), and console (surgeon's workstation).

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old male with a history of posterior urethral valves (PUV) resected in infancy is transitioning to adult care. His current GFR is 45 mL/min/1.73 m². According to historical data, what is his approximate risk of progressing to end-stage renal failure within the next 16 years?

options.text: Substantial risk, with most patients reaching ESRF within this timeframe
Minimal risk, as GFR above 40 mL/min/1.73 m² carries no significant risk
Moderate risk, but only if proteinuria is also present
No risk, provided he remains normotensive

mcqs.explanation: Historical data indicate that patients reaching adolescence with a GFR below 40 mL/min/1.73 m² are at substantial risk of progressing to end-stage renal failure within 16 years. This patient's GFR of 45 mL/min is near this threshold, and the downward trajectory can be delayed with rigorous control of proteinuria and blood pressure using ACE inhibitors, but the risk remains substantial.

mcqs.stem: A 19-year-old woman with a history of childhood ureteric reimplantation for vesicoureteric reflux is now pregnant. Which of the following best describes her risk of urinary tract infection during pregnancy compared to other groups?

options.text: She is at lower risk than women with persistent untreated reflux
She is at greater risk of urinary infection in pregnancy than any other group
Her risk is equivalent to that of the general obstetric population
She is at increased risk only if she has bilateral reflux nephropathy

mcqs.explanation: Studies have reported that women who have previously undergone ureteric reimplantation are at greater risk of urinary infection in pregnancy than any other group. The reason is unclear but may relate to the original severity of reflux or an unexplained consequence of the reimplantation procedure itself.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most important factor in determining whether an adult with a history of vesicoureteric reflux (VUR) that has resolved in childhood requires lifelong follow-up?

options.text: The presence of reflux nephropathy (renal scarring)
The severity of the original VUR grade
A history of breakthrough UTIs during childhood
The age at which reflux resolved

mcqs.explanation: Adults whose VUR has ceased or been corrected in childhood but who have reflux nephropathy (renal scarring) require lifelong follow-up. By age 18, 11% with unilateral scarring and 18% with bilateral scarring have developed hypertension, rising to 23% at 27 years. If renal damage reduces GFR below 40 mL/min/1.73 m², eventual renal failure may be inevitable.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old woman with persistent vesicoureteric reflux since childhood is asymptomatic. Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: No intervention is required as she is asymptomatic
Endoscopic treatment or surgical reimplantation is reasonable to protect against pyelonephritic scarring in future pregnancy
Lifelong continuous antibiotic prophylaxis is mandatory
Nephrectomy of the refluxing renal unit is indicated

mcqs.explanation: In asymptomatic females with persistent reflux, it is reasonable to recommend either endoscopic treatment or surgical reimplantation because their kidneys may still be at risk of developing pyelonephritic scarring during pregnancy. In asymptomatic males there is generally no indication for surgery in the absence of bladder outflow obstruction.

mcqs.stem: In a long-term follow-up study of patients with bladder exstrophy who underwent ureterosigmoidostomy with a mean follow-up of 24 years, which of the following was reported?

options.text: Daytime continence rate of 97% and stable renal function in 87% of patients
Daytime continence rate of 50% and progressive renal decline in most patients
Universal loss of sexual function with no reported pregnancies

mcqs.explanation: In one long-term follow-up study of ureterosigmoidostomy with follow-up of 24 years, 97% of patients were continent by day and 87% had stable renal function. However, 40% had disturbances of acid-base balance requiring alkaline supplements. Sexual function was impaired in all patients, but 40% of men achieved paternity and the women gave birth to six healthy children.

mcqs.stem: What is the reported continence rate following bladder neck closure combined with formation of a continent catheterisable stoma in patients with bladder exstrophy?
mcqs.explanation: A continence rate of 93% was reported in patients with bladder exstrophy who had undergone bladder neck closure combined with formation of a continent catheterisable stoma. This approach often requires bladder augmentation or subtotal substitution cystoplasty with intestinal segments.

mcqs.stem: In bladder exstrophy, the reported incidence of bladder cancer in long-term follow-up from the authors' unit was 4 out of 61 patients at a mean age of 41 years. How does this compare to the normal population?

options.text: 694 times greater than the normal population
Approximately 10 times greater than the normal population
No significant difference from the normal population
Lower than the normal population due to urinary diversion

mcqs.explanation: Bladder exstrophy is associated with a significantly higher incidence of malignancy. In a study from the authors' unit, 4 out of 61 patients developed bladder cancer at a mean age of 41 years — an incidence 694 times greater than that of the normal population. Most reported cases occurred in bladders defunctioned by urinary diversion in childhood.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old man with bladder exstrophy presents with concerns about penile length and curvature. What is the most appropriate first step in evaluating his penile deformity?

options.text: Referral for psychiatric evaluation of body dysmorphic disorder
Artificial erection test with saline injection into the corporal bodies
Magnetic resonance imaging of the pelvis

mcqs.explanation: In adults with bladder exstrophy, the extent of the penile deformity is evaluated by an artificial erection test in which saline is injected into the corporal bodies. Minor defects may be corrected by a ventral Nesbit's procedure, whereas severe deformity requires more extensive correction such as penile disassembly with rotation of the corpora.

mcqs.stem: What is the approximate incidence of uterine prolapse (procidentia) in women with bladder exstrophy?
mcqs.explanation: Troublesome uterine prolapse (procidentia) occurs in up to 50% of women with bladder exstrophy. Despite this, fertility is unaffected, and women can have normal sexual function. Labioplasty can improve cosmetic appearance.

mcqs.stem: According to the 2006 Chicago Consensus Document, what concern was raised regarding clitoral surgery performed in childhood for disorders of sex differentiation?
mcqs.explanation: The 2006 Chicago Consensus Document noted that clitoral surgery in childhood carries a risk of impaired sensation. It advocated a two-stage approach, particularly with regard to vaginoplasty. Studies have shown many young women who underwent vaginoplasty in childhood later required further vaginal surgery for normal comfortable intercourse.

mcqs.stem: What percentage of women who undergo clitoral surgery in childhood for virilised genitalia are reported to require further surgical revision?
mcqs.explanation: Further surgical revision is required in around 25% of women after clitoral surgery in childhood. This figure may be lower in the future because of changes in surgical techniques for feminising genitoplasty. Continuity of follow-up from childhood into early adulthood in a specialist adolescent gynaecology centre is essential.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old man who underwent hypospadias repair in childhood is dissatisfied with the cosmetic outcome. Which aspects of the result are most commonly the focus of patient dissatisfaction?

options.text: Urinary stream strength and post-void dribbling
Penile and glans size, penile curvature, scarring and absence of a normal prepuce
Recurrent urethral strictures and fistula formation
Reduced ejaculatory volume and pain during ejaculation

mcqs.explanation: Patients' dissatisfaction tends to centre on aspects of hypospadias that are the most difficult to correct: size of the penis and glans, penile curvature, scarring, and the absence of a normal prepuce. Surgeons tend to view their own results more favourably than patients, and up to 50% of men may experience a poor functional or cosmetic outcome, especially with proximal hypospadias.

mcqs.stem: Regarding sexual satisfaction outcomes in men who have undergone hypospadias repair, which of the following is accurate?

options.text: Over 95% of men report normal sexual satisfaction with no differences from controls
Overall sexual satisfaction averaging 80% has been reported, but men are more inhibited in seeking sexual contact
Sexual satisfaction is universally poor regardless of surgical technique
Sexual satisfaction correlates directly with uroflowmetry results

mcqs.explanation: An overall sexual satisfaction rate averaging 80% has been reported in different studies, although what constitutes sexual satisfaction is not always clearly defined. Men who have undergone surgical correction of hypospadias are more inhibited in seeking sexual contact. The presence of gross persistent abnormalities, especially chordee, may preclude normal intercourse.

mcqs.stem: A 20-year-old man with a history of penoscrotal hypospadias corrected in childhood is concerned about fertility. Which statement best describes the fertility outcomes in such patients?

options.text: Fertility is universally normal with paternity rates equivalent to the general population
Ejaculation is possible but poor, and infertility is common
Infertility is inevitable and all patients should be offered sperm banking
Fertility is normal if chordee has been adequately corrected

mcqs.explanation: Intercourse is possible following repair of penoscrotal or perineal hypospadias, but ejaculation is poor and infertility is common. Reduced semen quality has been reported in up to 50% of men with a history of hypospadias, although this does not appear to be accompanied by a corresponding decrease in paternity rates for less severe forms.

mcqs.stem: In patients with posterior urethral valves, which of the following is a recognised pattern of persistent bladder dysfunction despite successful valve resection?

options.text: Poor compliance, detrusor overactivity and reduced functional capacity
Persistent vesicoureteric reflux in all cases

mcqs.explanation: Differing patterns of bladder dysfunction may persist despite successful relief of obstruction by valve resection. These include poor compliance, detrusor overactivity and reduced functional capacity — collectively termed the 'valve bladder'. In adults, the pattern may evolve to chronic retention, often with high pressure.

mcqs.stem: According to a 1988 study, what was the reported incidence of renal failure at 30 years in patients with posterior urethral valves?
mcqs.explanation: A study published in 1988 reported the incidence of renal failure in PUV patients as 23% at 10 years, 30% at 16 years and 43% at 30 years. More recent data show no real change in recent decades, with 10-15% requiring renal replacement therapy and a 20-40% incidence of renal impairment.

mcqs.stem: In a 15-year-old boy with a history of PUV, why may normal plasma creatinine at the beginning of puberty not guarantee normal creatinine at the end of puberty?

options.text: Increasing muscle mass at puberty places an increased load on renal reserve
Creatinine assays become less accurate during adolescence
The remaining nephrons undergo accelerated apoptosis at puberty

mcqs.explanation: Increasing muscle mass at the time of puberty places an increased load on the renal reserve, with the result that normal levels of plasma creatinine at the beginning of puberty do not guarantee that the plasma creatinine will still be normal at the end of puberty. This underscores the importance of ongoing surveillance during the adolescent period.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is NOT recognised as a significant prognostic factor indicating poor long-term renal outcome in patients with posterior urethral valves?

options.text: Diagnosis before the age of 1 year
Diurnal incontinence persisting after 5 years of age

mcqs.explanation: Significant prognostic factors for poor long-term outcome in PUV include: diagnosis before age 1 year (including prenatal diagnosis), diurnal incontinence persisting after 5 years of age, persistent bilateral reflux, and polyuria. Unilateral reflux is not listed as a poor prognostic factor — it is persistent bilateral reflux that carries a worse prognosis.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old man with a history of PUV complains of poor ejaculation. Semen analysis shows lack of liquefaction, low sperm count, poor motility and high pH. What pH value would be consistent with this condition?
mcqs.explanation: The seminal abnormality in PUV patients is characterised by lack of liquefaction, low sperm count, poor motility and high pH (up to 9.5). Damage to the prostatic tissue from high-pressure outflow obstruction before valve resection causes impaired semen quality and poor ejaculation in up to 50% of patients.

mcqs.stem: What proportion of men with a history of PUV encounter difficulty in achieving paternity?
mcqs.explanation: Up to a third of men with a history of PUV encounter difficulty in achieving paternity. This is due to impaired semen quality (lack of liquefaction, low sperm count, poor motility, high pH) and poor ejaculation resulting from prostatic damage and posterior urethral dilatation.

mcqs.stem: In PUV patients who undergo renal transplantation, current 5-year graft survival rates compared to other transplant recipients are best described as:

options.text: Significantly lower than all other recipients
Comparable, although PUV patients experience more UTIs and higher plasma creatinine from 7 years onwards
Superior to other recipients due to pre-transplant bladder optimisation
Equivalent only if the native bladder has been removed

mcqs.explanation: Current 5-year graft survival figures in PUV patients are comparable to other recipients, although PUV transplant patients tend to experience more UTIs and have higher levels of plasma creatinine than other transplant patients, especially from 7 years onwards. A French series reported 60% graft survival at 15 years. Graft survival was not significantly different between those with and without enterocystoplasty when the bladder was 'safe' prior to transplantation.

mcqs.stem: In prune-belly syndrome, which of the following best describes the current understanding of testicular histology and fertility potential?

options.text: All men with prune-belly syndrome have Sertoli cell-only histology and are universally sterile
Testes are not always devoid of germ cells, and the outlook for fertility may not be as bleak as once thought
Germ cells are present but immotile due to absent epididymal development
Fertility is normal if orchidopexy is performed in infancy

mcqs.explanation: Historically, men with prune-belly syndrome were thought to be sterile with Sertoli cell-only histology. However, it has become apparent that testes are not always devoid of germ cells, so the outlook for fertility might not be as bleak as once thought. Serum testosterone levels are usually normal, but serum LH levels are significantly elevated.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the three characteristic components of prune-belly syndrome?

options.text: Absence of the muscle of the anterior abdominal wall
Variable functional and anatomical anomalies of the urinary tract

mcqs.explanation: The three components of prune-belly syndrome are: bilateral undescended testes, absence of the muscle of the anterior abdominal wall, and variable functional and anatomical anomalies of the urinary tract. Renal agenesis is not a component, although renal dysplasia of variable severity is present.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old man with prune-belly syndrome has normal renal function (GFR 85 mL/min/1.73 m²) but concerns about his abdominal appearance. Regarding abdominal wall reconstruction, which of the following is most accurate?

options.text: Abdominal wall reconstruction reliably restores normal core muscle function
The abdominal wall lacks necessary underlying muscular or fascial support, and reconstruction improves appearance but not function
The protuberant abdomen typically causes significant functional limitation

mcqs.explanation: Although abdominal wall reconstruction can improve the wrinkled appearance of the skin, it is doubtful whether this alone provides long-term benefit since the abdominal wall lacks the necessary underlying muscular or fascial support. In adult life the abdomen is protuberant resembling a 'pot-belly', but this creates no functional problem other than inability to sit up directly from lying.

mcqs.stem: In a cohort of prune-belly syndrome patients who had undergone minimal surgery in childhood, what proportion had normal renal function at a mean age of 24 years?
mcqs.explanation: In a cohort of 21 adults with prune-belly syndrome who had undergone minimal surgery in childhood, 17 (approximately 81%) had normal renal function with a mean GFR of 85 mL/min/1.73 m² at a mean age of 24 years. Four patients were in end-stage renal failure and another four were hypertensive. Prune-belly syndrome is now very rare in the UK due to prenatal detection and termination.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is a known long-term metabolic complication of enterocystoplasty?

options.text: Hyperchloraemic acidosis occurring in up to 14% of patients

mcqs.explanation: Hyperchloraemic acidosis occurs in up to 14% of patients after enterocystoplasty, although many more may have a metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Anaemia occurs in 8%, and Vitamin B12 deficiency is a potential complication after 5 years, especially if terminal ileum has been used.

mcqs.stem: A patient with a continent urinary diversion (Mitrofanoff procedure) presents with difficulty catheterising the stoma. What is the most common complication responsible for this presentation?

options.text: Stenosis at the junction between the skin and catheterisable channel
Calculus formation at the channel entrance

mcqs.explanation: The commonest complication of continent urinary diversion (Mitrofanoff procedure) is stenosis at the junction between the skin and the catheterisable channel (usually appendix). The reported incidence is 40%, with further revision required in 40% of those affected. Despite this, overall patient satisfaction with continent urinary diversion is very high.

mcqs.stem: Regarding malignancy risk after enterocystoplasty, which of the following statements is most accurate?

options.text: The risk of malignancy is higher than after ureterosigmoidostomy
Routine periodic cystoscopy is recommended as an effective screening tool
The overall risk of malignancy is likely to be considerably lower than following ureterosigmoidostomy, and routine periodic cystoscopy has not been shown to be effective for screening
Malignancy does not occur after enterocystoplasty

mcqs.explanation: Although nitrosamines and other carcinogens have been identified in bladders following enterocystoplasty, the overall risk of malignancy is likely to be considerably lower than following ureterosigmoidostomy. One study found a 3.8% incidence of bladder malignancy after 20-25 years of follow-up. Protocols no longer include routine periodic cystoscopy since this has not been shown to be effective for screening.

mcqs.stem: What is the reported incidence of stone formation in reconstructed urinary reservoirs after enterocystoplasty?
mcqs.explanation: The reported incidence of stone formation in reconstructed urinary reservoirs is at least 15%. Risk factors include infection, intermittent catheterisation (CIC) and retained mucus. All urinary reservoirs created from intestinal segments share the same basic long-term problems, including metabolic disturbances and stone formation.

mcqs.stem: According to survival data for patients with spina bifida, what percentage of patients are still alive at the age of 35?
mcqs.explanation: Only 45% of patients with spina bifida are still alive at the age of 35. The mortality rate in the first year is approximately 20%, followed by a period of stability in childhood, before mortality increases again from puberty onwards. Neurological complications related to hydrocephalus are the commonest cause of death in childhood and adolescence.

mcqs.stem: In spina bifida patients, what are the commonest causes of death in adults?
mcqs.explanation: Neurological complications related to hydrocephalus are the commonest cause of death in childhood and adolescence in spina bifida patients, whereas cardiovascular and pulmonary diseases are the commonest causes in adults. Renal insufficiency was once a major cause of mortality, but death from renal failure is now very unusual.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old with spina bifida has deteriorating urinary continence. The parents ask about surgical options. When should major reconstructive surgery ideally be completed in such patients?

options.text: Before puberty, as continence tends to deteriorate rather than improve after this age
After completion of growth to avoid the need for revision surgery
Only after the patient has demonstrated the ability to self-catheterise
In early adulthood when the bladder has reached its final capacity

mcqs.explanation: Whenever possible, any major reconstructive surgery should be completed before puberty because there is a natural tendency for continence to deteriorate rather than to improve after this age. Very few adults with spina bifida, especially females, can learn to perform urethral self-catheterisation if they were not taught it in childhood.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following best describes the approach to bowel management in patients with spina bifida?

options.text: All patients require a colostomy by adolescence
Faecal continence is usually dependent on acquiring a pattern of 'controlled constipation'
Biofeedback therapy achieves continence in most patients
Bowel management is unnecessary if the neurological level is below L2

mcqs.explanation: Even the mildest neurological deficit in spina bifida leads to incompetence of the anal sphincter. Faecal continence is usually dependent on acquiring a pattern of 'controlled constipation'. The Malone antegrade continence enema (MACE) procedure has been an important advance, and newer alternatives such as transanal irrigation systems are also effective.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old woman with spina bifida and a neurological level above T10 is concerned about sexual function. What proportion of women with higher cord lesions or urinary incontinence would be expected to have normal sexual function?
mcqs.explanation: Whereas sexual function is likely to be normal in the majority of females with a neurological level below L2, only 20% of those with higher cord lesions or urinary incontinence will have normal sexual function. Nevertheless, two-thirds of women form steady sexual relationships regardless of the degree of handicap or continence.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old woman with spina bifida is planning a pregnancy. What is the risk of neural tube defect in her offspring?

options.text: No increased risk compared to the general population

mcqs.explanation: The overall risk of neural tube defect in offspring of women with spina bifida is 1:23 (1:50 for sons and 1:13 for daughters). This risk can be significantly reduced by folic acid supplements for 3 months prior to conception. Few spina bifida patients accept antenatal screening and termination of affected pregnancies.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old man with spina bifida has absent sacral reflexes and a neurological level above T10. What is the approximate likelihood of him being potent?
mcqs.explanation: Of men with spina bifida who have absent sacral reflexes and a primary neurological level below T10, 64% are potent. However, the figure falls to only 14% in men with higher neurological lesions (above T10). Sildenafil (Viagra) may be helpful for erectile dysfunction in some cases.

mcqs.stem: What percentage of adult men with spina bifida have a regular sexual partner, regardless of their degree of mobility or continence?
mcqs.explanation: Despite the drawbacks of erectile dysfunction and impaired ejaculation, 60% of adult males with spina bifida have a regular sexual partner regardless of their degree of mobility or continence. Semen can be obtained from impotent men by electroejaculation.

mcqs.stem: In a study of men with bladder exstrophy-epispadias complex (mean age 32 years), what proportion reported objectively 'normal' erections?
mcqs.explanation: In a recent study of men with bladder exstrophy-epispadias complex with a mean age of 32 years, 55% reported objectively 'normal' erections. Despite impaired semen quality in 93% of individuals, one-third of this cohort had achieved parenthood — although this depended on assisted reproductive techniques in 50% of cases.

mcqs.stem: A 34-year-old woman with bladder exstrophy and a GFR of 45 mL/min/1.73 m² with untreated diastolic blood pressure of 95 mmHg is seeking advice about pregnancy. What is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.text: Pregnancy is safe with close monitoring
She should be cautioned against embarking on pregnancy due to significant risks of irreversible deterioration in renal function and/or hypertension
Pregnancy is safe only if she takes prophylactic antibiotics
She should undergo pre-conception ureteric reimplantation first

mcqs.explanation: Women with a GFR below 50 mL/min/1.73 m² and a diastolic blood pressure (untreated) above 90 mmHg should be cautioned against embarking on pregnancy because of the very significant risks of irreversible deterioration in renal function and/or hypertension. Spina bifida, bladder exstrophy, enterocystoplasty and previous ureteric reimplantation all have potential to cause problems in pregnancy, but despite counselling, very few women are deterred from wanting to have a baby.

mcqs.stem: During the transition from paediatric to adult care, which of the following is of pivotal importance in determining a young patient's future engagement with follow-up and lifelong maintenance of renal function?

options.text: Providing written information leaflets about their condition
Transition of care from paediatric specialists to the relevant adult disciplines
Ensuring all surgical revisions are completed before transition
Discharge from follow-up until symptoms develop

mcqs.explanation: The young patient's transition from the care of paediatric specialists to the relevant adult disciplines is of pivotal importance in determining their future engagement with follow-up and lifelong maintenance of their renal function. Close collaboration between paediatric and adolescent or adult urologists is essential to ensure continuity of follow-up and specialist care.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the most important reason why close collaboration between paediatric and adult urologists is essential during the transition period?

options.text: To reduce the cost of healthcare delivery
To ensure continuity of follow-up and specialist care
To allow paediatric urologists to focus on neonatal surgery
To enable adult urologists to learn paediatric surgical techniques

mcqs.explanation: Close collaboration between paediatric and adolescent or adult urologists is important primarily to ensure continuity of follow-up and specialist care. Many problems in adolescent urology are predominantly medical or psychosocial rather than surgical, and a holistic approach to long-term care is essential.

mcqs.stem: Why may measurement of plasma creatinine be potentially misleading when monitoring renal function in patients with congenital urological anomalies?

options.text: Creatinine assays are not validated for paediatric patients
A significant loss of GFR can sometimes occur before it is reflected in abnormal plasma creatinine levels
Creatinine is not filtered by dysplastic kidneys
Muscle wasting in congenital anomalies gives falsely low creatinine levels

mcqs.explanation: Although measurement of plasma creatinine represents the simplest and most practical means of monitoring renal function, it may be potentially misleading in patients with congenital malformations because a significant loss of GFR can sometimes occur before this is reflected in abnormal plasma creatinine levels. All patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care require risk assessment of their renal function.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old with bladder exstrophy who underwent successful bladder reconstruction in childhood now presents with deteriorating continence. Historically, what proportion of exstrophy patients who underwent bladder reconstruction in childhood progressed into adult life without the need for urinary diversion?
mcqs.explanation: Historically, only 23% of exstrophy patients who underwent bladder reconstruction in childhood progressed into adult life without the need for urinary diversion. Of those whose bladder reconstruction did endure beyond childhood, 60% nevertheless required further reconstruction in adolescence or early adult life.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following strategies has been shown to delay the downward trajectory of renal function in adolescents with PUV and declining GFR?

options.text: Rigorous control of proteinuria and blood pressure using ACE inhibitors

mcqs.explanation: The downward trajectory of renal function in PUV patients can be delayed with rigorous control of proteinuria and blood pressure using angiotensin-conversion enzyme (ACE) inhibitors. This is important because patients reaching adolescence with a GFR below 40 mL/min/1.73 m² are at substantial risk of progressing to end-stage renal failure within 16 years.

mcqs.stem: A risk stratification system for PUV patients categorises them into low, medium and high risk for significant renal impairment (CKD ≥3). What is the incidence of renal impairment in those categorised as high risk?
mcqs.explanation: When PUV patients are stratified according to their risk of significant renal impairment (defined as CKD ≥3), those categorised as low risk have a 6% incidence, medium risk a 40% incidence, and high risk a 70% incidence of renal impairment. The prognostic risk score is based on baseline plasma creatinine, nadir creatinine level and proteinuria.

mcqs.stem: In a large study of exstrophy patients followed for over 40 years, what proportion was still voiding spontaneously per urethra?
mcqs.explanation: In a large study of exstrophy patients who were followed for over 40 years, only 25% of patients were still voiding spontaneously per urethra. The remaining 75% were either reliant on catheterisation or had undergone urinary diversion. Most patients required complex reconstruction and subsequent revisions to achieve urinary continence.

mcqs.stem: What is the reported latent period for malignancy developing after ureterosigmoidostomy?
mcqs.explanation: Ureterosigmoidostomy is associated with a 15% incidence of malignancy (usually adenocarcinoma) occurring within the bowel reservoir, with a typical latent period of 20-30 years before malignancy develops. In contrast, the risk of malignancy after enterocystoplasty is likely to be considerably lower.

mcqs.stem: A 19-year-old with spina bifida is being prepared for transition to adult care. Which of the following would be the most important long-term objective to establish during childhood?

options.text: Academic achievement at a university level
Independent self-care, including adaptation of daily living, bladder/bowel management, sexual and general education
Complete surgical correction of all orthopaedic deformities

mcqs.explanation: Independent self-care should always be the long-term objective in spina bifida patients. Achieving this goal depends on: adaptation of daily living to permit self-sufficiency in tasks such as dressing, washing and household chores; effective management of the neuropathic bladder and bowel; sexual education; and general education.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is a recognised approach to managing the combination of a poorly compliant 'valve bladder' and polyuria in PUV patients to prevent worsening renal damage?

options.text: Aggressive bladder management with anticholinergics, self-catheterisation and clam cystoplasty
No intervention is required as the condition is self-limiting

mcqs.explanation: The combination of a poorly compliant 'valve bladder' and polyuria from renal tubular concentration defects may result in dangerously high bladder storage pressures, creating a vicious cycle of worsening renal damage. There is evidence that the onset of renal failure can be delayed (or possibly averted) by aggressive bladder management with anticholinergics, self-catheterisation and clam cystoplasty.

mcqs.stem: A 20-year-old man with prune-belly syndrome has normal serum testosterone but elevated serum luteinising hormone (LH). This hormonal profile is best explained by:

options.text: Primary gonadal failure with compensated Leydig cell dysfunction
Hypothalamic dysfunction due to congenital midline defects

mcqs.explanation: In prune-belly syndrome, serum testosterone levels in adulthood are usually in the normal range and adequate to maintain normal masculinisation, but serum LH levels are significantly elevated. This pattern indicates primary gonadal failure with compensated Leydig cell dysfunction — the testes are producing adequate testosterone but require increased LH stimulation to do so.

mcqs.stem: A 3-year-old girl is found to have a thin greyish midline band of tissue partially covering the vaginal introitus during a well-child examination. Lateral traction on the labia majora reveals a translucent avascular line in the midline. The child is otherwise asymptomatic. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Labial adhesions (agglutination) present as a midline avascular band of tissue when lateral traction is applied to the labia majora, ranging from partial to complete occlusion of the introitus. They are most common between 3 months and 3 years of age and are often asymptomatic, discovered incidentally during routine examination.

mcqs.stem: An 8-year-old girl presents with intense genital itching and perianal discomfort. On examination, hypopigmented skin in a figure-of-eight pattern surrounding the vestibule and anus is noted, with thinning of the vulvar skin resembling parchment paper. What is the recommended first-line treatment?

options.text: High-potency topical steroid ointment tapered over 2–3 months
Topical estrogen cream applied twice daily for 2 weeks

mcqs.explanation: Prepubertal lichen sclerosus is treated with a course of topical steroids over 2–3 months, starting with a high-potency steroid ointment (e.g., clobetasol propionate 0.05%) and then titrating down to a lower potency steroid. Surgery is reserved for symptomatic adhesions or scarring only.

mcqs.stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with vulvar redness, soreness, and a thin vaginal discharge. Her mother reports she has been wiping herself after bowel movements and occasionally uses bubble bath. What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

options.text: Non-specific vulvovaginitis due to irritants and poor hygiene

mcqs.explanation: The majority of vulvovaginitis in prepubertal girls is non-specific, caused by anatomical factors (thin vaginal mucosa, alkaline pH, close proximity to anus) and behavioral factors such as poor hand washing, incorrect wiping, and exposure to irritants like bubble bath. Yeast infection is rare in toilet-trained prepubertal girls due to the unestrogenized, alkaline vaginal environment.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old girl presents with a 6-month history of cyclic lower abdominal pain and has never menstruated. On physical examination, a bulging bluish membrane is noted at the introitus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Imperforate hymen typically presents in early adolescence with primary amenorrhea and cyclic abdominal pain due to hematometrocolpos. A bulging hymenal membrane at the perineum is a classic physical finding, distinguishing it from a transverse vaginal septum (no bulging at perineum) and MRKH (normal external genitalia with a blind-ending vagina).

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents with primary amenorrhea. She has normal breast development (Tanner stage V) and normal external genitalia with a blind-ending vaginal pouch. Pelvic MRI reveals absence of the uterus and upper vagina. Ovaries are present bilaterally. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: MRKH syndrome (Müllerian agenesis) results from failed development of the Müllerian ducts, occurring in ~1 in 4,500 female births. It presents with primary amenorrhea in a phenotypically normal female with normal pubertal development, absent uterus, and underdeveloped upper vagina. Ovaries are present and functional, distinguishing it from CAIS (which has absent ovaries/testes with androgen insensitivity).

mcqs.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting. The pain started suddenly 3 hours ago. On examination, there is marked tenderness with rebound and guarding. Ultrasound shows an enlarged right adnexa with peripheral cysts and centralized edema. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Proceed to diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian preservation
Observe with pain management and repeat ultrasound in 6 hours
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for pelvic inflammatory disease

mcqs.explanation: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. The clinical presentation of acute pelvic pain, nausea, vomiting, and peritoneal signs with suggestive ultrasound findings warrants diagnostic laparoscopy. Ovarian preservation (detorsion) is recommended because the intraoperative appearance of the adnexa does not reliably predict irreversible ischemic damage. A low threshold for laparoscopy is essential.

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old girl expresses concern about the appearance of her labia minora, which she feels are enlarged. She reports occasional irritation during exercise but no pain. On measurement, the labia minora protrude 5 cm from the vestibule. According to the chapter, what width is generally considered hypertrophied?
mcqs.explanation: The width or protrusion of the labia minora in adolescent females can range from a few millimeters to several centimeters. In general, a width of greater than 5–6 cm is considered hypertrophied. Clinicians should first reassure patients about normal variation before considering any intervention.

mcqs.stem: A 15-year-old girl presents with primary amenorrhea and worsening cyclical pelvic pain over 8 months. On examination, there is no bulging hymen or vaginal mass visible at the perineum. Pelvic MRI shows a complete vaginal septum in the mid-vagina with hematocolpos above it. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: A complete transverse vaginal septum presents similarly to imperforate hymen (primary amenorrhea, cyclical pain, hematocolpos), but crucially, no bulging hymen is visible on the perineum because the septum is located higher in the vagina. It results from failed fusion of the vaginal plate and Müllerian ducts and can occur anywhere along the vaginal length.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old girl presents with worsening dysmenorrhea beginning 6 months after menarche. She has regular periods. On bimanual examination, a lateral vaginal bulge is palpated. MRI reveals a didelphys uterus and an obstructed right hemi-vagina with ipsilateral renal agenesis. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.text: Surgical resection of the obstructing vaginal septum
Menstrual suppression with continuous oral contraceptives

mcqs.explanation: OHVIRA syndrome (Obstructed Hemi-Vagina with Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly) consists of didelphys uterus, obstructed hemi-vagina, and ipsilateral renal anomalies. Patients present with normal menarche but worsening dysmenorrhea. Definitive treatment is surgical resection of the obstructing septum. Hemi-hysterectomy may be needed if the septum is high or incompletely excised.

mcqs.stem: An ultrasound of a 17-year-old girl with pelvic pain reveals a complex cystic lesion containing sebaceous fluid, hair, and calcifications. The cyst is 6 cm in diameter. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Mature teratomas (dermoid cysts) are the most common germ cell tumor in adolescence and have characteristic ultrasound appearances due to the presence of sebaceous fluid, hair, and calcifications. They are bilateral in 10–15% of cases and are managed with ovarian cystectomy when large or symptomatic.

mcqs.stem: A 9-year-old girl with lichen sclerosus has completed a course of topical steroids with good resolution of symptoms. Her mother asks about the long-term outlook. What should the clinician advise regarding the natural history of prepubertal lichen sclerosus?

options.text: It always resolves completely by menarche
It is associated with a high risk of vulvar carcinoma in adolescence
Many patients experience persistent signs or symptoms into adolescence
It typically evolves into vulvar psoriasis

mcqs.explanation: Prepubertal lichen sclerosus was previously thought to resolve during puberty with endogenous estrogen production, but several reports have shown that many patients experience persistent signs or symptoms in adolescence. The risk of malignancy associated with prepubertal lichen sclerosus has not been established, unlike in postmenopausal women where there is an increased risk of vulvar squamous cell carcinoma.

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old girl has recurrent episodes of vulvar redness and discharge. Her mother asks about preventive measures. According to the vulvar hygiene recommendations, which of the following should be advised?

options.text: Use scented soap for thorough cleansing
Wear plain white cotton underwear and use unscented detergent
Use bubble baths to keep the area clean

mcqs.explanation: Vulvar hygiene recommendations include wearing plain white cotton underwear, using unscented detergent, avoiding irritants including bubble bath or scented soaps, changing quickly out of wet clothing, and wiping front to back after bowel movements. Non-medicated barrier cream can be applied, but antibiotic creams are not recommended for prevention.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old girl is noted to have extensive labial adhesions with only a pinpoint opening. She has had two urinary tract infections in the past 6 months. What is the recommended first-line medical management?

options.text: Topical estrogen applied twice daily for 2 weeks
Immediate manual separation in the clinic

mcqs.explanation: For symptomatic labial adhesions, first-line treatment is topical estrogen applied twice daily for 2 weeks. A small pea-sized amount is applied to the adhesion line. Topical estrogen is well tolerated with minimal systemic effects. Manual separation or sharp dissection is reserved for cases refractory to medical management.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl requests labiaplasty because she is unhappy with the appearance of her labia minora. On examination, the labia minora protrude 3.5 cm from the vestibule and she has no functional symptoms. According to best practice guidelines, what is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure and educate about normal anatomic variation; avoid surgery

mcqs.explanation: In the United States and many European countries, female genital surgery in children is legally prohibited unless there are valid medical indications. Surgical intervention (labiaplasty) should not be performed for cosmetic reasons in patients under 18 years of age. Labiaplasty should only be considered for patients with labial enlargement outside the typical range (>5 cm) and whose functional symptoms are not relieved by supportive measures.

mcqs.stem: A 15-year-old girl reports difficulty removing tampons, noting that they occasionally get "stuck" and she has to tug to retrieve them. She has regular, painless menstruation. On examination, a band of hymenal tissue is noted dividing the vaginal orifice. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: A septate hymen is the most common hymenal anomaly. Patients typically have normal menstruation but may report difficulty removing tampons, which remain stuck behind a hymenal band. The examination reveals a band of tissue dividing the hymenal opening, distinguishing it from microperforate or cribiform variants.

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old phenotypically female patient presents with primary amenorrhea. She has Tanner stage V breast development. External genitalia appear normal with a blind-ending vaginal pouch. Chromosomal analysis reveals 46,XX and plasma testosterone is within normal female range. Pelvic MRI shows absent uterus. What is the definitive diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: MRKH is confirmed by the combination of pelvic imaging showing absent uterus, normal ovaries, 46,XX karyotype, and normal female testosterone levels. This workup distinguishes MRKH from complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS), which would show 46,XY karyotype and elevated male-range testosterone in a phenotypic female.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl experiences acute right lower quadrant pain during ovulation. Ultrasound reveals a 3 cm simple, thin-walled, unilocular cyst. She is hemodynamically stable and pain resolves with oral analgesics. Repeat ultrasound in 4 weeks shows complete resolution of the cyst. What was the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Functional ovarian cysts (follicular or corpus luteum cysts) arise during ovulation. Follicular cysts are simple, thin-walled cysts that typically resolve spontaneously within 2–8 weeks. Surgical intervention is not indicated. Hormonal contraception can be used to prevent recurrence if needed.

mcqs.stem: An adolescent girl presents with acute onset of severe left-sided pelvic pain and vomiting. Ultrasound shows an enlarged left ovary with a "whirlpool" sign of the vascular pedicle. Doppler demonstrates both arterial and venous blood flow within the ovary. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure that blood flow rules out torsion

mcqs.explanation: The presence of arterial blood flow on Doppler does NOT rule out ovarian torsion. The diagnosis of torsion should be suspected based on clinical history and examination, and clinicians should have a low threshold for proceeding to diagnostic laparoscopy. Ultrasound findings such as enlarged adnexa and the "whirlpool" sign support the diagnosis but absence of Doppler flow abnormalities does not exclude it.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with genital itching and a superficial break in the perineal skin. On examination, there is a hypopigmented figure-of-eight pattern around the vestibule and anus with subepithelial hemorrhages. Which condition can this finding be mistaken for by those unfamiliar with it?
mcqs.explanation: The appearances of genital ecchymosis and bleeding in prepubertal lichen sclerosus can sometimes raise concerns regarding genital trauma or abuse. The classic hypopigmented figure-of-eight pattern surrounding the vestibule and anus, with parchment-like skin, is diagnostic. Lichen sclerosus should be considered in the differential of perineal findings that might otherwise be attributed to trauma.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old girl with persistent vaginal discharge and odor has been treated with a course of oral amoxicillin without improvement. The discharge is now blood-tinged. What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Vaginoscopy to look for a vaginal foreign body
CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

mcqs.explanation: Patients with foul-smelling, blood-tinged, or recurrent vaginal discharge that does not respond to typical treatments may need vaginoscopy to look for a possible vaginal foreign body. Vaginal foreign bodies are an important consideration in prepubertal girls with persistent or atypical discharge not responding to antibiotics.

mcqs.stem: What is the most common age range for labial adhesions in girls?
mcqs.explanation: Labial adhesions are most common between 3 months and 3 years of age. They occur in response to vulvar irritation and low estrogen levels in this age group. The mucosal surfaces of the labia minora become inflamed and raw from irritation, then adhere together in the midline. Adhesions typically resolve spontaneously with the onset of endogenous estrogen production at puberty.

mcqs.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with primary amenorrhea and a 4-month history of cyclic lower abdominal pain. She has an abdominal mass and on perineal inspection, a bulging, bluish membrane is seen at the introitus. What physical finding is most characteristic?

options.text: A midline avascular band on lateral traction
A vaginal septum dividing the canal

mcqs.explanation: Imperforate hymen presents with a bulging hymenal membrane at the perineum due to hematometrocolpos (accumulated menstrual blood behind the obstructed hymen). This is a key distinguishing feature from a transverse vaginal septum, where no bulging is visible at the perineum because the obstruction is higher in the vagina.

mcqs.stem: A 19-year-old woman has regular periods but reports that she cannot use tampons and that her partner experiences discomfort during intercourse. On examination, a longitudinal band of tissue divides the vagina into two halves. The uterus is felt to be septate on bimanual examination. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Resection of the longitudinal vaginal septum

mcqs.explanation: A longitudinal vaginal septum is a non-obstructing anomaly resulting from failed fusion of the distal Müllerian ducts, typically associated with a didelphys or septate uterus. Symptomatic patients (unable to use tampons, dyspareunia, postcoital hemorrhage) should be offered septum resection. Asymptomatic patients do not require intervention.

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old girl with known endometriosis has a 6 cm ovarian cyst found on surveillance ultrasound. The cyst appears complex with homogeneous low-level echoes and is multilocular. She reports persistent pelvic pain. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Expectant management with repeat ultrasound in 8 weeks

mcqs.explanation: Endometriomas do not resolve with hormonal management. Laparoscopic removal is indicated if the cyst is symptomatic or greater than 5 cm. On ultrasound, endometriomas appear as complex cystic lesions with homogeneous low-level echoes, and may be unilocular or multilocular.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl has a complex ovarian mass with both solid and cystic components. Tumor markers are ordered. Which combination of elevated tumor markers is most consistent with a malignant germ cell tumor?
mcqs.explanation: Germ cell tumors, the most common ovarian malignancies in adolescence, are associated with elevated alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG). Sex cord tumors are associated with inhibin A&B, estradiol, and testosterone. Epithelial tumors are associated with CA-125.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl is newly diagnosed with MRKH syndrome. What associated anomalies should be screened for?
mcqs.explanation: Associated renal and skeletal anomalies are common in MRKH syndrome. Screening should include a renal ultrasound scan and spinal X-ray. This reflects the embryologic relationship between the Müllerian ducts and the developing urinary tract and axial skeleton.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents with bilateral labial enlargement. She reports discomfort during physical activity and difficulty with menstrual hygiene. On examination, both labia minora protrude approximately 6 cm from the vestibule. What functional symptoms are most commonly associated with labial hypertrophy?

options.text: Irritation, discomfort with movement, and interference with intercourse or hygiene

mcqs.explanation: Functional symptoms of labial hypertrophy include irritation, discomfort or pulling with movement, interference with intercourse, and difficulty with menstrual hygiene. Many patients, however, present primarily because of dissatisfaction with the appearance of their genitalia.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old girl undergoes resection of a thick mid-vaginal transverse septum. Post-operatively, what is recommended to prevent stenosis and recurrent obstruction?

options.text: Monthly hymenal dilation in the clinic

mcqs.explanation: Post-operative dilation is recommended after treatment of a transverse vaginal septum because of the higher risks of stenosis and recurrent obstruction. For this reason, the age and maturity of the patient should be considered before definitive surgical management. In contrast, post-operative dilation is not required after hymenectomy for imperforate hymen.

mcqs.stem: A 15-year-old girl presents with worsening dysmenorrhea 8 months after menarche. She has regular periods. Pelvic examination reveals a lateral vaginal wall bulge. MRI shows a didelphys uterus and an obstructed right hemi-vagina. What renal anomaly is most likely to be present?
mcqs.explanation: OHVIRA syndrome (Obstructed Hemi-Vagina with Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly) characteristically includes a didelphys uterus, obstructed hemi-vagina, and ipsilateral renal anomaly (most commonly renal agenesis). The acronym OHVIRA itself highlights the association with ipsilateral renal anomalies.

mcqs.stem: During laparoscopic resection of a 7 cm dermoid cyst in a 17-year-old girl, the cyst ruptures and sebaceous contents spill into the peritoneal cavity. What is the most important immediate intraoperative measure to prevent a complication?

options.text: Copious irrigation of the abdominal cavity and pelvis
