Place a drain in the pouch of Douglas

mcqs.explanation: If dermoid cyst contents are spilled intraoperatively, the abdominal cavity and pelvis should be copiously irrigated to prevent chemical peritonitis. Dermoid cysts contain sebaceous fluid, hair, and other irritants that can cause a severe inflammatory reaction if left in the peritoneal cavity.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old girl is referred to a pediatric gynecologist with lichen sclerosus based on clinical findings. The referring physician requested a vulvar biopsy to confirm the diagnosis. What is the recommended approach to biopsy in prepubertal lichen sclerosus?

options.text: Punch biopsy is mandatory before starting treatment
Biopsy should be performed if topical steroids fail after 2 weeks
Excisional biopsy of the affected area is recommended
Vulvar biopsy is not routinely indicated; diagnosis is clinical

mcqs.explanation: Prepubertal lichen sclerosus can almost invariably be diagnosed on the basis of clinical history and examination of the external genitalia alone. Vulvar biopsy should not be routinely performed and is rarely indicated. This distinguishes it from adult lichen sclerosus where biopsy is more commonly considered.

mcqs.stem: A 5-year-old girl has had three episodes of vulvovaginitis in the past 6 months. Each episode was treated with oral amoxicillin with initial improvement, but symptoms recur. The current discharge is foul-smelling and blood-tinged. What should be suspected?
mcqs.explanation: Patients with foul-smelling, blood-tinged, or recurrent vaginal discharge which does not respond to typical treatments may have a vaginal foreign body and should undergo vaginoscopy. This is a classic clinical pearl in pediatric gynecology: persistent foul discharge unresponsive to antibiotics suggests a retained foreign body.

mcqs.stem: After successful medical treatment of labial adhesions in a 2-year-old girl, the mother asks how to prevent recurrence. What should be recommended?

options.text: Apply non-medicated barrier cream and maintain good vulvar hygiene
Apply topical steroid twice weekly for maintenance
Perform manual separation at home if adhesions recur

mcqs.explanation: To prevent recurrence of labial adhesions, non-medicated barrier cream and vulvar hygiene are recommended. Recurrence is common after medical management, but barrier creams help maintain separation of the labial edges. Prolonged topical estrogen can cause side effects such as vulvar hyperpigmentation or breast budding.

mcqs.stem: A 14-year-old girl reports that she has normal monthly periods but finds it very difficult to insert tampons, experiencing pain with attempted insertion. On examination, there is a small pinpoint hymenal opening. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Microperforate hymen has a very small pinpoint opening, allowing normal menstruation but causing difficulty with tampon use. It is one of the partially occlusive hymenal anomalies (along with septate and cribiform), which are characterized by normal menstruation but potential difficulty with tampons.

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old girl with MRKH syndrome desires creation of a functional vagina. She is motivated and has received counseling. What is the first-line treatment for vaginal agenesis in MRKH?
mcqs.explanation: First-line treatment for vaginal agenesis is vaginal dilation with serial dilators, which is successful in up to 90% of motivated patients. Surgery should only be considered when this is desired by the patient and after dilation has failed. The patient inserts a dilator for 20–30 minutes once to twice daily, with successful creation of a functional vagina usually requiring 6–12 months.

mcqs.stem: An asymptomatic 15-year-old girl is found to have a 5 cm simple ovarian cyst on ultrasound performed for an unrelated reason. The cyst is unilocular and anechoic. What is the most appropriate management?
mcqs.explanation: Asymptomatic simple ovarian cysts less than 10 cm in diameter can safely be observed and followed with serial pelvic ultrasound scans. Surgical management (ovarian cystectomy) is indicated for symptomatic cysts and for those that are persistent and enlarging beyond 4–6 cm. This particular cyst is 5 cm but asymptomatic and simple in appearance.

mcqs.stem: A 12-year-old girl presents with sudden-onset severe lower abdominal pain and vomiting. An ultrasound is performed to evaluate for ovarian torsion. Which of the following ultrasound findings is most commonly associated with ovarian torsion in this age group?

options.text: Enlarged adnexa with peripheral cysts and centralized edema
Normal ovarian size with increased vascular flow

mcqs.explanation: Ultrasound findings in ovarian torsion may include an enlarged adnexa, peripheral ovarian cysts with centralized edema, the "whirlpool" sign of the vascular pedicle, or absence of arterial blood flow on Doppler. However, the presence of arterial blood flow does NOT rule out torsion, and clinical assessment is paramount.

mcqs.stem: An 8-year-old girl is diagnosed with lichen sclerosus. Her mother has a history of vitiligo. Which of the following conditions is known to be associated with lichen sclerosus, supporting an autoimmune mechanism?

options.text: Systemic lupus erythematosus and Sjögren syndrome

mcqs.explanation: Lichen sclerosus has been associated with conditions such as vitiligo, alopecia, and thyroiditis, suggesting an autoimmune mechanism contributes to the disease. The estimated incidence in prepubertal girls is approximately 1 in 900, and the exact pathogenesis is likely multifactorial.

mcqs.stem: A 4-year-old toilet-trained girl presents with vulvar itching and discharge. Her mother is concerned about a yeast infection. Why is vulvovaginal candidiasis uncommon in this age group?

options.text: Prepubescent girls lack Candida receptors in the vagina
The vaginal epithelium is thick and acidic
The unestrogenized, alkaline vaginal environment does not support Candida growth

mcqs.explanation: Yeast infection is rarely the cause of vulvovaginitis in toilet-trained prepubertal girls because the unestrogenized and alkaline environment of the prepubertal vagina does not permit the growth of Candida. This contrasts with the estrogenized, acidic vaginal environment of reproductive-age women, where Candida thrives.

mcqs.stem: A newborn female is found to have multiple small openings in the hymenal membrane during the newborn examination. She is otherwise healthy. This finding is most consistent with which hymenal variant?
mcqs.explanation: A cribiform hymen has multiple small openings (like a sieve). Along with septate and microperforate hymen, it is a partially occlusive anomaly. These are typically asymptomatic in infancy but may cause difficulty with tampon use or menstrual hygiene in adolescence. Asymptomatic cases do not require intervention in childhood.

mcqs.stem: A 15-year-old girl with primary amenorrhea and cyclical pain is found to have a transverse vaginal septum on pelvic examination. What imaging modality is especially important to determine the thickness of the septum before planning surgical resection?
mcqs.explanation: It is especially important to determine the thickness of the septum with pelvic examination and MRI when planning surgery for a transverse vaginal septum. MRI provides the most accurate assessment of the septum thickness and the distance between the obstructed upper vagina and the perineum, which guides surgical approach.

mcqs.stem: A 2-year-old girl with labial adhesions is treated with topical estrogen cream. After 3 weeks of use, the mother notices some darkening of the vulvar skin and slight breast budding. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Immediately discontinue the cream and refer to endocrinology
These are known side effects; use smaller amount and consider tapering
Switch to oral estrogen for better control

mcqs.explanation: Side effects of prolonged topical estrogen use include vulvar hyperpigmentation and breast budding. Generally, topical estrogen is well tolerated with minimal systemic effects due to absorption. These side effects are reversible and should prompt a reduction in amount and duration of use rather than abrupt discontinuation in a patient who still needs treatment.

mcqs.stem: A 16-year-old girl is diagnosed with MRKH syndrome. After the diagnosis is explained, she becomes tearful and expresses concern about her ability to have children in the future. What is the most important aspect of her ongoing care?

options.text: Immediate referral for in vitro fertilization
Emotional support, counseling, and contact with support groups

mcqs.explanation: The loss of fertility is particularly challenging for young patients with MRKH, and emotional support is perhaps the most important aspect of their care. All patients should be put in contact with support groups and offered counseling. Surgery should only be considered when desired by the patient, and vaginal dilation is the first-line treatment.

mcqs.stem: In a case of OHVIRA syndrome where a high obstructing septum is noted, what surgical option may be required if the septum cannot be completely excised transvaginally?
mcqs.explanation: In OHVIRA, stenosis and re-obstruction are unlikely unless the septum is high or incompletely excised. In these cases, a hemi-hysterectomy may be indicated. Definitive treatment consists of surgical resection of the obstructing septum, but a hemi-hysterectomy is a salvage option for complex high septa.

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old girl is found to have a mature teratoma on ultrasound. She is counseled about the risk of bilaterality. What percentage of mature teratomas are bilateral in adolescent patients?
mcqs.explanation: Mature teratomas (dermoid cysts) are bilateral in 10–15% of cases. This is an important consideration during surgical planning, as both ovaries should be inspected at the time of surgery even if only one appears involved on preoperative imaging.

mcqs.stem: A 7-year-old girl with lichen sclerosus has completed 2 weeks of clobetasol propionate 0.05% twice daily and is now on once-daily application. According to the treatment protocol in the chapter, what should be the next step in the steroid taper?

options.text: Switch to triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% twice daily for 2 weeks

mcqs.explanation: The treatment protocol for lichen sclerosus begins with clobetasol propionate 0.05% twice daily for 2 weeks, then once daily for 2 weeks, followed by triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% twice daily for 2 weeks, then once daily for 2 weeks, and then 1–2 times weekly for maintenance. This gradual taper minimizes recurrence while avoiding prolonged high-potency steroid use.

mcqs.stem: A 6-year-old girl with persistent vaginal discharge has been treated with two courses of oral antibiotics without improvement. Vaginoscopy is planned. How should an intravaginal culture specimen be obtained to minimize trauma?

options.text: Using a speculum for direct visualization
Collecting discharge from the introitus only
Through a Foley catheter inserted into the vagina
A small moistened swab inserted gently past the hymen without touching it

mcqs.explanation: If an intravaginal specimen is needed, it should be obtained without touching the hymen. A small moistened swab can be inserted gently past the hymen to collect a specimen. This technique minimizes discomfort and trauma in the prepubertal child.

mcqs.stem: A 15-year-old girl is distressed about the appearance of her labia and asks if she is "normal." The clinician wishes to provide reassurance by showing examples of normal variation in female genital anatomy. Which resource is specifically recommended for this purpose?

options.text: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists patient education pamphlets
PubMed literature review of labial dimensions

mcqs.explanation: The chapter specifically recommends using online resources such as The Labia Library (www.labialibrary.org.au) and Great Wall of Vagina (www.greatwallofvagina.co.uk) to demonstrate differences in normal anatomy. These resources can be very helpful in educating and reassuring patients about normal variation.

mcqs.stem: During laparoscopy for suspected ovarian torsion, the ovary appears dark, edematous, and hemorrhagic. What is the recommended surgical approach based on current evidence?

options.text: Perform detorsion and preserve the ovary regardless of appearance
Proceed with oophorectomy due to likely irreversible ischemia
Perform detorsion only if Doppler flow is present
Biopsy the ovary first to assess viability

mcqs.explanation: Because the intraoperative appearance of the adnexa is not a reliable predictor of irreversible ischemic damage, ovarian preservation (detorsion/untwisting) is the recommended management of acute ovarian torsion. Oophoropexy is only indicated for cases of recurrent ovarian torsion. Even a severely discolored ovary can recover function after detorsion.

mcqs.stem: A 13-year-old girl has an asymptomatic 3.5 cm simple ovarian cyst detected incidentally on imaging for urinary symptoms. She is post-menarchal and has regular cycles. What is the most likely diagnosis and expected course?

options.text: Functional ovarian cyst that will resolve spontaneously within 2–8 weeks

mcqs.explanation: Simple unilocular anechoic cysts in post-menarchal adolescents are almost always benign functional cysts (follicular or corpus luteum) that resolve spontaneously within 2–8 weeks. Surgical intervention is not indicated for asymptomatic simple cysts, especially when less than 4–6 cm. Follow-up ultrasound in 6–8 weeks to confirm resolution is appropriate.
